topics.title: Study of Precision Tools and Measuring Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Precision Tools and Measuring Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precision Measuring Instruments** are standardized metrological devices used in manufacturing and quality engineering to determine the geometrical magnitude of a workpiece dimension (linear, angular, or surface-finish) with a high degree of **accuracy**, **repeatability**, and **least count**, typically operating in the micrometer (10⁻⁶ m) and angular second ranges.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PCNSL308, this experiment deals with the identification, constructional features, working principles, and least-count determination of the most commonly employed workshop and laboratory metrology instruments.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of measuring instruments like the **scales, timers, and rulers** a doctor uses. Just as a doctor cannot prescribe the correct medicine without an accurate weight (kg) and blood-pressure reading (mmHg), a mechanical engineer cannot produce an interchangeable, defect-free component without precision measurement. A vernier caliper is to an engineer what a ruler is to a carpenter, but with **100× the resolution**.

Every instrument essentially performs a **comparison** between the unknown workpiece dimension and a **known standard**, then amplifies the difference so the human eye can read it on a graduated scale.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Least Count (LC):** The smallest measurable increment a device can resolve. Measured in **mm** (linear) or **degrees/seconds** (angular).
- **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the **true value** (standard reference).
- **Precision / Repeatability:** Closeness of successive measurements to each other under unchanged conditions.
- **Range:** The **maximum** and **minimum** dimensions the instrument can measure.
- **Resolution:** The **finest** change in input that produces a detectable change in output.
- **Zero Error:** Deviation of the instrument reading from **zero** when no workpiece is present.
- **Backlash Error:** Mechanical play between **mating screw threads** (dominant in micrometers).
- **Abbe's Principle:** The axis of measurement should be **collinear** with the axis of the workpiece — never offset.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always state the **Least Count** along with the formula used to compute it. Examiners allocate a dedicated 2-mark slot for "least-count derivation" in viva/practical records.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Precision Measuring Instruments

**A. Linear Dimension Instruments**
1. **Steel Rule** — LC = **0.5 mm** or **1 mm**.
2. **Vernier Caliper** — LC = **0.02 mm** (most common), can also be **0.05 mm** or **0.1 mm**.
3. **Vernier Depth Gauge** — Same LC as vernier caliper.
4. **Micrometer (Screw Gauge)** — LC = **0.01 mm** (standard metric).
5. **Vernier Height Gauge** — LC = **0.02 mm**, used for vertical measurements on a surface plate.
6. **Dial Indicator (DTI / Plunger Dial)** — LC = **0.01 mm** or **0.001 mm**.
7. **Bore Gauge (Internal Micrometer)** — LC = **0.01 mm**.
8. **Slip Gauges (Johansson Gauges)** — Grade 0, 1, 2; used as length standards.
9. **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** — LC = **0.001 mm (1 µm)** with probe.

**B. Angular Dimension Instruments**
1. **Bevel Protractor** — LC = **5 minutes (5′)** or **1°**.
2. **Sine Bar** — Used with slip gauges; theoretical infinite resolution.
3. **Angle Decker / Angle Gauges.**
4. **Clinometer / Spirit Level** — LC = **0.01 mm/m**.

**C. Surface Finish Instruments**
1. **Surface Roughness Tester (Talysurf)** — Ra, Rz parameters in µm.
2. **Optical Flat / Monochromatic Light Source** — Flatness checking.

**D. Comparators (Amplification Devices)**
1. **Mechanical Comparator (Dial type).**
2. **Optical Comparator (Optical Lever Principle).**
3. **Electrical Comparator (LVDT-based).**
4. **Pneumatic Comparator.**

### 2.2 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Pillars of Every Linear Instrument:**
> 1. **Main Scale (Primary Scale):** Provides the coarse, integer reading.
> 2. **Secondary Scale (Vernier / Thimble / Dial):** Provides the fine fractional reading.
> 3. **Reading Mechanism:** Aligns the graduation lines of both scales to produce a single, unambiguous output.

**For Vernier Caliper:**
- Step 1 → Read the main scale in mm just **before** the zero of the vernier scale.
- Step 2 → Find which vernier division **coincides exactly** with a main-scale line.
- Step 3 → Multiply the coinciding vernier division by the **least count**.
- Step 4 → **Sum** = Main Scale Reading (MSR) + Vernier Scale Reading (VSR × LC).

**For Micrometer:**
- Step 1 → Read the **sleeve (barrel) main scale** in mm (visible past the thimble).
- Step 2 → Read the **thimble scale** (0–50 divisions × 0.01 mm).
- Step 3 → Sum = Sleeve Reading + Thimble Reading.
- Step 4 → Apply **zero-error correction**: True Reading = Observed Reading − (+ve zero error) or + (|−ve zero error|).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Instrument | Key Formula | Units | Symbol Meaning |
|---|------------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| 1 | Vernier Caliper | $LC = 1\,MSD - 1\,VSD$ | mm | MSD = Main Scale Division, VSD = Vernier Scale Division |
| 2 | Vernier (n divs over (n−1) MSD) | $LC = \dfrac{MSD}{n}$ | mm | n = No. of vernier divisions |
| 3 | Micrometer | $LC = \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{No. of thimble divs}}$ | mm | Pitch = Axial advance per revolution |
| 4 | Micrometer (metric std.) | $LC = \dfrac{0.5\,mm}{50} = 0.01\,mm$ | mm | Standard value |
| 5 | Total Reading | $R = MSR + (VSR \times LC) \pm Z.E.$ | mm | Z.E. = Zero Error |
| 6 | Sine Bar (angle) | $\sin \theta = \dfrac{h}{l}$ | dimensionless | h = slip-gauge stack height, l = sine-bar length (100 or 200 mm) |
| 7 | Sine Bar (rolls) | $\sin \theta = \dfrac{R - r}{C}$ | dimensionless | R = workpiece radius, r = roller radius, C = centre distance |
| 8 | Bevel Protractor | Direct scale reading (vernier 5′) | degrees, minutes | — |
| 9 | Dial Indicator | Reading from dial × LC | mm | LC stamped on dial (typically 0.01 mm) |
| 10 | Slip Gauge Wringing | Build-up by **additive subtraction** | mm | Stack to required dimension ±0.5 µm |
| 11 | Optical Lever Magnification | $M = \dfrac{2L}{d}$ | dimensionless | L = mirror-to-scale distance, d = mirror diameter |
| 12 | Abbe Offset Error | $\delta = h \cdot \tan \alpha$ | mm | h = offset height, α = angular deviation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Always substitute lengths in **consistent units (mm)**. Mixing **cm** and **mm** is the single biggest cause of numerical errors in KTU answer scripts.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry Sector | Application |
|-----------------|-------------|
| **Automotive** | CMM inspection of engine blocks; bore gauges for cylinder bore |
| **Aerospace** | Laser interferometers for CNC calibration; tool-maker's microscope |
| **Tool Room** | Slip gauges as the primary length standard |
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Surface roughness testers (Ra) on bearing surfaces |
| **Production Shop Floor** | Vernier & micrometer for first-off inspection |
| **Assembly Lines** | Pneumatic comparators for fast go/no-go gauging |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of Least Count of Vernier Caliper

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement to Prove:** The least count of a vernier caliper having $n$ vernier scale divisions coinciding with $(n-1)$ main scale divisions equals $\dfrac{1\,mm}{n}$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let the value of **1 Main Scale Division (MSD)** be $1\,mm$ (standard metric rule).

By the design principle of the vernier scale:
$$n \text{ VSD} = (n - 1) \text{ MSD}$$

Therefore, the value of one **Vernier Scale Division (VSD)** is:
$$\begin{aligned}
1 \text{ VSD} &= \dfrac{(n-1) \text{ MSD}}{n} \\
&= \dfrac{(n-1) \times 1\,mm}{n} \\
&= \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

The **Least Count** is defined as the difference between **1 MSD** and **1 VSD**:
$$\begin{aligned}
LC &= 1 \text{ MSD} - 1 \text{ VSD} \\
&= 1\,mm - \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{n}\right)\,mm \\
&= 1\,mm - 1\,mm + \dfrac{1\,mm}{n} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{n}\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{LC = \dfrac{1\,mm}{n}}$

**Numerical Verification (Typical KTU Problem):**
For a vernier caliper with **20 VSD = 19 MSD**:
$$LC = \dfrac{1\,mm}{20} = 0.05\,mm$$

For the common **50 VSD = 49 MSD** vernier:
$$LC = \dfrac{1\,mm}{50} = 0.02\,mm$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Least Count of Micrometer (Screw Gauge)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement to Prove:** For a metric micrometer with a screw pitch of **0.5 mm/rev** and **50** graduations on the thimble, the least count is **0.01 mm**.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The micrometer spindle is a precision screw. The **pitch** $p$ is the axial distance the spindle advances in one complete revolution.

For a standard metric micrometer:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Pitch, } p &= 0.5\,mm/\text{revolution} \\
\text{Number of thimble divisions, } n &= 50
\end{aligned}$$

The **Least Count** is defined as the linear advance per **one thimble division**:
$$\begin{aligned}
LC &= \dfrac{\text{Pitch}}{\text{No. of thimble divisions}} \\
&= \dfrac{p}{n} \\
&= \dfrac{0.5\,mm}{50} \\
&= 0.01\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{LC = 0.01\,mm \text{ (for standard metric micrometer)}}$

**Conversion to Microns:** $0.01\,mm = 10\,\mu m$ — adequate for most workshop measurements but insufficient for ultra-precision jobs (hence CMM and laser interferometers are used).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Sine Bar Angle

> [!NOTE]
> **Statement to Prove:** $\sin \theta = \dfrac{h}{l}$ for a sine bar of length $l$ with a slip-gauge stack of height $h$ raising one end to set an angle $\theta$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Consider a sine bar of length $l$ resting on a flat surface plate at one roller (A), and on a slip-gauge stack of height $h$ at the other roller (B).

The geometry forms a **right-angled triangle**:
- **Hypotenuse** = $l$ (the sine bar length).
- **Opposite side (to angle $\theta$)** = $h$ (slip-gauge stack height).
- **Adjacent side** = horizontal distance on surface plate.

From the definition of sine in a right triangle:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin \theta &= \dfrac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Hypotenuse}} \\
&= \dfrac{h}{l}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the **angle** the sine bar makes with the surface plate is:
$$\boxed{\theta = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{h}{l}\right)}$$

**Typical KTU Problem:**
> Set a **15°** angle using a **200 mm** sine bar. Calculate the slip-gauge height required.

**Solution:**
$$\begin{aligned}
h &= l \times \sin \theta \\
&= 200 \times \sin 15° \\
&= 200 \times 0.2588 \\
&= 51.76\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

So the student must build a slip-gauge stack totalling **51.76 mm** (or 51.76 + multiples of 0.01 for fine corrections).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Instrument Selection Decision Tree

| Workpiece Feature | Recommended Instrument | Reason |
|-------------------|------------------------|--------|
| Outside diameter (small, 0–25 mm) | **Micrometer** | High accuracy (LC = 0.01 mm) |
| Outside diameter (25–300 mm) | **Vernier Caliper** | Convenient range and adequate LC |
| Inside diameter (large) | **Inside Micrometer / Bore Gauge** | Self-centering, repeatable |
| Depth of slot | **Vernier Depth Gauge** | Vertical needle reference |
| Height from surface plate | **Vernier Height Gauge** | Reference from datum plane |
| Surface flatness | **Optical Flat + Monochromatic Light** | Interference fringe method |
| Surface roughness | **Stylus Profilometer (Talysurf)** | Quantitative Ra / Rz |
| Taper angle | **Sine Bar + Slip Gauges** | Highest angular accuracy |
| Quick angle check | **Bevel Protractor** | Portable and fast |
| Runout / concentricity | **Dial Indicator on Lathe Mandrel** | Dynamic eccentricity check |
| 3D feature inspection | **Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM)** | Multi-axis probe scanning |

---

### 3.5 Zero-Error Correction Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Procedure for Applying Zero Error:**
> $$\text{Corrected Reading} = \text{Observed Reading} - (\text{Zero Error})$$

**Case 1 — Positive Zero Error:**
The thimble/vernier zero is **below** the datum line.
- Reading with zero error: **+0.03 mm**
- Corrected reading = Observed − 0.03 mm

**Case 2 — Negative Zero Error:**
The thimble/vernier zero is **above** the datum line.
- Reading with zero error: **−0.02 mm**
- Corrected reading = Observed − (−0.02) = Observed + 0.02 mm

**Numerical Problem (KTU pattern):**
> The main scale reads **12.00 mm** and the vernier coincidence is at **7**, with LC = 0.02 mm. The instrument has a zero error of **+0.04 mm**. Find the true dimension.

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Observed Reading} &= MSR + (VSR \times LC) \\
&= 12.00 + (7 \times 0.02) \\
&= 12.00 + 0.14 \\
&= 12.14\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{True Dimension} &= 12.14 - (+0.04) \\
&= 12.10\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

$\boxed{\text{True Dimension} = 12.10\,mm}$

---

### 3.6 Comparison Table: Vernier Caliper vs Micrometer

| Parameter | Vernier Caliper | Micrometer |
|-----------|-----------------|------------|
| LC (typical) | **0.02 mm** | **0.01 mm** |
| Accuracy | ±0.02 to ±0.05 mm | ±0.01 to ±0.02 mm |
| Range (standard) | 0–150, 0–300 mm | 0–25 mm (incremental sets) |
| Principle | Vernier coincidence | Screw thread pitch |
| Reading method | Manual coincidence on two scales | Thimble vs sleeve graduation |
| Zero error possibility | Yes (sliding jaw) | Yes (anvil-spindle contact) |
| Major limitation | Parallax + jaw wear | Limited range; requires ratchet |
| Best for | Quick, versatile shop-floor checks | High-precision diameter measurements |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification of Precision Instruments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PRECISION MEASURING INSTRUMENTS"]
    B1["LINEAR DIMENSIONS"]
    B2["ANGULAR DIMENSIONS"]
    B3["SURFACE FINISH"]

    C1["Vernier Caliper"]
    C2["Micrometer Screw Gauge"]

    C7["CMM"]

    D1["Bevel Protractor"]
    D2["Sine Bar With Slip Gauges"]
    D3["Spirit Level Clinometer"]

    E1["Stylus Profilometer Talysurf"]
    E2["Optical Flat Monochromatic Light"]

    F1["Mechanical Comparator"]
    F2["Optical Comparator"]
    F3["Electrical LVDT Comparator"]
    F4["Pneumatic Comparator"]

    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    A --> B3
    A --> B4

    B1 --> C1
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> C3
    B1 --> C4
    B1 --> C5
    B1 --> C6
    B1 --> C7

    B2 --> D1
    B2 --> D2
    B2 --> D3

    B3 --> E1
    B3 --> E2

    B4 --> F1
    B4 --> F2
    B4 --> F3
    B4 --> F4
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Functional Architecture of a Measuring Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WP["WORKPIECE TO BE MEASURED"]
    STD["CALIBRATION STANDARD\nSlip Gauge OR Reference Block"]
    INST["MEASURING INSTRUMENT\nVernier / Micrometer / DTI"]
    OP["OPERATOR OBSERVATION\nReading and Coincidence"]
    CALC["CALCULATION STEP\nMSR plus VSR times LC"]
    ZC["ZERO ERROR CORRECTION"]
    OUT["FINAL DIMENSION OUTPUT\nMillimetre or Micron"]
    QC["QUALITY CONTROL DECISION\nACCEPT or REJECT within Tolerance"]

    WP --> INST
    STD --> INST
    INST --> OP
    OP --> CALC
    CALC --> ZC
    ZC --> OUT
    OUT --> QC
    QC -->|Within Tolerance| PASS["ACCEPTED PART"]
    QC -->|Outside Tolerance| FAIL["REJECTED OR REWORK"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Working Principle of Optical Comparator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Light Source"]
    L1["Collimating Lens"]
    M["Mirror Mounted on Comparator Plunger"]
    L2["Projecting Lens"]
    S["Screen With Magnified Scale"]
    WP["Workpiece Placed Under Plunger"]

    P --> L1
    L1 --> M
    M --> L2
    L2 --> S
    WP -->|Displaces plunger| M
```

> [!TIP]
> **Working Note:** When the workpiece lifts (or lowers) the plunger by a distance $x$, the mirror tilts through an angle $\theta = x/d$ (where $d$ is the mirror-to-pivot distance). The reflected beam swings through $2\theta$, magnifying the displacement onto the screen — typically achieving magnification of **×1000 to ×5000**.

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Slip Gauge Wringing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SELECT SLIP GAUGES FROM GRADE 0 SET"]
    B["WIPE BOTH FACES WITH CHAMOIS LEATHER"]
    C["APPLY LIGHT FILM OF PETROLEUM"]
    D["PRESS GAUGES TOGETHER WITH SLIDING MOTION"]
    E["CHECK WEDGE LIGHT INTERFERENCE FRINGES"]
    F["STACK BUILDS UP TO TARGET DIMENSION"]
    G["USE FOR SINE BAR OR HEIGHT GAUGE CALIBRATION"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E -->|No Fringes| F
    E -->|Fringes Detected| H["REJECT AND RECLEAN"]
    H --> B
    F --> G
```

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define least count of a measuring instrument. Calculate the least count of a vernier caliper having 25 divisions on the vernier scale coinciding with 24 divisions on the main scale (1 MSD = 1 mm).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Definition (1 mark):** *Least count* is the smallest dimension that an instrument can measure or resolve accurately. It is the difference between one main scale division and one vernier scale division.
>
> **Calculation (2 marks):**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> LC &= \dfrac{MSD}{n} \\
> &= \dfrac{1\,mm}{25} \\
> &= 0.04\,mm
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> $\boxed{LC = 0.04\,mm}$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State Abbe's Principle. Why is it important in precision measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Statement (2 marks):** *Abbe's Principle* states that *"the axis of the measuring scale should be collinear with the axis of the dimension being measured."* In other words, the measurement should be taken **in line** with the feature, not offset.
>
> **Importance (1 mark):** When the measuring scale is offset from the workpiece axis by a distance $h$ and the component tilts by a small angle $\alpha$, an **Abbe offset error** $\delta = h \tan \alpha$ is introduced. For precision instruments like micrometers and height gauges, the measurement axis is therefore always **aligned** with the feature to minimize this error.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Always state the given data** before applying any formula — half the marks go to data identification.
> 2. **Mention units** at every step. Missing units = −1 mark per page.
> 3. **Box the final answer** in a $\boxed{}$ to make it instantly visible to the examiner.
> 4. **Draw a labelled sketch** of the instrument in Part B answers — at least 1 mark is reserved for diagrams.
> 5. **Do not mix up** zero error signs; confusing positive and negative zero error loses 2–3 marks.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **vernier height gauge**. Discuss how the least count is determined. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workpiece is measured using a vernier height gauge. The main scale reads **85 mm** and the vernier scale division **14** coincides with the main scale line. If the vernier has **50 divisions** on a **49 mm** main-scale length, calculate:
- (i) The least count of the instrument.
- (ii) The total reading observed.
- (iii) The true dimension, given a zero error of **−0.04 mm**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working of Vernier Height Gauge (7 marks)**

A **vernier height gauge** is a precision instrument used to measure the **vertical height** of features from a reference surface plate. It consists of:

1. **Base (Heavy Cast Iron):** Provides a stable, flat reference on the surface plate. The base has a **reference edge** that sits flat on the plate.
2. **Vertical Column (Beam):** A rigid, polished steel column graduated with a **main scale in mm**.
3. **Sliding Vernier Carriage:** Mounted on the column, contains the **vernier scale** and a clamping screw.
4. **Scriber / Measuring Jaw:** A pointed scriber (or an attachment for a dial probe) is fixed to the carriage for transferring the dimension.
5. **Fine Adjustment Slider:** Enables precise vertical positioning.
6. **Surface Plate (Accessory):** A precision granite reference on which the gauge rests.

**Working Principle:**
- The base of the height gauge is placed on a **flat surface plate**.
- The scriber is adjusted to touch the **top surface** of the workpiece (or feature to be measured).
- The main-scale reading is taken at the **edge** of the vernier carriage.
- The vernier coincidence is identified to obtain the fractional reading.
- The dimension = MSR + (VSR × LC).

**Sketch (1 mark):**
```
        ┌────────┐  ← Scriber
        │        │
   ─────┤  Vernier Carriage ────► Main Scale (mm)
        │        │
        │  Beam  │
        │ Column│
        │        │
        └────────┘
   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ← Surface Plate
   ═══════╧═══════  ← Reference Base
```

**Least Count Determination (3 marks):**
For a 50-division vernier coinciding with 49 mm on the main scale:
$$\begin{aligned}
1\,VSD &= \dfrac{49\,mm}{50} = 0.98\,mm \\
1\,MSD &= 1\,mm \\
LC &= 1\,MSD - 1\,VSD \\
&= 1.00 - 0.98 \\
&= 0.02\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Least Count (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
LC &= \dfrac{MSD}{n} = \dfrac{1\,mm}{50} = 0.02\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Total Observed Reading (2 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Reading} &= MSR + (VSR \times LC) \\
&= 85 + (14 \times 0.02) \\
&= 85 + 0.28 \\
&= 85.28\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) True Dimension with Zero Error Correction (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{True Dimension} &= \text{Observed} - (\text{Zero Error}) \\
&= 85.28 - (-0.04) \\
&= 85.28 + 0.04 \\
&= 85.32\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

$\boxed{\text{True Dimension} = 85.32\,mm}$

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** Explain with a sketch the **principle and working of an optical comparator**. Derive the expression for its magnification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **sine bar** of length **200 mm** is used to set an angle of **20°30′**. Calculate the height of the slip-gauge stack required. If the sine bar is used with rollers of **20 mm diameter** placed on a **100 mm centre distance**, derive the expression and find the required stack height for the same angle using the roller method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Optical Comparator (7 marks)**

An **optical comparator** is a non-contact precision instrument that amplifies small linear displacements of a workpiece through an **optical lever** mechanism, projecting the magnified image onto a viewing screen.

**Constructional Components:**
1. **Light Source** — produces a parallel, intense beam.
2. **Collimating Lens** — makes the light rays parallel.
3. **Plane Mirror** — small mirror mounted on a tilting pivot connected to the comparator plunger.
4. **Projecting Lens** — focuses the reflected beam onto the screen.
5. **Ground Glass Screen** — graduated in mm; magnified image of an index line is projected onto it.
6. **Plunger** — contacts the workpiece; vertical displacement tilts the mirror.

**Working Principle:**
- When the workpiece is placed under the plunger, the plunger rises by a small distance $x$.
- The mirror tilts through an angle $\theta = x/d$ (where $d$ is the mirror-to-pivot distance).
- The reflected beam rotates through $2\theta$, and the projected spot on the screen moves through a magnified distance $y = 2L \tan \theta \approx 2L\theta$ (for small $\theta$).

**Magnification Derivation (3 marks):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnification, } M &= \dfrac{y}{x} \\
&= \dfrac{2L \theta}{x} \\
&= \dfrac{2L \cdot \dfrac{x}{d}}{x} \\
&= \dfrac{2L}{d}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{M = \dfrac{2L}{d}}$$

Where:
- $L$ = distance from mirror to screen.
- $d$ = distance from plunger to mirror pivot.

Typical values of $M$ range from **×500 to ×5000**.

---

**(b) Sine Bar Numerical (7 marks)**

**Method 1 — Direct Slip-Gauge Method (3 marks):**
For a 200 mm sine bar at angle $\theta = 20°30′$:
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= 20° + \dfrac{30}{60}° = 20.5° \\
h &= l \times \sin \theta \\
&= 200 \times \sin 20.5° \\
&= 200 \times 0.35021 \\
&= 70.04\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

**Method 2 — Using Rollers (4 marks):**
When rollers of radius $r$ are placed on both rollers with the workpiece on top, the formula becomes:
$$\begin{aligned}
\sin \theta &= \dfrac{R - r}{C}
\end{aligned}$$

Where $R$ is the radius of the workpiece, $r$ is the roller radius, and $C$ is the centre distance. The slip-gauge stack height $h$ between the rollers is then:
$$h = (R - r) + \sqrt{C^2 - (R - r)^2} \cdot 0 \quad \text{(for direct calculation, simplified to } h = R - r \text{ for thin rollers)}$$

For practical slip-gauge calculation:
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Roller radius} &= \dfrac{20}{2} = 10\,mm \\
\text{Centre distance, } C &= 100\,mm \\
\sin 20.5° &= 0.35021
\end{aligned}$$

From $\sin \theta = \dfrac{R - r}{C}$:
$$\begin{aligned}
R - r &= C \cdot \sin \theta \\
&= 100 \times 0.35021 \\
&= 35.021\,mm
\end{aligned}$$

The required slip-gauge stack height (vertical lift between rollers):
$$\begin{aligned}
h &= (R - r) + 2r = 35.02 + 20 = 55.02\,mm \\
&\text{(if rollers are placed under the workpiece)} \\
\text{or simply } h &= R - r = 35.02\,mm \text{ (for centre-height method)}
\end{aligned}$$

$\boxed{h = 70.04\,mm \text{ (direct)} \quad | \quad h = 35.02\,mm \text{ (roller method)}}$

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ✅ **Vernier Caliper LC** = $1/n$ mm (most common **0.02 mm** with $n = 50$).
- ✅ **Micrometer LC** = Pitch / Thimble divisions = **0.5/50 = 0.01 mm** (standard metric).
- ✅ **Zero Error Formula:** $R_{\text{true}} = R_{\text{observed}} - Z.E.$
- ✅ **Sine Bar:** $\sin \theta = h / l$ → calculate $h$ for required angle.
- ✅ **Bevel Protractor** reads directly with vernier LC of **5 minutes**.
- ✅ **Dial Indicator (DTI):** LC marked on dial face (commonly 0.01 mm); total plunger travel = ±1 mm or ±0.8 mm.
- ✅ **Slip Gauges:** Wringing builds up dimensions; **Grade 0** is the standard reference grade.
- ✅ **Optical Comparator Magnification:** $M = 2L / d$.
- ✅ **Abbe's Principle:** Measurement axis collinear with workpiece axis.
- ✅ **Surface Roughness:** Ra (arithmetic mean) is the most reported parameter, in µm.
- ✅ **CMM:** Three-axis probe; resolution **1 µm**; uses Cartesian coordinates.
- ✅ Always **state LC and units** at the start of any numerical.
- ✅ **Box the final answer** for examiner visibility.
- ✅ Sketch even crude diagrams — at least **1 mark** is reserved for them.
- ✅ Common mistake: **forgetting the sign** of zero error — double-check before final answer.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Centre Lathe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Centre Lathe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centre Lathe (Engine Lathe):** A Centre Lathe is a fundamental, manually or semi-automatically controlled machine tool used for removing unwanted material from a cylindrical workpiece by rotating the workpiece against a stationary single-point cutting tool. The workpiece is held between two rigid supports — a *live centre* (driven by the spindle in the headstock) and a *dead centre* (stationary support in the tailstock) — hence the name "Centre Lathe."

It is the most versatile and widely used machine tool in any manufacturing industry, capable of performing a diverse range of operations such as turning, facing, knurling, parting, thread cutting, drilling, boring, and reaming on metallic and non-metallic workpieces.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Potter's Wheel and a Sharpened Stick**
> 
> Imagine an ancient **potter's wheel**. The potter spins a lump of wet clay (the *workpiece*) continuously. While the clay rotates, the potter gently presses a shaped tool (their fingers or a wooden rib — the *cutting tool*) against the surface, slowly shaving away the excess to craft a perfect bowl. 
> 
> A **Centre Lathe works on the exact same principle**, but with industrial precision:
> - The **potter's wheel** = the **Spindle & Chuck** (rotating drive)
> - The **rotating clay** = the **Workpiece** (held and spun)
> - The **potter's fingers** = the **Single-Point Cutting Tool** (removes material)
> - The **potter's steady hand sliding sideways** = the **Carriage & Cross-slide** (controlled linear feed)
> 
> The lathe simply mechanises, powers, and adds rigid precision to this ancient craft.

## 1.3 Standard Classification Metrics

The performance and physical envelope of any centre lathe is quantified by **five standard specifications**, which are **bold**-marked for KTU emphasis:

1. **Length of Bed** – Maximum length of workpiece that can be mounted (e.g., 1000 mm, 1500 mm).
2. **Swing Over Bed** – Maximum diameter of workpiece that can rotate above the bed ways (e.g., 250 mm, 400 mm).
3. **Swing Over Cross Slide** – Maximum diameter that can be turned using the cross-slide (always less than swing over bed).
4. **Spindle Bore** – Internal diameter of the hollow spindle through which long bar stock can be fed.
5. **Number of Spindle Speeds / Feed Rates** – Discrete or infinitely variable speed/feed settings available.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Whenever a numerical problem mentions a "standard lathe," the examiner expects you to identify which specification limits the operation. For example, if *Swing Over Bed = 250 mm*, the maximum **diameter** of any workpiece must be ≤ 250 mm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle – "Rotating Work, Stationary Tool"

The operational logic of a centre lathe can be broken into **four sequential energy-conversion steps**:

- **Step 1: Electrical → Mechanical (Prime Mover)** — An electric motor (typically 3-phase AC induction, 1–10 HP) supplies rotational energy to a multi-step **gearbox** (headstock).
- **Step 2: Speed Reduction & Selection** — The gearbox reduces the high motor RPM (e.g., 1440 rpm) to usable spindle speeds (typically 30–2000 rpm) via lever-operated gear trains.
- **Step 3: Workpiece Holding & Rotation** — The spindle, fitted with a **chuck** or **face plate**, clamps the workpiece and rotates it at the selected RPM (N).
- **Step 4: Controlled Tool Engagement (Feed)** — A single-point cutting tool, mounted on the **tool post** of the carriage, is advanced linearly against the rotating workpiece. The **cross-slide** provides radial feed (depth of cut), while the **longitudinal carriage** provides axial feed (turning length).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind the Design:** The lathe deliberately rotates the *workpiece* (not the tool) because the workpiece is generally bulkier, asymmetric, and harder to dynamically balance at high speeds. Keeping the tool stationary allows precise, vibration-free linear motion via slides.

## 2.2 Main Parts of a Centre Lathe (Anatomy)

A centre lathe is built from the following primary subassemblies. Each has a strict, non-interchangeable function:

| # | Part | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bed** | Heavy, cast-iron base. Provides rigid support and accurate alignment of headstock, tailstock, and carriage via precision-ground **V-ways** and **flat ways**. |
| 2 | **Headstock** | Houses the **spindle**, gear train, and speed-change levers. Drives the workpiece via a chuck. |
| 3 | **Tailstock** | Supports the free end of long workpieces via a **dead centre**. Can be slid along the bed and clamped. Its **quill** allows in/out travel for drilling. |
| 4 | **Carriage** | Moves longitudinally along the bed. Houses the apron, cross-slide, compound rest, and tool post. |
| 5 | **Cross-Slide** | Mounted on the carriage. Provides **radial (in-feed)** motion for depth of cut. |
| 6 | **Compound Rest** | Swivel-mounted on cross-slide. Can be rotated to any angle (essential for taper turning and thread cutting). |
| 7 | **Tool Post** | Clamps the single-point cutting tool. Four-way (or quick-change) type allows up to 4 tools simultaneously. |
| 8 | **Apron** | Attached to the carriage front. Contains feed mechanism, half-nuts (for thread cutting), and operating levers. |
| 9 | **Feed Rod & Lead Screw** | Transmit power from headstock to the apron for automatic longitudinal feed and thread cutting respectively. |
| 10 | **Legs / Cabinet** | Support the bed and house the motor, coolant pump, and electricals. |

## 2.3 Work-Holding Devices (Chucks & Supports)

- **3-Jaw Self-Centring Chuck** – Holds round/hexagonal workpieces; all 3 jaws move simultaneously to centre the work automatically.
- **4-Jaw Independent Chuck** – Each jaw moves independently; used for **square, rectangular, or off-centre** workpieces.
- **Face Plate** – A circular plate bolted to the spindle; workpieces are bolted/clamped to it for irregular shapes.
- **Mandrel** – A hardened cylindrical plug used to hold **finished hollow workpieces** (e.g., bushes) by driving it through the bore.
- **Between Centres** – For long slender shafts, using a **lathe dog** and a **dead centre** in the tailstock.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL NOTE FOR TABLES:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is forbidden inside table rows. Absolute value is written using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown syntax.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutting Speed (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | Surface speed of workpiece |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Required spindle rpm |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (fr)** | $f_r = f \times N$ | mm/min | Tool advance per minute |
| 4 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | Volume of metal removed per minute |
| 5 | **Machining Time (tm)** | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f_r} = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | Time to complete one pass |
| 6 | **Power (P)** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \times U_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ | kW | Spindle power requirement |
| 7 | **Taper (T)** | $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | mm/mm | Taper ratio (e.g., 1:20) |

**Legend of Variables:**
- $D$ = Initial/Major diameter of workpiece (mm)
- $d$ = Final/Minor diameter after turning (mm)
- $L$ = Length of cut (mm)
- $N$ = Spindle speed (rpm)
- $f$ = Feed per revolution (mm/rev)
- $f_r$ = Feed rate (mm/min)
- $U_c$ = Specific cutting energy (J/mm³)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** These seven equations form the **backbone of every CNC programming manual, process planning sheet, and shop-floor costing calculation** in modern manufacturing. A process engineer in an automotive plant uses Equation (1) daily to balance tool life vs. cycle time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Cutting Speed and Spindle Speed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Expectation:** When asked to "derive" a formula, you must start from a **geometric first principle** (definition of the quantity), apply the relevant **SI unit conversion**, and arrive at the final engineering form. Skipping the geometry step is the #1 reason students lose marks.

### 3.1.1 Geometric Starting Point

The **cutting speed (V)** is defined as the **relative surface speed between the cutting tool and the workpiece at the point of contact**. For a rotating cylindrical workpiece, this is simply the tangential velocity of a point on its outer surface.

The circumference of a circle of diameter $D$ (in millimetres) is:

$$C = \pi \cdot D \quad \text{[mm per revolution]}$$

### 3.1.2 Relating Revolutions to Time

If the spindle rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then the **linear distance** traversed by any point on the surface per minute is:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = N \times C = N \times \pi \cdot D \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

### 3.1.3 Unit Conversion to Metres per Minute

By KTU and standard shop-floor convention, cutting speed is expressed in **metres per minute**, not millimetres per minute. Therefore, we divide by **1000**:

$$V = \frac{N \times \pi \cdot D}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

This is the **final derived form** of the cutting-speed equation. Rearranging for spindle speed:

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{[rpm]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Justification:** 1 metre = 1000 mm. Since $D$ is given in mm and we need $V$ in m/min, the division by 1000 is **mandatory** to prevent a 1000× unit error. This is a classic KTU valuation pitfall.

## 3.2 Derivation: Machining Time for a Turning Operation

### 3.2.1 Definition of Feed

The **feed (f)** is the distance the cutting tool advances along the workpiece **per spindle revolution**, measured in mm/rev. Therefore, in one minute (N revolutions), the tool advances:

$$f_r = f \times N \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

### 3.2.2 Definition of Machining Time

**Machining time ($t_m$)** is the total time required for the tool to traverse the full length of the cut ($L$).

$$t_m = \frac{\text{Total Length of Cut}}{\text{Distance Travelled per Minute}} = \frac{L}{f_r}$$

### 3.2.3 Substituting the Feed Rate

Substituting $f_r = f \times N$:

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{[minutes]}$$

### 3.2.4 Full Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A mild-steel shaft of initial diameter $D = 80$ mm is to be turned down to $d = 76$ mm over a length $L = 300$ mm. The recommended cutting speed is $V = 30$ m/min and feed is $f = 0.3$ mm/rev. Calculate: **(a)** Spindle speed $N$, **(b)** Feed rate $f_r$, **(c)** Machining time $t_m$, **(d)** Depth of cut $d_c$, **(e)** MRR.

**Step 1: Depth of Cut ($d_c$)**

$$d_c = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{80 - 76}{2} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ mm}$$

*(We use the average diameter for force/MRR calculations, but the starting diameter $D$ for spindle speed.)*

**Step 2: Spindle Speed (N)** — Using the initial diameter $D = 80$ mm (largest, slowest surface speed):

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 80} = \frac{30000}{251.327} \approx 119.37 \text{ rpm}$$

We round up to the **nearest available spindle speed on the lathe**, e.g., **120 rpm**.

**Step 3: Feed Rate ($f_r$)**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.3 \times 120 = 36 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 4: Machining Time ($t_m$)**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{300}{36} = 8.333 \text{ min} = 8 \text{ min } 20 \text{ sec}$$

**Step 5: Material Removal Rate (MRR)**

$$MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f \cdot d_c = \pi \times 80 \times 300 \times 0.3 \times 2 = 45,238.93 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 120 \text{ rpm}, \quad f_r = 36 \text{ mm/min}, \quad t_m = 8.33 \text{ min}, \quad MRR \approx 45,239 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common Lathe Operations vs. Tool Geometry

| Operation | Tool Used | Tool Motion | Workpiece Mounting | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Straight Turning** | Turning tool (right-hand) | Longitudinal | Chuck / Centres | ★★★★★ |
| **Facing** | Facing tool | Radial (cross-slide) | Chuck / Centres | ★★★★★ |
| **Taper Turning** | Compound rest / Tool offset | Angular | Chuck | ★★★★ |
| **Thread Cutting** | Threading tool (60° HSS) | Synchronized via lead screw | Chuck / Centres | ★★★★★ |
| **Parting / Cut-off** | Parting tool | Radial in-feed | Chuck | ★★★ |
| **Knurling** | Knurling tool (diamond/parallel) | Longitudinal (pressed in) | Centres | ★★ |
| **Drilling** | Drill bit in tailstock | Tailstock quill feed | Chuck | ★★★ |
| **Boring** | Boring bar | Longitudinal | Chuck (pre-drilled hole) | ★★★ |
| **Reaming** | Reamer | Tailstock quill | Chuck (pre-drilled) | ★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Centre Lathe

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Block Diagram (and not a physical sketch)?** Mermaid cannot render photorealistic engineering drawings. A **block-level functional architecture flow** is the KTU-accepted alternative, showing how power and motion flow between subassemblies.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MOTOR 3-Phase AC] --> B[SPINDLE GEARBOX]
    B --> C[MAIN SPINDLE]
    C --> D[CHUCK 3-Jaw]
    D --> E[WORKPIECE CYLINDRICAL]
    E --> F[DEAD CENTRE TAILSTOCK]

    B --> G[FEED ROD]
    G --> H[APRON GEAR TRAIN]
    H --> I[CARRIAGE LONGITUDINAL]

    I --> J[CROSS SLIDE RADIAL]
    J --> K[COMPOUND REST ANGULAR]
    K --> L[TOOL POST 4-WAY]
    L --> M[CUTTING TOOL SINGLE POINT]

    B --> N[LEAD SCREW]
    N --> H
    H --> O[HALF NUTS THREADING]

    P[TAILSTOCK HANDWHEEL] --> Q[QUILL DRILLING]
    Q --> E

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style M fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.1.1 Power & Motion Flow Explanation

- **Primary Power Path (Red Path):** Motor → Gearbox → Spindle → Chuck → Workpiece. This delivers the **rotational motion** required for cutting.
- **Feed Power Path (Orange Path):** Gearbox → Feed Rod → Apron → Carriage. This delivers **automatic longitudinal feed** to the tool.
- **Threading Power Path (Purple Path):** Gearbox → Lead Screw → Half-Nuts → Carriage. The lead screw's precisely cut helical groove **synchronises** tool movement with spindle rotation to generate accurate threads.
- **Radial Feed Path (Yellow Path):** Cross-slide handwheel → Cross-slide → Compound Rest → Tool post. Provides **depth of cut**.
- **Drilling Path (Pink Path):** Tailstock handwheel → Quill → Drill bit → Workpiece. Independent of spindle rotation; used for axial hole-making.

## 4.2 Mermaid Tree — Hierarchy of Lathe Operations

```mermaid
graph TD
    LATHE[Centre Lathe Operations]
    LATHE --> OP1[Turning]
    LATHE --> OP2[Facing]
    LATHE --> OP3[Thread Cutting]
    LATHE --> OP4[Knurling]
    LATHE --> OP5[Parting]
    LATHE --> OP6[Hole Making]
    HOLE[Hole Making] --> DRILL[Drilling]
    HOLE --> BORE[Boring]
    HOLE --> REAM[Reaming]
    HOLE --> TAP[Tapping]
    OP1 --> ST[Straight Turning]
    OP1 --> TAP2[Taper Turning]
    OP1 --> CONTOUR[Contour Turning]
    OP3 --> INTM[Internal Thread]
    OP3 --> EXTM[External Thread]

    style LATHE fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    style HOLE fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OP1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style OP3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Tool-Workpiece Geometry in Turning

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Schematic** (acceptable in KTU answer scripts when diagram tools are unavailable):

```
                  Spindle Rotation (N rpm)
                          ↓
                  ┌───────────────────┐
   Feed (f)  →    ║  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ║   ← Workpiece (Ø D)
   direction →    ║  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ║
   (axial)   →    ╚═══════════════════╝
                        ║
                        ║ Depth of cut (d_c)
                        ║
                        ▼
                  ──────────────────  ← New surface (Ø d)
                       
                  ▲
                  │
                  └── Single-point cutting tool
                      (Rake angle α, Clearance angle γ)
```

**Key Geometric Parameters on the Tool:**
- **Rake Angle (α):** Angle of the tool face that allows the chip to flow smoothly.
- **Clearance Angle (γ):** Prevents the tool flank from rubbing against the freshly machined surface.
- **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ):** Orientation of the cutting edge relative to the workpiece axis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme — PCNSL308):** Lab courses under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme typically have a **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** component (record + viva + day-to-day assessment) of **60 marks** and an **End Semester Evaluation (ESE)** of **40 marks**. Questions below model the ESE written/oral pattern, with **3-mark short answer (Part A)** and **14-mark analytical (Part B)** weightages.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1]**
> **Q1. List any six main parts of a centre lathe and state the function of the headstock.**
> *(3 Marks — CO1, Remember Level)*

**Model Answer:**

The six main parts of a centre lathe are: (i) **Bed**, (ii) **Headstock**, (iii) **Tailstock**, (iv) **Carriage**, (v) **Cross-slide**, and (vi) **Tool Post**.

**Function of the Headstock:** The headstock is mounted on the left end of the bed and houses the **main spindle**, the **gear train (gearbox)**, and the **speed-change levers**. Its primary function is to (a) transmit rotational power from the electric motor to the spindle, (b) allow selection of the required spindle speed (rpm) for different diameters and materials, and (c) hold the **chuck or face plate** via the spindle nose for clamping the workpiece. *(3 marks — 1 for listing, 2 for the headstock function)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2]**
> **Q2. Differentiate between a 3-jaw self-centring chuck and a 4-jaw independent chuck.**
> *(3 Marks — CO2, Understand Level)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | 3-Jaw Self-Centring Chuck | 4-Jaw Independent Chuck |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Jaws | 3 jaws | 4 jaws |
| Movement | All 3 jaws move **simultaneously** via scroll plate | Each jaw moves **independently** |
| Centring | **Automatic** centring of round/hex work | **Manual** centring; suitable for square/rectangular work |
| Centring Accuracy | Moderate (±0.05 mm) | High (±0.01 mm with dial indicator) |
| Typical Use | Round bars, hex bars | Square blocks, off-centre mounting, asymmetric work |
| Operation Time | Fast | Slow and skilled |

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for valid points on each chuck)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Analytical Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> 
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a centre lathe**, clearly identifying the **primary power transmission path** from the motor to the cutting tool. Label the headstock, tailstock, carriage, feed rod, and lead screw. *(7 Marks — CO1, Understand Level)*
> 
> **(b)** A **mild-steel workpiece of 60 mm diameter and 250 mm length** is to be reduced to **56 mm diameter** in a single pass on a centre lathe. The recommended cutting speed is $V = 25$ m/min and the feed is $f = 0.25$ mm/rev. Calculate: **(i)** Spindle speed $N$, **(ii)** Feed rate $f_r$, **(iii)** Depth of cut, and **(iv)** Machining time. *(7 Marks — CO3, Apply Level)*

### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 Marks):**
*(Draw the Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 here, or a neatly labelled sketch.)*

The power transmission path is:
- **Step 1:** 3-phase AC motor → 
- **Step 2:** Spindle gearbox (speed reduction and selection) → 
- **Step 3:** Main spindle → 
- **Step 4:** 3-jaw chuck → 
- **Step 5:** Workpiece (rotates) → 
- **Step 6:** Single-point cutting tool removes material.

The **feed rod** (for plain turning) and **lead screw** (for thread cutting) transmit power from the headstock gearbox to the **apron** on the carriage, which converts rotary motion into linear carriage feed. The **tailstock** supports long workpieces via a dead centre and its quill enables drilling. *(7 marks — 4 for diagram, 3 for explanation)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $D = 60$ mm, $d = 56$ mm, $L = 250$ mm, $V = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev.

**(i) Spindle Speed:**

$$N = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{25000}{188.496} \approx 132.63 \text{ rpm}$$

**Round up to standard available speed: $N = 140$ rpm** *(Nearest standard on most lathes)*

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.25 \times 140 = 35 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Depth of Cut:**

$$d_c = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{60 - 56}{2} = 2 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Machining Time:**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{250}{35} = 7.143 \text{ min} = 7 \text{ min } 8.6 \text{ sec}$$

**Final Answer Box:** $N \approx 140$ rpm, $f_r = 35$ mm/min, $d_c = 2$ mm, $t_m \approx 7.14$ min.

*(7 marks — 1.75 each sub-part)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative]**
> 
> **Question B (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** Explain the following lathe operations with neat sketches: **(i) Facing, (ii) Knurling, (iii) Parting off**. State one industrial application of each. *(7 Marks — CO2, Understand Level)*
> 
> **(b)** A **cast-iron shaft of 100 mm diameter** is turned at a **spindle speed of 200 rpm** with a **feed of 0.4 mm/rev** over a **length of 500 mm**. Calculate: **(i)** Cutting speed, **(ii)** Feed rate, **(iii)** MRR, and **(iv)** Machining time. *(7 Marks — CO3, Apply Level)*

### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Operations Explanation (7 Marks):**

- **(i) Facing:** The tool is fed **radially inward** (via cross-slide) from the outer diameter toward the axis of rotation, producing a flat surface perpendicular to the axis. **Application:** Producing a flat reference face on a shaft shoulder. *(2.3 marks)*
- **(ii) Knurling:** A hard, patterned roller tool is **pressed** into the rotating workpiece (no actual chip removal; it displaces material) to create a diamond or straight-line textured pattern. **Application:** Providing a slip-resistant grip on handles, knobs, and instrument shafts. *(2.3 marks)*
- **(iii) Parting Off:** A narrow, straight cutting tool is fed **radially inward** until the workpiece is completely severed. **Application:** Cutting finished components from a long bar stock. *(2.4 marks)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm, $N = 200$ rpm, $f = 0.4$ mm/rev, $L = 500$ mm. *(Depth of cut not given — we assume $d_c = 1$ mm, a standard finishing pass; if not specified, state the assumption clearly.)*

**Assumption:** Depth of cut $d_c = 1$ mm.

**(i) Cutting Speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 100 \times 200}{1000} = \frac{62831.85}{1000} \approx 62.83 \text{ m/min}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate:**

$$f_r = f \times N = 0.4 \times 200 = 80 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) MRR:**

$$MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f \cdot d_c = \pi \times 100 \times 500 \times 0.4 \times 1 = 62,831.85 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**(iv) Machining Time:**

$$t_m = \frac{L}{f_r} = \frac{500}{80} = 6.25 \text{ min} = 6 \text{ min } 15 \text{ sec}$$

**Final Answer Box:** $V \approx 62.83$ m/min, $f_r = 80$ mm/min, $MRR \approx 62,832$ mm³/min, $t_m = 6.25$ min.

*(7 marks — 1.75 each sub-part)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the "/1000" Factor:** The single most common mistake. $D$ is in mm, but $V$ must be in m/min. Writing $V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ without dividing by 1000 yields an answer that is **1000× too large** → **ZERO marks** for the step. KTU examiners are trained to scan for this exact slip.
> 
> 2. **Using Final Diameter Instead of Initial for Spindle Speed:** Always use the **initial (larger) diameter $D$** to calculate $N$, because as the diameter reduces during turning, the surface speed increases — but the spindle RPM remains constant. Using $d$ instead of $D$ undercuts the safe speed.
> 
> 3. **Forgetting to Round to Standard Spindle Speeds:** Lathes have a finite set of discrete speeds. After calculating $N$, you **must round to the nearest available standard RPM** and recalculate dependent parameters. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 
> 4. **Unit Inconsistencies in MRR:** Mixing mm and cm, or mm/min and m/min. Always state units explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Confusing Feed Rod with Lead Screw:** Feed rod = plain turning; Lead screw = thread cutting. Examiners deduct full marks for swapping these.
> 
> 6. **Missing Block Diagram Labels:** In 14-mark questions, the diagram must be **fully labelled**. An unlabelled diagram scores ≤ 2 out of 7.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorise These Bullets:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Centre Lathe = rotating workpiece + stationary single-point cutting tool.
- ✅ **Five Specifications:** Length of bed, Swing over bed, Swing over cross-slide, Spindle bore, Number of speeds.
- ✅ **Main Parts (10):** Bed, Headstock, Tailstock, Carriage, Cross-slide, Compound Rest, Tool Post, Apron, Feed Rod, Lead Screw.
- ✅ **Primary Power Flow:** Motor → Gearbox → Spindle → Chuck → Workpiece.
- ✅ **Feed vs. Lead Screw:** Feed rod = turning; Lead screw = thread cutting (uses half-nuts).
- ✅ **Three Chucks:** 3-jaw (auto-centre round), 4-jaw (independent, square/off-centre), Face plate (irregular).
- ✅ **Core Equations (Must memorise — derive at least once before exam):**
  - $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ (m/min)
  - $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ (rpm)
  - $f_r = f \times N$ (mm/min)
  - $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ (min)
  - $d_c = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$ (mm)
  - $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f \cdot d_c$ (mm³/min)
- ✅ **Operation-to-Motion Mapping:** Facing = radial in-feed; Turning = longitudinal feed; Taper = compound rest at angle; Threading = synchronized lead-screw feed; Parting = radial cut-off.
- ✅ **Common Units:** $D$ in mm, $L$ in mm, $f$ in mm/rev, $V$ in m/min, $N$ in rpm, $t_m$ in min, $MRR$ in mm³/min.
- ✅ **Always use the larger (initial) diameter $D$** for spindle-speed calculation.
- ✅ **Always round $N$ to the nearest available standard RPM** and use that rounded value for all subsequent calculations.
- ✅ **Thread Cutting Rule:** Tool advance per spindle revolution must equal the **pitch** of the thread being cut, which is automatically enforced by the lead-screw-and-half-nut mechanism.
- ✅ **Centre Height:** The tailstock dead centre must be **exactly aligned** with the spindle axis to avoid tapers in cylindrical turning.

> **End of Notes — Study of Centre Lathe | PCNSL308 | Module Experiment 2**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Performing Various Operations in Lathe: Turning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Performing Various Operations in Lathe: Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Lathe (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lathe** is a machine tool that removes unwanted material from a workpiece in the form of chips, by holding the workpiece rigidly and rotating it about a fixed axis against a single-point cutting tool, which is fed against the rotating workpiece. The operation performed on a lathe is called **Turning**, and the cutting tool is known as a **Single-Point Cutting Tool (SPCT)**.

The workpiece rotates (primary motion) while the tool advances linearly along the rotating surface (secondary motion / feed motion) to produce a cylindrical or conical surface of desired diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Use Verbatim):**
> *"Turning is a machining process in which a single-point cutting tool removes material from the surface of a rotating cylindrical workpiece. The workpiece is held in a chuck or between centers and rotated at a predetermined spindle speed, while the cutting tool, held in the tool post, is advanced along the axis and/or radially inward to produce a workpiece of desired dimensions and surface finish."*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sharpening a wooden pencil using a stationary blade while the pencil is held and rotated in your hand. Your hand rotates the pencil (this is the **spindle/primary motion**), and the blade is moved slowly along the length of the pencil (this is the **longitudinal feed**) and pressed gently sideways (this is the **depth of cut**). The wood shavings that come out are the **chips**, and the smooth outer surface of the pencil is your **machined surface**.

A lathe does exactly this — but with a rotating metal workpiece, a hardened cutting tool, and precise mechanical control over speeds, feeds, and depth of cut.

## 1.3 Various Operations Performed on a Lathe (Beyond Plain Turning)

| Sl. No | Operation | Purpose | Tool Movement |
|:------:|:----------|:--------|:--------------|
| 1 | **Plain (Straight) Turning** | Reduce diameter of cylindrical workpiece | Tool moves parallel to axis |
| 2 | **Step Turning** | Produce multiple diameters on same shaft | Tool moves radially + longitudinally |
| 3 | **Taper Turning** | Produce conical surface | Tool moves at an angle to axis |
| 4 | **Facing** | Produce flat surface perpendicular to axis | Tool moves radially inward |
| 5 | **Knurling** | Produce patterned rough surface (for grip) | Knurling tool pressed radially |
| 6 | **Parting Off** | Cut the workpiece into two parts | Narrow tool fed radially |
| 7 | **Thread Cutting** | Produce helical threads on surface | Tool fed with synchronized pitch |
| 8 | **Drilling / Boring** | Enlarge an existing hole | Tool fed along axis |
| 9 | **Reaming** | Finish an existing hole to size | Tool fed along axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Module Experiment 2 Focus:** The KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual specifically covers **Plain Turning, Step Turning, Taper Turning, Facing, and Grooving** as the primary operations for evaluation.

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics & Definitions

- **Cutting Speed (V):** The relative surface speed between the tool and the workpiece, measured in **m/min**. Standard cutting speed for mild steel is **25 m/min**, for aluminium is **100 m/min**.
- **Feed (f):** The distance the tool advances per revolution of the workpiece, measured in **mm/rev**. Typical range: **0.1 to 0.5 mm/rev**.
- **Depth of Cut (d):** The radial thickness of material removed in one pass, measured in **mm**. Typical range: **0.5 to 3 mm**.

## 1.5 Principal Parts of a Centre Lathe (Required for Viva)

1. **Bed** — Heavy base that supports all parts and provides alignment.
2. **Headstock** — Houses the spindle, chuck, and gear train for speed variation.
3. **Tailstock** — Supports the other end of long workpieces via a dead centre.
4. **Carriage** — Mounted on bed ways; carries the tool post and moves longitudinally/cross-wise.
5. **Cross-slide** — Mounted on carriage; moves tool perpendicular to spindle axis.
6. **Compound Slide** — Angled movement for taper turning; can be swiveled.
7. **Tool Post** — Rigidly clamps the single-point cutting tool.
8. **Apron** — Houses feed mechanism levers and engages power feed.
9. **Feed Rod / Lead Screw** — Transmits motion from headstock to carriage/threading dial.
10. **Legs / Cabinet** — Support bed and house the motor and coolant pump.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Turning Process

The turning process on a lathe follows a strict operational sequence. The "why" and "how" behind each step is critical for KTU 2024 lab viva.

### Step 1: Workpiece Mounting (Why first?)
- **Why:** The workpiece must be held concentric with the spindle axis to ensure true rotation without runout.
- **How:** A three-jaw self-centring chuck is used for round/hex workpieces; a four-jaw independent chuck is used for square/irregular shapes; centres between headstock and tailstock are used for long slender shafts.

### Step 2: Tool Setting (Why this step matters)
- **Why:** Tool height must match the centre of the workpiece. Even 0.5 mm offset causes poor finish, tool wear, and inaccurate dimensions.
- **How:** The tool tip is aligned with the tailstock centre using a steel rule (eyeball method) or a tool height gauge.

### Step 3: Selection of Cutting Parameters
- **Why:** Wrong speed/feed causes tool failure, poor surface finish, or excessive cycle time.
- **How:** Use the **machining data handbook** or standard tables based on work material, tool material (HSS / Carbide), and operation type.

### Step 4: Spindle Speed Calculation (Why pre-calculate?)
- **Why:** The chuck is rotated manually to confirm clearance, then power-on at calculated RPM.
- **How:** Calculate spindle speed **N** from cutting speed **V** and workpiece diameter **D** using the formula below.

### Step 5: Trial Cut (Why always a trial?)
- **Why:** First pass confirms tool geometry, clearance, and actual diameter being removed.
- **How:** Touch tool to workpiece (zero setting), then advance cross-slide by 0.5 mm and take a short longitudinal cut of 10–15 mm. Measure diameter with vernier caliper.

### Step 6: Final Cut (Why the last pass is unique?)
- **Why:** A finishing pass with reduced depth of cut (0.1–0.3 mm) yields better surface finish and dimensional accuracy.
- **How:** Reduce feed and depth of cut for the final 1–2 passes.

### Step 7: Retraction and Measurement (Why retract carefully?)
- **Why:** Retracting too fast or while rotating can damage the tool tip or workpiece.
- **How:** Stop spindle rotation, retract cross-slide, then retract longitudinal feed handle.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet for Numerical Problems)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are universally tested in KTU lab viva and ESE numerical problems. Memorize with units.

| Sl. No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Standard Value (Mild Steel) |
|:------:|:----------|:--------|:------|:----------------------------|
| 1 | **Cutting Speed (V)** | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | 25 m/min (HSS), 150 m/min (Carbide) |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed (N)** | $N = \dfrac{V \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rev/min (RPM) | 300–1500 RPM |
| 3 | **Feed Rate (f_n)** | $f_n = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | 50–150 mm/min |
| 4 | **Longitudinal Feed (f_l)** | $f_l = f \cdot N \cdot L$ (per pass) | mm/min | Function of length |
| 5 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | — |
| 6 | **Power Required (P)** | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_s}{60 \cdot 10^6}$ | kW | 0.5–5 kW |
| 7 | **Machining Time (T_m)** | $T_m = \dfrac{L + l_a + l_o}{f \cdot N}$ | min | — |
| 8 | **Cutting Force (F_c)** | $F_c = K_s \cdot d \cdot f$ | N | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Unit Convention (Lose no marks!):**
> - **D** (workpiece diameter) is in **mm**
> - **V** is in **m/min**
> - **N** is in **rev/min**
> - **f** is in **mm/rev**
> - **K_s** (specific cutting force) for mild steel is **2000 N/mm²** (always state value used)

## 2.3 Symbol Dictionary for KTU Valuation

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|:------:|:--------|:-------------|
| $V$ | Cutting speed | m/min |
| $D$ | Workpiece diameter | mm |
| $N$ | Spindle speed (RPM) | rev/min |
| $f$ | Feed per revolution | mm/rev |
| $d$ | Depth of cut | mm |
| $L$ | Length of cut | mm |
| $l_a$ | Approach distance | mm |
| $l_o$ | Over-travel distance | mm |
| $K_s$ | Specific cutting energy / force | N/mm² |
| $\pi$ | Pi constant | 3.14159 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The formulas above are not academic — they are used daily in CNC programming (G-code: S=spindle speed, F=feed), in **CAM software (Mastercam, Fusion 360)** for tool path generation, and in **production cost estimation** in industry. Cutting speed selection determines **tool life** (the Taylor tool life equation: $V \cdot T^n = C$), which directly affects manufacturing cost per component.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** If asked in viva *"Why cannot you use the same cutting speed for steel and aluminium?"* — answer: *"Because K_s (specific cutting force) and allowable heat differ. Aluminium has lower K_s (~750 N/mm²) and dissipates heat faster, so V is higher. Steel requires lower V to prevent tool tip softening."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed (V)

### Conceptual Basis
Cutting speed (V) is defined as the **peripheral linear speed** of the workpiece surface where the tool is engaged. If the workpiece has diameter **D** and rotates at **N** revolutions per minute, then the linear distance covered at the periphery per minute equals **π·D·N** (in mm/min, since D is in mm).

To express this distance in **meters per minute**, we divide by 1000.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1:** Distance covered in one revolution = Circumference = $\pi \cdot D$

**Step 2:** Distance covered in **N** revolutions per minute = $\pi \cdot D \cdot N$ (in mm/min)

**Step 3:** Convert mm/min to m/min by dividing by 1000:

$$V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for N by cross-multiplication:

$$\pi \cdot D \cdot N = 1000 \cdot V$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{[rev/min]}$$

### Worked Example (KTU 2024 Numerical Style)

> **Question:** A mild steel workpiece of **40 mm diameter** is to be turned on a lathe. The recommended cutting speed is **25 m/min** (HSS tool). Calculate the spindle speed in RPM.

**Step 1 — List given values:**
- $D = 40$ mm
- $V = 25$ m/min
- $\pi = 3.14159$

**Step 2 — Substitute in formula:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 25}{3.14159 \cdot 40}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator:**

$$N = \dfrac{25000}{125.6636}$$

**Step 4 — Final answer:**

$$N = 198.94 \approx 199 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5 — Select nearest available spindle speed on the lathe:** Usually the lathe provides 180, 200, 224, 250 RPM. Select **200 RPM**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern:** Always round off to the nearest available gear speed, then state the *selected* RPM. Recomputing actual V from selected N is a common follow-up sub-question worth 2–3 marks.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

### Conceptual Basis
MRR is the volume of material removed per unit time. For turning, consider a cylindrical shell of diameter D, depth d, advancing at feed f per revolution at N RPM.

In one revolution, the tool moves forward by **f** mm. The cross-sectional area of the chip is approximately **d × f** (rectangular approximation, valid for small feed angles). The volume removed per revolution is therefore: $V_{rev} = d \cdot f \cdot \pi \cdot D$.

Multiplying by N revolutions per minute:

$$MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}$$

### Worked Example

> **Question:** During turning of a 50 mm diameter MS shaft, depth of cut = 2 mm, feed = 0.2 mm/rev, spindle speed = 320 RPM. Find MRR.

**Step 1 — Substitute values:**

$$MRR = \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 320$$

**Step 2 — Multiply step by step:**

$$MRR = 3.14159 \cdot 50 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 320$$

$$MRR = 3.14159 \cdot 6400$$

$$MRR = 20106.19 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to cm³/min (common unit asked):**

$$MRR = 20.106 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Machining Time (T_m)

### Conceptual Basis
Machining time is the total time the tool is engaged in cutting. It depends on the length of the cut and the feed rate. We must add **approach distance (l_a)** and **over-travel (l_o)** to the actual workpiece length L, because the tool needs distance to engage and clear the workpiece.

$$T_m = \dfrac{L + l_a + l_o}{f \cdot N} \quad \text{[min]}$$

### Worked Example

> **Question:** A workpiece of 100 mm length is to be turned from Ø40 mm to Ø36 mm. Feed = 0.2 mm/rev, N = 200 RPM, approach = 3 mm, over-travel = 2 mm. Calculate machining time.

**Step 1 — Total length of tool travel:**

$$L_{total} = 100 + 3 + 2 = 105 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate in mm/min:**

$$f_n = f \cdot N = 0.2 \cdot 200 = 40 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — Machining time:**

$$T_m = \dfrac{105}{40} = 2.625 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to seconds for viva:**

$$T_m = 2.625 \times 60 = 157.5 \text{ seconds}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Required (P) at Spindle

The power required to remove metal is the product of the cutting force component in the direction of motion and the cutting velocity.

**Specific cutting energy** $K_s$ (units: N/mm²) when multiplied by **undeformed chip cross-section** $(d \times f)$ gives the tangential cutting force:

$$F_c = K_s \cdot d \cdot f \quad \text{[N]}$$

Power is force × velocity. Velocity in m/s is $\dfrac{V}{60}$, so:

$$P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{K_s \cdot d \cdot f \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[kW]}$$

### Worked Example

> **Question:** For mild steel, $K_s = 2000$ N/mm², d = 2 mm, f = 0.2 mm/rev, V = 25 m/min. Find power.

**Step 1 — Cutting force:**

$$F_c = 2000 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.2 = 800 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Power:**

$$P = \dfrac{800 \cdot 25}{60 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{20000}{60000} = 0.333 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to HP (1 HP = 0.746 kW):**

$$P = \dfrac{0.333}{0.746} = 0.447 \text{ HP}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Cutting Parameters

| Workpiece Material | Tool Material | V (m/min) | f (mm/rev) | d (mm) | Surface Finish |
|:-------------------|:--------------|:----------|:-----------|:-------|:---------------|
| Mild Steel | HSS | 25–35 | 0.1–0.3 | 0.5–2.0 | Good |
| Mild Steel | Carbide | 120–180 | 0.1–0.4 | 0.5–3.0 | Excellent |
| Aluminium | HSS | 80–120 | 0.1–0.4 | 0.5–3.0 | Excellent |
| Cast Iron | Carbide | 60–90 | 0.1–0.3 | 0.5–2.0 | Good |
| Brass | HSS | 50–80 | 0.05–0.2 | 0.3–1.0 | Excellent |
| Stainless Steel | Carbide | 80–120 | 0.1–0.2 | 0.5–1.5 | Good |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Centre Lathe

This diagram shows how power flows from the main motor to the cutting tool and the various functional subsystems of a lathe.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Three Phase AC Main Supply] --> B[Main Motor 1 to 3 HP]
    B --> C[Headstock Gear Train]
    C --> D[Spindle with Chuck]
    D --> E[Rotating Workpiece]
    F[Feed Rod Drive from Headstock] --> G[Apron Mechanism]
    G --> H[Carriage Assembly]
    H --> I[Cross Slide]
    I --> J[Compound Slide]
    J --> K[Tool Post with SPCT]
    K --> L[Cutting Action on Workpiece]
    E --> L
    M[Tailstock with Dead Centre] --> E
    N[Coolant Pump] --> O[Coolant Nozzle]
    O --> L
    P[Lead Screw] --> G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Power flows from **A → B → C → D → E** (rotates the workpiece).
- Feed/threading power flows from **F → G → H → I → J → K** (advances the tool).
- The cutting action occurs at node **L**, where rotating workpiece meets the tool tip.
- Node **P** (lead screw) engages only during thread cutting; disengaged during plain turning.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Step-by-Step Procedure for Plain Turning (KTU Lab Record Format)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Step 1: Study the Drawing and Identify Dimensions] --> S2[Step 2: Select Workpiece Material and Measure Initial Diameter]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Mount Workpiece in Three Jaw Chuck and Tighten]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Mount Tool in Tool Post and Set Tool Height to Centre]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Calculate Spindle Speed N from V and D]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Engage Required Gear and Switch on Spindle]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Touch Tool to Workpiece Surface and Set Zero on Cross Slide]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Move Cross Slide to Required Depth of Cut]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Engage Power Feed and Take Longitudinal Cut]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Retract Tool at End of Cut and Stop Spindle]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Measure Final Diameter with Vernier Caliper]
    S11 --> S12{Within Tolerance}
    S12 -->|No| S13[Step 13: Take Additional Light Cut of 0.1 to 0.2 mm]
    S13 --> S11
    S12 -->|Yes| S14[Step 14: Stop Machine and Record Observations in Lab Record]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The flow shows an iterative quality loop (Steps 11 → 12 → 13 → 11) — this is critical in KTU lab evaluation; the examiner looks for a **trial-measure-correct** loop, not a single blind cut.

## 4.3 Schematic Block Diagram — Force System on a Single-Point Cutting Tool

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Workpiece Rotating at N RPM] --> B[Cutting Tool Tip Engaged]
    B --> C[Three Orthogonal Force Components]
    C --> D[Fc: Main Cutting Force Tangential to Work]
    C --> D2[Ff: Feed Force along Feed Direction]
    C --> D3[Fp: Radial Force Pushing Tool Away from Work]
    D --> E[Power Consumption Component]
    D2 --> F[Feed Drive Load]
    D3 --> G[Tool Deflection and Chatter]
```

**Reading the diagram:** $F_c$ is the largest and most important force (used for power calculation); $F_f$ and $F_p$ are secondary. In KTU viva, students are often asked: *"Which force determines power?"* — answer: **Fc, the tangential cutting force**.

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Tool Nomenclature and Geometry (SPCT)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Single Point Cutting Tool] --> B[Shank]
    A --> C[Cutting Part]
    C --> D[Rake Face]
    C --> E[Flank Face]
    C --> F[Nose Radius]
    B --> G[Tool Angles to be Ground]
    G --> G1[Back Rake Angle Gamma]
    G --> G2[Side Rake Angle Alpha]
    G --> G3[End Relief Angle Epsilon]
    G --> G4[Side Relief Angle Beta]
    G --> G5[End Cutting Edge Angle]
    G --> G6[Side Cutting Edge Angle]
    G --> G7[Nose Radius r]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **rake face** is where the chip slides; the **flank face** provides clearance. Tool angles G1 through G6 must be ground correctly for proper chip flow and tool life. KTU viva often asks: *"What happens if the side relief angle is zero?"* — answer: *"The flank rubs against the machined surface, causing chatter, poor finish, and rapid tool wear."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

### Question A1: Define cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut in turning. State the SI and practical units used.

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate 1 mark per term):**
- **Cutting Speed (V):** The relative surface speed between the tool tip and the workpiece surface at the point of cut, measured in **m/min** (practical) or **m/s** (SI).
- **Feed (f):** The distance the tool advances per revolution of the workpiece along the feed direction, measured in **mm/rev**.
- **Depth of Cut (d):** The radial thickness of material removed in a single pass, measured in **mm**.

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

### Question A2: List any six operations that can be performed on a centre lathe and state the purpose of each in one line.

**Model Answer (3 marks — list any 6, half-mark per correct operation):**
1. **Plain Turning** — to reduce diameter
2. **Facing** — to produce a flat end surface
3. **Taper Turning** — to produce a conical surface
4. **Thread Cutting** — to produce helical threads
5. **Knurling** — to produce a patterned gripping surface
6. **Parting Off** — to cut the workpiece into two parts
7. **Drilling** — to produce a hole
8. **Boring** — to enlarge an existing hole

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a centre lathe. Explain the functions of headstock, tailstock, and carriage.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Construction (4 marks):**
A centre lathe consists of the following main parts:

1. **Bed** — A heavy, accurately machined casting with precision V-ways and flat ways on which the carriage and tailstock slide. It provides rigidity and alignment.

2. **Headstock** — Mounted on the left end of the bed. It contains the **main spindle**, **chuck**, and **gear train** for obtaining various spindle speeds. The spindle is hollow to accommodate long bar stock.

3. **Tailstock** — Mounted on the right end of the bed, it can be slid along the bed and clamped at any desired position. It houses a **dead centre** that supports the free end of long workpieces, preventing deflection.

4. **Carriage** — Mounted on the bed ways between headstock and tailstock. It consists of the **saddle**, **cross-slide**, **compound slide**, and **tool post**. It carries the cutting tool and provides longitudinal, cross, and angular feed motions.

5. **Apron** — Attached to the front of the carriage. It houses the **feed mechanism**, **half-nuts** (for thread cutting), and the **clutch lever** for engaging power feed.

6. **Feed Rod and Lead Screw** — The feed rod transmits motion for plain turning feed; the lead screw is used for thread cutting.

7. **Legs/Cabinet** — Support the bed and house the **main motor** and **coolant system**.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
The workpiece is held in the chuck and rotated at a predetermined RPM by the spindle motor. The single-point cutting tool is rigidly clamped in the tool post. As the workpiece rotates, the tool is advanced either manually (hand feed) or by power feed (auto feed) along the axis to remove material in the form of chips. The depth of material removed in one pass is controlled by the cross-slide. The relative motion between the rotating workpiece and the linearly moving tool produces a cylindrical surface of reduced diameter.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A mild steel shaft of 60 mm diameter is to be turned to 54 mm diameter in a single pass. The cutting speed is 25 m/min, feed is 0.25 mm/rev, and approach + over-travel = 5 mm. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate in mm/min, (iii) MRR, and (iv) machining time for a 150 mm length.**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.75 each):**

**Given:**
- $D = 60$ mm, $d_{final} = 54$ mm
- Depth of cut $d = (60 - 54)/2 = 3$ mm
- $V = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev
- $L = 150$ mm, $l_a + l_o = 5$ mm

**(i) Spindle Speed (1.75 marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 25}{3.14159 \cdot 60} = \dfrac{25000}{188.495} = 132.63 \text{ RPM} \approx 133 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate in mm/min (1.75 marks):**

$$f_n = f \cdot N = 0.25 \cdot 132.63 = 33.16 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) MRR (1.75 marks):**

$$MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N = 3.14159 \cdot 60 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 132.63$$

$$MRR = 3.14159 \cdot 60 \cdot 3 \cdot 33.16 = 18749.7 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 18.75 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Machining Time (1.75 marks):**

$$T_m = \dfrac{L + l_a + l_o}{f \cdot N} = \dfrac{150 + 5}{33.16} = \dfrac{155}{33.16} = 4.674 \text{ min}$$

$$\text{In seconds: } 4.674 \times 60 = 280.5 \text{ seconds} \approx 4 \text{ min } 40 \text{ s}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
