**Explain with a neat diagram the geometry of a single-point cutting tool. Define the following angles: (i) Back Rake Angle, (ii) Side Rake Angle, (iii) End Relief Angle, (iv) Side Relief Angle. State one function of each.**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.75 each):**

**Diagram (2 marks — describe verbally as drawing):** Show the tool with rake face on top, flank face on side, nose radius at tip. Reference system: orthogonal reference system with three planes — **reference plane (horizontal)**, **longitudinal plane (vertical, parallel to feed)**, and **transverse plane (vertical, perpendicular to feed)**.

**(i) Back Rake Angle (γ):** The angle between the **rake face** of the tool and a line parallel to the base of the tool, measured in the **longitudinal plane**. **Function:** Controls chip flow direction; positive rake reduces cutting force and power consumption.

**(ii) Side Rake Angle (α):** The angle between the **rake face** and the reference plane, measured in the **transverse plane**. **Function:** Determines chip thickness and curl; higher side rake improves surface finish on softer materials.

**(iii) End Relief Angle (ε):** The angle between the **end flank face** and a plane perpendicular to the base, measured in the **longitudinal plane**. **Function:** Provides clearance between the tool flank and the freshly machined surface, preventing rubbing and chatter.

**(iv) Side Relief Angle (β):** The angle between the **side flank face** and a plane perpendicular to the base, measured in the **transverse plane**. **Function:** Prevents the side flank from rubbing against the cylindrical workpiece, reducing friction and heat generation.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**During a turning operation on a 50 mm diameter mild steel workpiece with HSS tool, the following data are recorded: cutting speed = 30 m/min, feed = 0.2 mm/rev, depth of cut = 2 mm, length of cut = 200 mm, approach = 3 mm, over-travel = 2 mm, specific cutting force K_s = 2000 N/mm². Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, (iii) MRR, (iv) cutting force, and (v) power required at spindle.**

**Model Answer (7 marks — 1.4 each):**

**Given:** D = 50 mm, V = 30 m/min, f = 0.2 mm/rev, d = 2 mm, L = 200 mm, $l_a$ = 3 mm, $l_o$ = 2 mm, $K_s$ = 2000 N/mm²

**(i) Spindle Speed (1.4 marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 50} = \dfrac{30000}{157.08} = 190.99 \approx 191 \text{ RPM}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate (1.4 marks):**

$$f_n = 0.2 \cdot 191 = 38.2 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) MRR (1.4 marks):**

$$MRR = \pi \cdot 50 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 191 = 3.14159 \cdot 3820 = 12000.7 \text{ mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 12 \text{ cm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iv) Cutting Force (1.4 marks):**

$$F_c = K_s \cdot d \cdot f = 2000 \cdot 2 \cdot 0.2 = 800 \text{ N}$$

**(v) Power (1.4 marks):**

$$P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \cdot 1000} = \dfrac{800 \cdot 30}{60000} = \dfrac{24000}{60000} = 0.4 \text{ kW}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Turning Calculations (Top 7 Errors)**
>
> 1. **Unit Mix-up in V formula (–2 marks):** Students use $V = \pi D N$ without dividing by 1000. Always remember: D is in **mm**, N in **rpm**, V in **m/min** → divide by 1000.
>
> 2. **Wrong Depth of Cut (–2 marks):** Depth of cut is **radial**, not diametrical. If reducing Ø50 to Ø46, then $d = (50-46)/2 = 2$ mm, NOT 4 mm.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Approach and Over-travel in Machining Time (–1 mark):** The formula is $T_m = (L + l_a + l_o)/(f \cdot N)$, NOT just $L/(f \cdot N)$.
>
> 4. **Not rounding to available gear speed (–0.5 mark):** If calculated N = 198 RPM, the lathe has 180 or 200 RPM — select 200 and **recalculate V** using actual selected N.
>
> 5. **Confusing MRR units (–1 mark):** Be ready to give answer in **mm³/min** OR **cm³/min** as asked. $1$ cm³ = $1000$ mm³.
>
> 6. **Not stating K_s value used (–1 mark):** Examiner cannot give full credit if you write 2000 N/mm² without mentioning "for mild steel". Always state the material and the corresponding K_s.
>
> 7. **Drawing mistakes in tool geometry (–2 marks):** Side rake and back rake are on different reference planes. Mixing them up loses drawing marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Module 2 Revision — Turning on Lathe**

- **Lathe = Primary motion (workpiece rotation) + Secondary motion (tool feed).** This is the single most important viva definition.

- **The four cardinal cutting parameters:** $V$ (m/min), $N$ (RPM), $f$ (mm/rev), $d$ (mm). Master their inter-relations.

- **Golden formula trio:**
  - $V = \pi D N / 1000$ → unit trap: divide by 1000.
  - $MRR = \pi D d f N$ → no division by 1000 here.
  - $T_m = (L + l_a + l_o) / (f N)$ → always add approach and over-travel.

- **Tool setting rule:** Tool tip MUST be at the centre of workpiece. Use a steel rule aligned to the tailstock centre.

- **For mild steel (HSS tool):** Use $V = 25$ m/min as the default value unless specified.

- **For aluminium (HSS tool):** Use $V = 100$ m/min; this is **4× the steel value** — a common viva question.

- **Trial cut is mandatory** in the lab — never take the full depth of cut in the first pass. KTU rubric penalizes skipping this step.

- **Vernier caliper accuracy:** 0.02 mm. Micrometer accuracy: 0.01 mm. Use micrometer for final pass, vernier for trial measurement.

- **Chip color tells the temperature:**
  - **Blue chip** = too high cutting speed → reduce V.
  - **Slight yellow/straw chip** = ideal.
  - **Continuous chip without color** = too low V (also undesirable).

- **Depth of cut is RADIAL:** Reducing Ø40 to Ø36 means d = 2 mm per side.

- **Specific cutting force K_s values to remember:**
  - Mild steel: 2000 N/mm²
  - Aluminium: 750 N/mm²
  - Cast iron: 1500 N/mm²
  - Brass: 1500 N/mm²

- **Safety in lab:** Always wear **safety goggles, apron, and closed-toe shoes**. Never leave chuck key in the chuck. Never measure a rotating workpiece. Stop spindle before any adjustment.

- **KTU Examiner's pet question:** *"What is the difference between feed rod and lead screw?"* — **Feed rod** is used for plain turning feed (rotates continuously); **Lead screw** is used for thread cutting (rotates with the spindle, drives the half-nuts engaged only when threading).

- **Practical lab tip:** The feed rod and lead screw are NEVER engaged simultaneously — doing so damages the apron mechanism.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lathe: Grooving, Taper Turning, and Step Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Lathe: Grooving, Taper Turning, and Step Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lathe — The Mother of All Machine Tools (Quick Recap)

A **lathe** is a fundamental machine tool used to produce cylindrical (and sometimes non-cylindrical) components by removing excess material using a stationary cutting tool while the workpiece rotates about a fixed axis. In the KTU 2024 Scheme lab syllabus for *PCNSL308*, the lathe is introduced as the workhorse for **diameter-reduction, profiling, and parting operations** — and this experiment focuses on three of the most widely-evaluated operations in University lab exams:

1. **Grooving (Recessing)**
2. **Taper Turning**
3. **Step Cutting (Shoulder Facing)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> *"Lathe: Plain turning, step cutting, grooving, taper turning, knurling, threading."*
> The operations in **bold** are covered in this experiment. Knurling and threading are covered in subsequent experiments.

---

## 1.2 Grooving (Recessing) — Definition

**Grooving** is a lathe operation in which a narrow cutting tool is fed *radially inward* (perpendicular to the workpiece axis) to machine a recess/groove of specified **width** and **depth** on the external surface of a cylindrical workpiece. The tool does not traverse along the axis (no longitudinal feed) — only a transverse feed is used.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Grooving is the process of cutting a narrow groove on a cylindrical or conical surface using a single-point grooving tool, where the depth of cut is achieved by radial infeed and groove width equals the width of the cutting tool.

### 1.2.1 Intuitive Analogy 🍞
Imagine using a **butter knife to cut a deep narrow channel into a soft cylindrical bread roll** by pressing the knife straight down (no sideways movement). The width of the channel = width of the knife blade; the depth = how hard you press. That's exactly what a grooving tool does on a spinning workpiece.

**Common Applications:**
- Relief grooves for thread run-out (ANSI/ISO standard)
- Oil-retention grooves in bearings and bushings
- Decorative/functional grooves on shafts
- Undercutting for grinding clearance

---

## 1.3 Step Cutting (Shoulder Facing) — Definition

**Step cutting** is a lathe operation in which material is removed to produce a **sudden change in diameter (a "step" or "shoulder")** on a workpiece. It is essentially a *combination of facing + turning* — the tool first feeds radially inward to the smaller diameter, then longitudinally along the axis to produce a flat shoulder perpendicular (or at an angle) to the axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Step cutting is a turning operation used to produce a sudden change in diameter on a workpiece by combining radial and longitudinal feed movements of a single-point cutting tool, resulting in a stepped cylindrical profile.

### 1.3.1 Intuitive Analogy 🪜
Picture carving a **wooden staircase baluster** on a lathe — the wood spins, and the chisel first plunges in to reduce the diameter, then slides sideways to form a flat landing (shoulder). Repeat for each step. The number of "stairs" defines the number of operations.

**Common Applications:**
- Shafts with multiple diameters (motor shafts, gear blanks)
- Shouldered bolts and pins
- Spacers and collars
- Pre-machining for thread relief

---

## 1.4 Taper Turning — Definition

**Taper turning** is a lathe operation in which the diameter of a workpiece *gradually and uniformly changes* along its length, producing a conical surface. The **Taper (T)** is defined as the difference in diameter over a given length of the workpiece, usually expressed as:

$$T = \frac{D - d}{L}$$

where $D$ = large diameter (mm), $d$ = small diameter (mm), $L$ = length of taper (mm).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> Taper turning is a turning operation in which a workpiece is machined into a conical shape by traversing the cutting tool at an angle to the axis of rotation of the workpiece, or by offsetting the tailstock, or by using a special taper-turning attachment.

### 1.4.1 Intuitive Analogy 🕯️
Look at a **candle's flame-shaped top** or a **sharpened pencil tip** — both are tapers. Machining such a shape on a lathe is taper turning. The angle of the taper can be controlled by *swiveling* the cutting tool (compound rest), *shifting* the tailstock sideways (offset method), or using a dedicated **hydraulic/copying attachment**.

**Common Applications:**
- Tapered pins, mandrels, and tool shanks (e.g., MT — Morse Taper)
- Lathe tailstock and spindle nose tapers
- Pipe and shaft couplings
- Sheet-metal rolling roll ends

---

## 1.5 Visual Summary of Operations

| Operation | Tool Feed Direction | Resulting Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Turning** | Longitudinal (parallel to axis) | Uniform diameter |
| **Step Cutting** | Radial → Longitudinal | Sudden diameter change (shoulder) |
| **Grooving** | Radial only (no traverse) | Narrow circumferential recess |
| **Taper Turning** | At an angle to axis | Gradually changing diameter (cone) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tools, Workholding & Setup (Common to All Three Operations)

| Item | Specification for This Experiment |
|---|---|
| **Lathe** | Centre lathe (e.g., HMT NH22 / NH26) |
| **Workpiece** | Mild steel (EN8) or aluminium rod, Ø 30–50 mm, length ~150–200 mm |
| **Chucks/Workholding** | 3-jaw or 4-jaw self-centring chuck + live centre at tailstock |
| **Cutting tool for turning/step** | HSS right-hand tool bit (e.g., 16×16×100 mm) ground to standard geometry |
| **Grooving tool** | HSS parting/grooving blade, width 3 mm (or 4 mm) |
| **Compound rest** | Used for taper-angle setting (swivel base) |
| **Tailstock** | Used with offset for the offset-taper method |
| **Measuring instruments** | Vernier caliper (LC 0.02 mm), micrometer (LC 0.01 mm), taper gauge/sine bar |

---

## 2.2 Grooving — Theory

### 2.2.1 Tool Geometry
A **grooving tool** is essentially a **narrow parting-off tool** with:
- **Width (W)** = final groove width (typically 3, 4, or 5 mm)
- **Rake angle (α)** = 0° to 10° (positive rake for free-cutting steels)
- **Clearance angle (γ)** = 6° to 10° (side clearance vital to prevent rubbing)
- **Nose radius** = 0 (sharp) to 0.4 mm (small radius for better surface finish)

### 2.2.2 Cutting Parameters
| Parameter | Typical Value (Mild Steel) | Typical Value (Aluminium) |
|---|---|---|
| **Cutting speed (V)** | 25–35 m/min | 60–90 m/min |
| **Spindle speed (N)** | computed from $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$ | higher |
| **Feed (f)** | 0.05–0.15 mm/rev (manual) | 0.08–0.20 mm/rev |
| **Depth of groove** | as per drawing (e.g., 2–5 mm) | as per drawing |

### 2.2.3 Procedure
1. Mount and centre the workpiece; set spindle speed.
2. Set tool post approximately **perpendicular to the axis** (90° to lathe bed).
3. Touch tool to workpiece; zero the cross-slide dial.
4. Feed cross-slide in to the required **groove depth**.
5. Withdraw, traverse longitudinally to groove position, plunge in again — but for grooving we do **not** traverse longitudinally; the groove is a single radial cut.
6. Withdraw tool, stop spindle, measure groove width and depth.
7. Repeat for multiple grooves, indexing on the cross-slide or carriage dial.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro-Tip from KTU Valuation Key:** Always state the **width of the grooving tool = width of the machined groove** in the record. Examiners specifically test this conceptual link.

### 2.2.4 Important Safety Points
- **Never** use excessive feed — the narrow tool can shatter.
- Use **cutting fluid** (soda water or soluble oil) generously.
- For deep grooves, **peck-cutting** (multiple radial passes with retraction) is mandatory.

---

## 2.3 Step Cutting — Theory

### 2.3.1 Tool Path Geometry
A step is defined by **three dimensions**:
- **D₁** = larger diameter
- **D₂** = smaller diameter
- **L** = length of the smaller-diameter portion (shoulder width)

The depth of cut for the radial feed = $(D_1 - D_2)/2$.

### 2.3.2 Procedure
1. Face the end of the workpiece to clean reference face.
2. Turn the larger diameter (D₁) to size along the required length.
3. Mark the shoulder position using a **scriber/height gauge** or by indexing off the carriage dial.
4. **Step 1 — Radial plunge:** Feed the cross-slide inward by $(D_1 - D_2)/2$.
5. **Step 2 — Longitudinal feed:** Withdraw tool slightly, then traverse along the axis by length L using the carriage handwheel.
6. Repeat **roughing passes** (deeper cuts) and a final **finishing pass** (~0.2–0.3 mm depth).
7. Measure each step with vernier/micrometer.
8. Deburr the sharp corner (a slight chamfer is acceptable).

### 2.3.3 Common Defects and Remedies
| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Step is not square (90°) | Compound rest not set to 90° | Re-set compound rest to 0° (perpendicular) |
| Shoulder has a burr | No chamfering done | Use a separate chamfer tool or deburr by hand |
| Diameter oversize | Over-feeding on roughing | Take lighter finishing pass |
| Tool dig-in at shoulder | Excessive feed at corner | Reduce feed when reaching the shoulder |

---

## 2.4 Taper Turning — Theory (⭐ KTU High-Weightage ⭐)

There are **four methods** to produce a taper on a centre lathe. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on:

1. **Compound Rest Method (Swivel Slide Method)**
2. **Tailstock Set-Over Method (Offset Method)**
3. **(Briefly) Form Tool Method**
4. **(Briefly) Taper Turning Attachment Method**

### 2.4.1 Method 1 — Compound Rest Method

The compound rest is **swivelled** by an angle $\alpha$ (the half-taper angle) so that when the compound-slide handwheel is turned, the tool moves at that angle to the lathe axis.

**Derivation of the Angle:**
Consider a taper with large diameter $D$, small diameter $d$, and length $L$. Imagine "unrolling" the conical surface into a right triangle. The base is $L$ (axial), and the perpendicular rise is $(D - d)/2$ (radial).

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{(D - d)/2}{L} = \frac{D - d}{2L}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)\;}$$

**Accuracy:** **Very high** — limited only by operator skill and tool wear.
**Taper range:** Up to about **±15° half-angle (30° included angle)**.
**Limitation:** Only **one length of taper** can be cut per setup; for multiple tapers, the tool must be reset.

### 2.4.2 Method 2 — Tailstock Offset Method

The tailstock is **shifted laterally** (perpendicular to the lathe axis) by a calculated offset. The workpiece is supported between chuck and live centre; as it rotates, the offset centre causes the cutting tool to generate a conical surface.

**Derivation of the Offset:**
Let:
- $L$ = length of taper on workpiece
- $l$ = **total length of the workpiece** (between chuck and tailstock centre)
- $D$ = large diameter, $d$ = small diameter

By similar triangles:

$$\frac{\text{Offset}}{l} = \frac{(D - d)/2}{L}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Offset} = \frac{(D - d)\,l}{2L}\;}$$

**Accuracy:** **Moderate** — best for long, gentle tapers.
**Taper range:** Limited to small tapers (typically up to **8–10° half-angle**).
**Limitation:** A slight error in $l$ measurement causes taper error. The tailstock base must be moved parallel to the cross-slide.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently use $L$ (length of taper) instead of $l$ (total length of workpiece) in the offset formula. The correct variable is $l$ = total length, because the offset is set at the tailstock, which is the pivot for the *entire* workpiece.

### 2.4.3 Method 3 — Form Tool Method (Brief)

A **flat-form tool** ground to the exact taper profile is plunged radially into the workpiece. Used for **short, steep tapers**. Limited to small-diameter workpieces due to chatter on large diameters.

### 2.4.4 Method 4 — Taper Turning Attachment (Brief)

A **sine bar / hydraulic copying attachment** guides the cross-slide along a linear path inclined to the lathe axis. Used for **long, accurate tapers** in production. Not commonly available in academic labs.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ | Taper per unit length (Taper ratio) | mm/mm (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)$ | Compound rest swivel angle (half-taper angle) | degrees (°) |
| 3 | $\text{Offset} = \dfrac{(D - d)\, l}{2L}$ | Tailstock lateral offset | mm |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$ | Spindle speed from cutting speed | rev/min (rpm) |
| 5 | $f_r = \dfrac{f \, N}{1000}$ | Table feed / radial feed rate | mm/min |
| 6 | $V_c = \pi \, D \, N / 1000$ | Cutting speed verification | m/min |
| 7 | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{D + d}{2}$ | Average diameter for parameter selection | mm |
| 8 | $\tan(\theta) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | Half-taper angle (compact form) | radians → converted to ° |
| 9 | $TPI = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Taper per inch (English system variant) | in⁻¹ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Vigilance (Board-Exam Favourite Pitfall):**
> - $D$, $d$, $L$, $l$, and Offset must all be in the **same units** (preferably mm).
> - $N$ (rpm) is **per minute**, $V$ (cutting speed) is **m/min** → factor of 1000 must be applied.
> - $\alpha$ from the $\tan^{-1}$ expression is in **radians** by default — **multiply by $\dfrac{180}{\pi}$ to convert to degrees**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Compound Rest Angle ($\alpha$)

### Statement
A workpiece of length $L$ is to be turned from diameter $D$ to diameter $d$ over that length. Show that the compound rest must be set to an angle $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Visualise the geometry.**
Imagine the lathe axis is horizontal. The tool path is a straight line making angle $\alpha$ with the lathe axis. The starting point of the cut is on the larger-diameter end (diameter $D$); the ending point is on the smaller-diameter end (diameter $d$).

**Step 2 — Decompose the tool travel into two components.**
For a tool travelling a slant distance $S$ along the inclined line:
- Axial component (along the lathe axis) = $S \cos(\alpha)$
- Radial component (perpendicular to lathe axis) = $S \sin(\alpha)$

**Step 3 — Equate the axial component to the taper length.**
$$S \cos(\alpha) = L \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = \dfrac{L}{\cos(\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the radial component to the half-diameter difference.**
$$S \sin(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Step 3 into Step 4.**
$$\dfrac{L}{\cos(\alpha)} \cdot \sin(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$$

$$L \tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\alpha$.**

$$\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$$

$$\boxed{\;\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)\;}$$

This is the **half-taper angle** measured from the lathe axis. It is the angle to which the compound rest base must be swivelled.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Tailstock Offset

### Statement
A workpiece of total length $l$ (between chuck face and tailstock centre) is to be turned from diameter $D$ to diameter $d$ over a length $L$ of the workpiece. Derive the tailstock offset.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Conceptual model.**
When the tailstock is offset by a small distance $e$ (perpendicular to the lathe axis), the workpiece centre-line is no longer parallel to the tool path. The workpiece effectively rotates about a tilted axis. A point at the chuck end stays at one radial position; a point at the tailstock end is at a different radial position — generating a taper.

**Step 2 — Set up the similar triangles.**
Looking at the top view of the workpiece, the tailstock centre is displaced by $e$ from the headstock centre line. Over a length $l$, this gives a radial "rise" of $e$ at the tailstock end.

**Step 3 — The radial drop required over the length $L$ of taper is $(D - d)/2$.**

**Step 4 — Use similar triangles (small-angle assumption, $\tan(\alpha) \approx \alpha$):**

$$\dfrac{e}{l} = \dfrac{(D - d)/2}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $e$:**

$$\boxed{\;e = \text{Offset} = \dfrac{(D - d)\, l}{2L}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The small-angle approximation used here introduces a *negligible* error (typically < 0.5%) for tapers under 10° — well within manual-lathe tolerance.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

### Problem
A mild-steel workpiece of **total length 200 mm** and diameter **50 mm** is to be taper-turned over a length of **120 mm** to a final diameter of **30 mm**.

**(a)** Find the **compound rest angle**.
**(b)** Find the **tailstock offset**.
**(c)** Find the **taper ratio** and express the taper in **mm per 100 mm**.
**(d)** Choose a suitable **cutting speed** of 30 m/min and calculate the **spindle speed** for the initial diameter.

### Given Data
- $D = 50$ mm
- $d = 30$ mm
- $L = 120$ mm (length of taper)
- $l = 200$ mm (total length of workpiece)
- $V = 30$ m/min (cutting speed)

### Solution

#### (a) Compound Rest Angle
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{50 - 30}{2 \times 120}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{20}{240}\right)$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!(0.0833) = 4.76^\circ$$

**Answer:** $\alpha \approx 4.76^\circ$ (≈ 4° 46')

#### (b) Tailstock Offset
$$\text{Offset} = \frac{(D - d)\, l}{2L} = \frac{(50 - 30) \times 200}{2 \times 120} = \frac{20 \times 200}{240} = \frac{4000}{240}$$

$$\text{Offset} = 16.67 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** Offset ≈ 16.67 mm (≈ 16.7 mm)

#### (c) Taper Ratio
$$T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{50 - 30}{120} = \frac{20}{120} = 0.1667 \text{ mm/mm}$$

Per 100 mm of length: $0.1667 \times 100 = 16.67$ mm / 100 mm

**Answer:** Taper = **0.1667 mm/mm** = **16.67 mm per 100 mm length**

#### (d) Spindle Speed
For the **initial (largest) diameter** $D = 50$ mm:

$$N = \frac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{30000}{157.08} = 190.99 \text{ rpm}$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 191$ rpm. Use the nearest available speed on the lathe (typically 180 or 200 rpm).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Taper-Turning Methods

| Criterion | Compound Rest | Tailstock Offset | Form Tool | Attachment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | ★★★★★ High | ★★★☆☆ Moderate | ★★★★☆ High | ★★★★★ High |
| **Taper range** | up to 30° included | up to ~10° half-angle | Short steep tapers | Any practical |
| **Suitable for long tapers** | No (limited traverse) | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Suitable for internal tapers** | No | No | Yes | No |
| **Setup time** | Low | Moderate | Low | High |
| **KTU exam importance** | **Very high** | **Very high** | Low (theory only) | Low (theory only) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Taper Turning (Compound Rest Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Study Drawing and Read D, d, L] --> B[Mount and Centre Workpiece]
    B --> C[Set Spindle Speed from V and Dmax]
    C --> D[Compute Half-Taper Angle alpha = tan inverse of D minus d over 2L]
    D --> E[Swivel Compound Rest Base by alpha Degrees]
    E --> F[Set Tool Bit Perpendicular to Compound Slide]
    F --> G[Touch Tool to Larger Diameter End and Zero Dials]
    G --> H[Engage Longitudinal Feed on Carriage to Position Tool at Taper Start]
    H --> I[Turn Compound Slide Handwheel to Feed Tool at Angle alpha]
    I --> J{Check Taper with Taper Gauge or Sine Bar}
    J -->|Within Tolerance| K[Take Finishing Pass and Deburr]
    J -->|Out of Tolerance| L[Re-set Compound Angle or Tool Height]
    L --> J
    K --> M[Stop Lathe and Measure with Micrometer]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Grooving Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Groove Width and Depth from Drawing] --> B[Select Grooving Tool of Required Width]
    B --> C[Mount Workpiece and Set Spindle Speed]
    C --> D[Touch Tool to Workpiece Surface and Zero Cross Slide Dial]
    D --> E[Calculate Groove Position from Carriage Dial]
    E --> F[Traverse Carriage to Groove Position]
    F --> G[Plunge Tool Radially to Required Depth in Multiple Passes]
    G --> H[Withdraw Tool, Stop Spindle]
    H --> I[Measure Groove Width and Depth with Vernier]
    I --> J{Dimensions within Tolerance}
    J -->|Yes| K[Deburr and Proceed to Next Groove]
    J -->|No| L[Adjust and Recut]
    L --> J
    K --> M[End]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — Step Cutting Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read D1, D2, and Step Length L from Drawing] --> B[Mount and Centre Workpiece]
    B --> C[Turn Larger Diameter D1 to Size]
    C --> D[Mark Shoulder Position on Carriage Dial]
    D --> E[Set Compound Rest to 0 Degree for Square Shoulder]
    E --> F[Plunge Cross Slide by D1 minus D2 over 2]
    F --> G[Feed Carriage Longitudinally by Step Length L]
    G --> H[Take Roughing Passes First, then Finishing]
    H --> I[Measure Smaller Diameter D2 with Micrometer]
    I --> J{D2 within Tolerance}
    J -->|Yes| K[Chamfer the Corner and Deburr]
    J -->|No| L[Take Light Finishing Cut]
    L --> J
    K --> M[End]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparison of Four Taper Turning Methods

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Compound Rest Method]
        A1[Swivel Tool Slide] --> A2[High Accuracy] --> A3[Short Tapers]
    end
    subgraph B[Tailstock Offset Method]
        B1[Shift Tailstock Laterally] --> B2[Moderate Accuracy] --> B3[Long Gentle Tapers]
    end
    subgraph C[Form Tool Method]
        C1[Plunge Ground Tool] --> C2[Single Pass] --> C3[Short Steep Tapers]
    end
    subgraph D[Attachment Method]
        D1[Sine Bar Guide] --> D2[Highest Accuracy] --> D3[Production Tapering]
    end
    A -.->|Alternative Approaches| B
    B -.->|Alternative Approaches| C
    C -.->|Alternative Approaches| D
```

## 4.5 Tool-Engagement Schematic (Text-Based)

```
         TURNING              GROOVING              STEP CUTTING
     (Side Feed)            (Radial Plunge)         (Plunge + Slide)
         |                       |                       |
         v                       v                       v
   ~~~~~~~~~~               ~~~~~~~~~~               ~~~~~~~~~~
  /          \             /          \             /          \
 |  ==========>           |  ||||     |           |  =====>     |
  \          /             \          /             \    |     /
   ~~~~~~~~~~               ~~~~~~~~~~               ~~~~~~~~~~  ---> Feed
                          groove width =             step (shoulder)
                          tool width
```
