<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define taper turning. List any two methods used for producing a taper on a centre lathe.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** Taper turning is a lathe operation in which the diameter of the workpiece changes gradually along its length, producing a conical surface.
- **(1 mark) Taper expression:** $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$, where $D$ and $d$ are the large and small diameters, and $L$ is the length of taper.
- **(1 mark) Any two methods:** *Compound rest (swivel slide) method* and *Tailstock set-over (offset) method*. (Other valid methods: form tool, taper turning attachment.)

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between grooving and step cutting in lathe operations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Grooving:** A narrow recess is produced on a cylindrical surface by feeding the tool **radially inward only** (no longitudinal feed). The groove width equals the width of the grooving tool. Used for oil grooves, thread relief, etc.
- **(1 mark) Step cutting:** A sudden change in diameter (a shoulder) is produced by combining **radial plunge feed + longitudinal traverse**. The diameter change is abrupt, not gradual.
- **(1 mark) Key contrast:** Grooving = single radial feed, narrow width; Step cutting = combined feed, produces a flat shoulder perpendicular to the axis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **compound rest method of taper turning**. Derive the formula for the swivel angle of the compound rest. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel workpiece of **50 mm diameter** and **180 mm length** is to be taper turned over a length of **100 mm** to a final diameter of **35 mm** using the **tailstock offset method**. Calculate (i) the taper ratio, (ii) the tailstock offset, and (iii) the compound rest angle. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Compound Rest Method

**Sketch (description in lieu of drawing, 2 marks):**
A labelled sketch should show: lathe bed, headstock, tailstock, workpiece held between chuck and live centre, cutting tool mounted on the swivelled compound rest, with the compound rest base inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the lathe axis, and the taper length $L$, large diameter $D$, small diameter $d$ marked on the workpiece.

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- The compound rest is a graduated slide that can be swivelled to any angle on its graduated base.
- For taper turning, the compound rest is set to the **half-taper angle** $\alpha$ and locked.
- Only the **compound slide handwheel** is used to feed the tool — the cross-slide and carriage are disengaged from auto-feed.
- The tool moves in a straight line inclined at $\alpha$ to the lathe axis, cutting a conical surface.
- This method is highly accurate and is preferred for **steep tapers** and when a **precise, repeatable angle** is required.

**Derivation (2 marks):**
See Section 3.1 above. The result is:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{D - d}{2L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)$$

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $D = 50$ mm, $d = 35$ mm
- $L = 100$ mm (length of taper)
- $l = 180$ mm (total length of workpiece)

**(i) Taper ratio (2 marks):**
$$T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{50 - 35}{100} = \frac{15}{100} = 0.15 \text{ mm/mm}$$

**Answer:** Taper = **0.15 mm/mm** = **15 mm per 100 mm length**

**(ii) Tailstock offset (3 marks):**
$$\text{Offset} = \frac{(D - d) \, l}{2L} = \frac{(50 - 35) \times 180}{2 \times 100} = \frac{15 \times 180}{200} = \frac{2700}{200}$$

$$\text{Offset} = 13.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Answer:** Offset = **13.5 mm**

**(iii) Compound rest angle (2 marks):**
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{50 - 35}{2 \times 100}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{15}{200}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.075)$$

$$\alpha = 4.29^\circ$$

**Answer:** $\alpha \approx$ **4.29°** (≈ 4° 17')

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **tailstock offset method** of taper turning with a neat sketch. State any **two advantages** and **two limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Perform the following operations on a **40 mm diameter mild steel workpiece of 150 mm length**:
- (i) Turn a **groove of width 4 mm and depth 3 mm** at the middle of the workpiece.
- (ii) Machine a **step** from 40 mm to 30 mm diameter over a length of 25 mm at one end.

State the **tooling, cutting parameters, and procedure** for each operation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Tailstock Offset Method

**Sketch (2 marks):**
Top view of the lathe showing the headstock centre line, the tailstock shifted laterally by offset $e$, the workpiece held between chuck and live (offset) centre, and a right-angled triangle with base $l$ (total length) and height $e$ (offset).

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- The tailstock base is unclamped from the lathe bed.
- It is shifted **perpendicular to the lathe axis** (i.e., along the cross-slide direction) by a calculated offset.
- The workpiece is then mounted between the chuck (headstock centre) and the offset tailstock centre.
- As the workpiece rotates, the axis of the workpiece is inclined, and a straight tool feed (parallel to the lathe axis) produces a tapered surface.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$\text{Offset} = \frac{(D - d) \, l}{2L}$$

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
- Suitable for **long tapers** (full length of the workpiece can be tapered).
- Setup is simple; no need for special attachments.

**Limitations (1 mark — any two):**
- Limited to **small tapers** (typically < 10° half-angle) because excessive offset causes tool-workpiece collision.
- The live centre wears unevenly on the offset side.
- Not suitable for hollow workpieces that need internal support.

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Grooving and Step Cutting

**Given:** $D_{initial} = 40$ mm, length $= 150$ mm, mild steel.

**(i) Grooving (3.5 marks):**

- **Tooling:** HSS grooving tool, 4 mm width, ground with 0° rake and 6° side clearance. Tool post set at 90° to lathe axis.
- **Cutting parameters:**
  - $V = 25$ m/min (mild steel).
  - $N = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 40} = 199$ rpm ≈ **200 rpm**.
  - Feed: 0.08 mm/rev (manual cross-feed).
- **Procedure:**
  1. Mount and centre the workpiece; set spindle speed to 200 rpm.
  2. Touch tool to surface; zero the cross-slide dial.
  3. Move carriage to the **midpoint (75 mm)** using the carriage handwheel.
  4. Apply cutting fluid.
  5. Feed cross-slide **incrementally** to a total depth of 3 mm (e.g., three passes of 1 mm each, with full retraction between passes for swarf clearance).
  6. Withdraw tool, stop spindle, and **measure groove width** with vernier (should be 4 mm) and **depth** with a depth micrometer.
  7. Deburr the groove edges.

**(ii) Step Cutting (3.5 marks):**

- **Tooling:** Standard HSS turning tool, ground to standard geometry (rake 10°, clearance 6°). Compound rest set to 0° for a square shoulder.
- **Cutting parameters:**
  - Same $V = 25$ m/min; for the 40 mm diameter, $N \approx 200$ rpm.
  - Depth of radial cut = $(40 - 30)/2 = 5$ mm.
  - Feed: 0.10–0.15 mm/rev (roughing); 0.05 mm/rev (finishing).
- **Procedure:**
  1. Face the end of the workpiece to a clean reference.
  2. Turn the full 40 mm diameter along the entire length (rough + finish).
  3. Set carriage dial to **0** at the end face; traverse carriage by **25 mm** to mark the shoulder position.
  4. Take a **roughing radial plunge** of 4 mm (i.e., diameter reduced to 32 mm).
  5. Feed longitudinally along the 25 mm length to form the shoulder.
  6. Repeat with a **finishing pass** of 0.5 mm depth to reach 30 mm.
  7. Chamfer the sharp corner with a light file or a separate chamfer tool.
  8. Measure the 30 mm portion with a micrometer (verify diameter) and the 25 mm length with vernier (verify length).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit-mixing in offset formula:** Students often substitute $L$ (length of taper) in place of $l$ (total length of workpiece) in the offset formula. *Always* re-read the question carefully and label your variables.
> 2. **Forgetting the half:** The compound rest angle uses $(D - d)/2$, not $(D - d)$. The factor of 2 is the **most-skipped** step in the board exam.
> 3. **Radian-degree confusion:** The $\tan^{-1}$ function on a calculator returns degrees *only* if set to "DEG" mode. Otherwise, the answer is in radians. **Always state the mode** or convert.
> 4. **Grooving tool width:** Forgetting to state that **groove width = tool width** costs 0.5–1 mark. Mention it explicitly.
> 5. **Spindle speed not computed:** The 14-mark question often has a sub-part asking for cutting speed or spindle speed. Use $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$ and the **largest diameter** for a safe starting speed.
> 6. **Sketch quality:** Sketches must include labels (lathe bed, workpiece, tool, taper angle, large/small diameter, length of taper). An unlabelled sketch earns partial credit only.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your final-night revision sheet. Each bullet is a guaranteed high-yield fact for the KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Grooving** = radial feed only; **groove width = tool width**; peck-cutting is essential for deep grooves.
- ✅ **Step cutting** = radial plunge *plus* longitudinal feed; produces a sudden diameter change (shoulder).
- ✅ **Taper turning** = gradual diameter change producing a conical surface.
- ✅ **Compound rest angle** formula: $\alpha = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{D - d}{2L}\right)$ — angle is set on the **graduated base** of the compound rest.
- ✅ **Tailstock offset** formula: $\text{Offset} = \dfrac{(D - d)\, l}{2L}$ — variable $l$ is **total** length, not taper length.
- ✅ Compound rest method → **steep, short, accurate** tapers; best for **single taper** on a workpiece.
- ✅ Tailstock offset method → **gentle, long tapers**; tool feed is **parallel to the lathe axis** (no swivelling).
- ✅ Taper ratio $T = \dfrac{D - d}{L}$ is a **dimensionless** number (same units throughout).
- ✅ Spindle speed from cutting speed: $N = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D}$; use the **largest diameter** for the safest initial rpm.
- ✅ Always state the **width of grooving tool = width of groove** explicitly in the record.
- ✅ Sketch every taper-turning question — labelled diagrams are compulsory for full marks in 14-mark questions.
- ✅ Standard cutting parameters for **mild steel**: $V = 25\text{–}35$ m/min, feed $0.1\text{–}0.2$ mm/rev.
- ✅ For **aluminium**: $V = 60\text{–}90$ m/min; higher spindle speeds; use cutting fluid (kerosene or soluble oil).
- ✅ **Safety:** Never leave the chuck key in the chuck; wear safety goggles; use a chip guard; retract the tool before stopping the spindle.
- ✅ Common defects and remedies must be memorised (burr at shoulder, oversize diameter, non-square shoulder, taper-angle error).

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Lathe: Grooving, Taper Turning, and Step Cutting** 🔧
*Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) assessment guidelines.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lathe: Thread Cutting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Lathe: Thread Cutting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2 — Lathe: Thread Cutting

## 1. Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thread Cutting** on a lathe is a precision machining operation in which a **single-point cutting tool**, ground to the inverse profile of the required thread, is advanced **radially** and simultaneously fed **axially** along a rotating cylindrical workpiece, following a precise **helical path** governed by the leadscrew–half-nut mechanism, in order to produce a continuous helical groove forming **external (or internal) screw threads**.

### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Syllabus Vocabulary)

- **Major Diameter ($D$)**: The largest diameter of the screw thread (measured at the crest for external threads).
- **Minor Diameter ($d$)**: The smallest diameter of the screw thread (measured at the root).
- **Pitch ($p$)**: The axial distance between two adjacent crests (or roots) of a single-start thread. Unit: **mm** (metric) or **threads per inch / TPI** (imperial).
- **Lead ($L$)**: The axial advance of the thread per revolution of the workpiece. For a single-start thread, $L = p$.
- **Thread Angle ($2\alpha$)**: The included angle between the two flanks of the thread profile.
- **Depth of Thread ($h$)**: The radial distance from the crest to the root of the thread.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Wrapped Ribbon Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine wrapping a **triangular ribbon** tightly around a pencil. The ribbon's cross-sectional shape (V, square, trapezoidal) is exactly the inverse of what the tool must cut. The **spacing between successive wraps** is the *pitch*, the **axial distance the pencil advances per wrap** is the *lead*, and the **angle of the V-cut into the ribbon's edge** is the *thread angle*. The lathe's leadscrew acts like a giant, mechanically-perfect version of your hand guiding that ribbon at a constant pace.

In essence, **the leadscrew dictates the helix**, and **the tool dictates the profile**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Thread Cutting Is a Special Operation

Unlike ordinary turning where feed is independent of spindle speed, **thread cutting demands perfect synchronization** between the **spindle rotation** and the **axial feed of the tool**. This is achieved through the **leadscrew–half-nut (split nut) mechanism**, which mechanically couples carriage movement to spindle rotation at a fixed ratio (the leadscrew pitch).

### 2.2 Geometric Decomposition of a V-Thread

For a sharp V-thread (theoretical) with included angle $2\alpha$ and pitch $p$:

- The theoretical triangle height $H$ is:
$$H = \frac{p}{2} \cdot \cot(\alpha)$$

- For the ISO metric profile, $2\alpha = 60° \Rightarrow \alpha = 30° \Rightarrow \cot(30°) = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$.

- However, **the actual thread is truncated** at the crest and root (each by an amount $H/8$ for ISO metric), so the working depth of engagement is:
$$h = H - 2 \cdot \frac{H}{8} = \frac{3H}{4} = 0.6134 \cdot p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard depth-of-cut figure **0.6134 p** is the single most-tested number for ISO metric thread cutting in KTU examinations. Memorize it.

### 2.3 Spindle Speed Selection

The cutting speed in thread cutting is intentionally **low (typically 1/3 to 1/2 of normal turning speed)** to:
- Reduce tool wear (the tool is in continuous engagement).
- Avoid chatter from the slender thread-cutting tool geometry.
- Prevent heat build-up in a narrow cutting zone.

$$N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$$

where $N$ is spindle speed in **rpm**, $V$ is cutting speed in **m/min**, and $D$ is the major diameter of the workpiece in **mm**.

### 2.4 Feed Per Revolution

Unlike turning, the feed in thread cutting is **not freely chosen** — it is locked to the pitch:
$$f_{\text{rev}} = p \quad (\text{mm/rev})$$

The tool advances axially by exactly $p$ mm for every spindle revolution because the half-nut is engaged to the leadscrew.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Thread Cutting — Master These for the Board Exam.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lead (single start) | $L = p$ | mm | Single-start helix |
| 2 | Lead (multi start) | $L = n \cdot p$ | mm | $n$ = number of starts |
| 3 | TPI to Pitch | $p = 25.4 / \text{TPI}$ | mm | Imperial-to-metric conversion |
| 4 | Pitch to TPI | $\text{TPI} = 25.4 / p$ | threads/inch | Metric-to-imperial |
| 5 | Theoretical height | $H = 0.866 \cdot p$ | mm | ISO metric V-thread ($60°$) |
| 6 | Actual depth (metric) | $h = 0.6134 \cdot p$ | mm | ISO metric, truncated |
| 7 | Actual depth (Whitworth) | $h = 0.6403 \cdot p$ | mm | BSW / BSF ($55°$) |
| 8 | Minor diameter (external) | $d = D - 2h$ | mm | Final machined root dia. |
| 9 | Spindle speed | $N = (V \cdot 1000) / (\pi \cdot D)$ | rpm | $V$ in m/min |
| 10 | Feed per revolution | $f = p$ | mm/rev | Locked to pitch |
| 11 | Number of passes | $n_p = h / (\Delta d)$ | dimensionless | $\Delta d$ = depth increment per pass |

> **Note on notation:** Absolute values (when required) are rendered as $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking the table layout.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Thread cutting on a lathe is the **backbone of fastener manufacturing, hydraulic fittings, lead screws for CNC machines, and custom-threaded shafts** in low-volume production. While CNC and thread-rolling dominate mass production, **lathe thread cutting remains indispensable for repair work, prototypes, large-diameter threads, and odd-pitch custom threads** that cannot be rolled or cut by self-opening die heads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Actual Depth of an ISO Metric V-Thread

We start from the fundamental geometry of the sharp V-thread profile.

**Step 1 — Geometry of the sharp V-triangle.**
The thread profile is an isosceles triangle with apex angle $2\alpha$ and base equal to the pitch $p$. The height $H$ of this triangle is the perpendicular distance from apex to base.

Using trigonometry on one half of the triangle (a right triangle with base $p/2$ and angle $\alpha$ at the apex):
$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{p/2}{H}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the theoretical height $H$.**
$$H = \frac{p/2}{\tan(\alpha)} = \frac{p}{2} \cdot \cot(\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute ISO metric values.**
For the ISO metric thread, $2\alpha = 60°$, hence $\alpha = 30°$.
$$\begin{aligned}
H &= \frac{p}{2} \cdot \cot(30°) \\
  &= \frac{p}{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} \\
  &= 0.8660254 \cdot p
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Account for crest and root truncation.**
Per ISO 261, both the crest and the root of the thread are truncated by an amount equal to $H/8$ (to provide clearance and to prevent stress concentration at a theoretical sharp corner). The working depth becomes:
$$\begin{aligned}
h &= H - 2 \cdot \frac{H}{8} \\
  &= H \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) \\
  &= \frac{3H}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form for ISO metric.**
$$\begin{aligned}
h &= \frac{3}{4} \cdot 0.8660254 \cdot p \\
  &= 0.649519 \cdot p \quad \text{(theoretical engagement depth)}
\end{aligned}$$

The machining reference value $0.6134 \cdot p$ is the **radial infeed of the tool** to the minor diameter (which is slightly less than the engagement depth due to tool-nose radius compensation and the standard ISO fundamental-triangle geometry often tabulated as $H_1 = 0.5413 \cdot p$). For KTU board exam purposes, **$h = 0.6134 \cdot p$ is the accepted answer** for the depth of cut.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** An **M20 × 2.5** external thread is to be cut on a mild-steel workpiece of **30 mm diameter** on a lathe. Given: cutting speed $V = 15$ m/min, roughing depth = **0.2 mm/pass**, finishing depth = **0.05 mm/pass**. Determine the major design parameters.

**Given Data:**
- Nominal (major) diameter: $D = 20$ mm
- Pitch: $p = 2.5$ mm
- Workpiece blank diameter: $D_w = 30$ mm
- Cutting speed: $V = 15$ m/min
- Roughing increment: $\Delta d_r = 0.2$ mm
- Finishing increment: $\Delta d_f = 0.05$ mm

**Step 1 — Depth of thread.**
$$h = 0.6134 \cdot p = 0.6134 \times 2.5 = 1.5335 \text{ mm} \approx 1.534 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Minor diameter.**
$$d = D - 2h = 20 - 2(1.534) = 20 - 3.068 = 16.932 \text{ mm} \approx 16.93 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Spindle speed.**
$$N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{15 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{15000}{62.832} \approx 238.7 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Number of roughing passes.**
Using a total roughing depth of approximately 90% of $h$ (leaving 10% for finishing):
$$h_r = 0.9 \times 1.534 = 1.3806 \text{ mm}$$
$$n_r = \frac{h_r}{\Delta d_r} = \frac{1.3806}{0.2} = 6.903 \Rightarrow 7 \text{ roughing passes}$$

**Step 5 — Remaining depth for finishing.**
$$h_f = h - h_r = 1.534 - 1.3806 = 0.1534 \text{ mm}$$
$$n_f = \frac{h_f}{\Delta d_f} = \frac{0.1534}{0.05} = 3.068 \Rightarrow 4 \text{ finishing passes}$$

**Step 6 — Total number of passes.**
$$n_{\text{total}} = n_r + n_f = 7 + 4 = 11 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 7 — Feed per revolution (locked to pitch).**
$$f = p = 2.5 \text{ mm/rev}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Major diameter | $D$ | **20.000** | mm |
| Pitch | $p$ | **2.500** | mm |
| Depth of thread | $h$ | **1.534** | mm |
| Minor diameter | $d$ | **16.932** | mm |
| Spindle speed | $N$ | **239** | rpm |
| Feed per rev | $f$ | **2.500** | mm/rev |
| Roughing passes | $n_r$ | **7** | passes |
| Finishing passes | $n_f$ | **4** | passes |
| Total passes | $n_{\text{total}}$ | **11** | passes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Thread Cutting on a Lathe

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional workflow** of an external thread-cutting experiment, including the decision gates and verification points encountered in a typical KTU lab session.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Mount Workpiece in 3-Jaw Chuck and Support with Live Center] --> B[Step 2: Mount Thread Cutting Tool with Correct Profile on Tool Post]
    B --> C[Step 3: Set Tool Tip Exactly at Workpiece Center Height using Gauge]
    C --> D[Step 4: Set Compound Slide to Half Thread Angle 29 degrees for Metric]
    D --> E[Step 5: Calculate Spindle Speed N and Set Gear Train for Required Pitch]
    E --> F[Step 6: Engage Half Nut When Thread Dial Mark Aligns with Reference Line]
    F --> G[Step 7: Make First Pass with Small Radial Infeed]
    G --> H[Step 8: Stop Spindle Rapidly and Reverse to Return Carriage to Start]
    H --> I[Step 9: Disengage Half Nut and Increment Infeed by Delta d]
    I --> J{Step 10: Thread Depth Achieved as per Calculation?}
    J -->|No| F
    J -->|Yes| K[Step 11: Verify Thread Profile with Thread Ring Gauge]
    K --> L{Step 12: Gauge Passes Smoothly?}
    L -->|No| I
    L -->|Yes| M[Step 13: Disengage Half Nut, Retract Tool, Stop Machine]
    M --> N[Step 14: Measure Minor Diameter with Micrometer and Record Result]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Leadscrew–Half-Nut Synchronization Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Spindle Motor and Gearbox] --> B1[Spindle and Chuck rotating Workpiece]
    B1 --> C1[Leadscrew coupled via Change Gear Train to Spindle]
    C1 --> D1[Half Nut engaged on Leadscrew]
    D1 --> E1[Carriage translates axially at Pitch Rate]
    E1 --> F1[Compound Slide feeds tool radially for Depth Increment]
    F1 --> G1[Thread Profile generated on Workpiece]
    H1[Thread Dial Indicator] --> D1
    H1 -. Validates engagement position .-> D1
```

### 4.3 Tool Geometry — Thread Cutting Tool (Profile View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Thread Cutting HSS Tool] --> T2[Thread Angle 60 degrees metric]
    T1 --> T3[Side Rake Angle 0 to 5 degrees]
    T1 --> T4[Front Clearance 10 to 15 degrees]
    T1 --> T5[Side Clearance 10 to 15 degrees]
    T1 --> T6[Nose Radius as per Pitch and Profile]
    T2 --> T7[Generates V Profile on Workpiece]
    T3 --> T8[Zero Rake Preferred to Avoid Chatter in Narrow Profile]
    T6 --> T9[Smaller Nose for Fine Pitch, Larger for Coarse]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the diagrams:** These schematics are **functional architecture flow maps**, not physical engineering drawings. They map the *interactions* between machine subsystems (spindle, leadscrew, half-nut, carriage, compound slide, thread dial) that together produce a thread. In your KTU lab record, you are expected to draw a **labelled line diagram of the lathe** with the thread-cutting tool and the workpiece, indicating the directions of rotation, feed, and infeed.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define (i) Pitch, (ii) Lead, and (iii) Thread angle. How are pitch and lead related for a single-start and a multi-start thread?**

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Pitch ($p$):** The axial distance between two adjacent crests (or roots) of a thread, measured parallel to the axis. Unit: mm or TPI.
- **(ii) Lead ($L$):** The axial distance the thread advances in one complete revolution of the workpiece. Unit: mm or in.
- **(iii) Thread Angle ($2\alpha$):** The included angle between the two flanks of the thread profile, measured at the crest. For ISO metric threads, $2\alpha = 60°$.

**Relation:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Single-start: } L &= p \\
\text{Multi-start: } L &= n \cdot p
\end{aligned}$$
where $n$ is the number of independent thread starts (helices).

*(3 marks: 1 mark per definition + 1 mark for relation. Examiner's tip: students commonly lose marks by writing only "lead equals pitch" without qualifying it as single-start.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is the function of a thread dial indicator? Why is it preferred over arbitrary half-nut engagement in thread cutting?**

**Model Answer:**

The **thread dial indicator (TDI)** is a graduated dial coupled to the leadscrew. Its function is to **indicate the precise spindle angular position at which the half-nut must be re-engaged** for the next cutting pass, ensuring that the carriage and spindle are perfectly in-phase.

**Why it is essential:**
- For a **single-start thread**, the dial can be engaged at **any line** as the spindle returns to the same position once per revolution.
- For a **multi-start thread**, the spindle must complete $n$ revolutions before the thread profile repeats, so the dial must be engaged at the **same reference line (typically zero)** for each pass.
- Without the TDI, a small angular error would cause the tool to cut into a previously machined groove, **chipping the tool or producing a defective thread**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the role and engagement rules.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Style, Module Experiment 2]**

---

#### ✦ Question A (14 Marks)

**An M20 × 2.5 external thread is to be cut on a mild steel workpiece of 30 mm blank diameter on a lathe. Cutting speed $V = 15$ m/min. Roughing depth per pass = 0.2 mm; finishing depth per pass = 0.05 mm.**

**(a) Calculate the depth of thread, minor diameter, spindle speed, total number of passes, and the feed per revolution.**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Depth of thread** (ISO metric, $2\alpha = 60°$):
$$h = 0.6134 \cdot p = 0.6134 \times 2.5 = 1.5335 \text{ mm} \approx 1.534 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Minor diameter:**
$$d = D - 2h = 20 - 2(1.534) = 16.932 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{V \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{15 \times 1000}{\pi \times 20} = \frac{15000}{62.832} = 238.7 \text{ rpm} \approx 239 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 4 — Total depth available:**
Total radial infeed required = $h = 1.534$ mm.
Allowing 90% for roughing and 10% for finishing:
$$h_r = 0.9 \times 1.534 = 1.381 \text{ mm}, \quad h_f = 0.153 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Number of roughing passes:**
$$n_r = \frac{h_r}{\Delta d_r} = \frac{1.381}{0.2} = 6.9 \Rightarrow 7 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 6 — Number of finishing passes:**
$$n_f = \frac{h_f}{\Delta d_f} = \frac{0.153}{0.05} = 3.06 \Rightarrow 4 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 7 — Total passes:**
$$n_{\text{total}} = 7 + 4 = 11 \text{ passes}$$

**Step 8 — Feed per revolution** (locked to pitch):
$$f = p = 2.5 \text{ mm/rev}$$

**Tabulated Result:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Depth of thread, $h$ | **1.534 mm** |
| Minor diameter, $d$ | **16.932 mm** |
| Spindle speed, $N$ | **239 rpm** |
| Total passes | **11** |
| Feed per rev, $f$ | **2.5 mm/rev** |

---

**(b) With the help of a procedure, explain the step-by-step methodology of cutting this thread on a lathe, including tool geometry, machine settings, and verification.**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Tool Geometry** (single-point HSS thread-cutting tool, ISO metric profile):
- Thread angle: **60°**
- Side rake angle: **0°** (zero rake to avoid chatter; the narrow profile is fragile)
- Front clearance angle: **10° to 15°**
- Side clearance angle: **10° to 15°**
- Nose radius: matched to the pitch and profile

**Procedure:**

1. **Workpiece mounting:** Hold the mild-steel blank (30 mm Ø) in a 3-jaw self-centering chuck, support the free end with a **live center** to prevent deflection.

2. **Tool mounting:** Mount the thread-cutting tool on the tool post with its **tip exactly at the workpiece center height** (verified with a height gauge). Any error causes flank-angle distortion.

3. **Compound slide setting:** Set the **compound slide to 29°** (i.e., half of the 60° thread angle), so the infeed is normal to the thread flank — this produces a cleaner cut and easier swarf flow.

4. **Spindle speed selection:** Set the spindle to **239 rpm** as computed.

5. **Change gear and leadscrew engagement:** Set the **gear train / quick-change gearbox** to the position corresponding to a pitch of **2.5 mm** on the leadscrew.

6. **First cut (spring cut / scratch pass):** Bring the tool tip gently to touch the workpiece OD (set zero on the cross-slide dial). Move the carriage to the start of the thread, with the half-nut **disengaged**. Engage the half-nut when the **thread dial mark** aligns with the reference line. Take a very light scratch pass (~0.05 mm) along the entire thread length, then stop the spindle.

7. **Checking the pitch:** Use a **thread pitch gauge** on the scratch thread to **verify the pitch is exactly 2.5 mm** before proceeding. This is a critical checkpoint — if pitch is wrong, the gear train selection is incorrect and must be reset.

8. **Successive roughing passes:** Withdraw the cross-slide, return the carriage to the start, **re-engage the half-nut at the same thread-dial mark**, and make successive passes increasing the radial infeed by **0.2 mm** per pass. After each pass, **stop the spindle** (do not reverse it) and return the carriage to the start position with the cross-slide withdrawn.

9. **Finishing passes:** After **7 roughing passes** (1.4 mm of infeed), switch to **0.05 mm finishing infeed** for the remaining **4 passes** to reach the final minor diameter of **16.93 mm**.

10. **Verification:** Test the finished thread with a **thread ring gauge (Go / No-Go type)**. The "Go" gauge should screw on smoothly; the "No-Go" gauge should not enter more than 1–2 threads. Measure the **minor diameter with a micrometer** and confirm it is **16.93 ± 0.05 mm**.

11. **Disengagement:** Stop the spindle, **disengage the half-nut first**, then withdraw the tool. Reversing this order may damage the leadscrew or half-nut.

**Safety:** Always use **eye protection**, keep hands clear of the rotating chuck, and never leave the half-nut engaged while the carriage is being hand-cranked.

---

#### ✦ Question B (14 Marks)

**Explain in detail the thread cutting operation on a center lathe with:**

**(a) A labelled description of the standard thread nomenclature and the various standard thread profiles (V, ACME, Square, Buttress) with their included angles and typical applications.**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Standard Thread Nomenclature:**

- **Major diameter ($D$):** Largest diameter, measured at the crest.
- **Minor diameter ($d$):** Smallest diameter, measured at the root.
- **Pitch diameter ($d_p$):** The diameter of an imaginary cylinder on which a helical line of width equal to the thread space would lie.
- **Pitch ($p$):** Axial distance between adjacent crests.
- **Lead ($L$):** Axial advance per revolution.
- **Thread angle ($2\alpha$):** Included angle between the two flanks.
- **Depth of thread ($h$):** Radial distance from crest to root.

**Standard Thread Profiles:**

| Profile | Thread Angle | Typical Application | Depth Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ISO Metric V** | **60°** | General fasteners, bolts, nuts (international standard) | $h = 0.6134 \cdot p$ |
| **BSW / BSF (Whitworth)** | **55°** | Legacy British engineering, pipes, general fasteners | $h = 0.6403 \cdot p$ |
| **ACME** | **29°** | Power transmission, lead screws of machine tools (e.g., lathe carriage) | $h = 0.5 \cdot p + 0.010"$ |
| **Square** | **0°** (with 5° clearance flanks) | Power transmission where high efficiency is needed | $h = 0.5 \cdot p$ |
| **Buttress** | **45° / 7°** | High one-directional load (e.g., screw jacks, dam gates) | Profile-specific |

**Key observations:**
- The V-profile is strongest in tension but produces a wedging action.
- ACME and square are preferred where high torque transmission and low friction are needed.
- The buttress combines high axial-load capacity in one direction with ease of machining.

---

**(b) The role of the leadscrew–half-nut mechanism and the thread dial indicator, with the rules for their use in single-start and multi-start thread cutting.**
**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Leadscrew–Half-Nut Mechanism:**

- The **leadscrew** is a precision long-threaded shaft running parallel to the bed, driven through a **change-gear train** (or quick-change gearbox) from the spindle.
- Its pitch is **fixed by the gear train selection**; the ratio determines the axial feed per spindle revolution.
- The **half-nut (split nut)** is a clamp that engages the leadscrew threads. When closed, it rigidly couples the carriage to the leadscrew, forcing the carriage to translate axially at a fixed rate.
- For thread cutting, this rate **must equal the required thread pitch $p$** — this is what synchronizes the tool's axial motion with the spindle's rotation.

**Thread Dial Indicator (TDI) — Role and Rules:**

- The TDI is a small graduated dial on the carriage, geared to the leadscrew. It indicates the **angular position of the leadscrew**, and hence the spindle.
- Its purpose: indicate the **exact moment** to engage the half-nut so the tool picks up the previously cut thread groove perfectly.

**Engagement Rules:**

| Thread Type | TDI Line to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-start, any pitch** | **Any line** on the dial | The spindle returns to the same position once per revolution. |
| **Multi-start, $n$ starts** | **Same reference line** (typically zero) for all passes | The profile repeats only every $n$ revolutions. |
| **Coarse threads (pitch > 6 mm or TPI < 4)** | **Any line** | Engagement window is wide; no error introduced. |
| **Fine threads (pitch < 6 mm or TPI > 4)** | **Reference line only** | The engagement window is narrow; only the zero line ensures the nut falls into the same leadscrew thread. |

**Procedure implication:** The half-nut must be **disengaged** before the tool is returned to the start, and **re-engaged only when the chosen TDI mark** aligns. Failure to follow this protocol causes **chattering, tool chipping, or a multi-start thread with an irregular helix**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Thread-Cutting Questions:**
>
> 1. **Wrong depth formula** — Writing $h = 0.866 p$ (the theoretical sharp V height) instead of the **accepted $h = 0.6134 p$**. The former is geometric depth; the latter is the **machining reference** for the tool infeed.
> 2. **Confusing pitch and lead** — For multi-start threads, $L = n \cdot p$. Writing $L = p$ unconditionally costs full marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the thread pitch verification (spring cut)** — Always take a light scratch pass and verify with a thread pitch gauge before any heavy roughing cut. KTU lab record viva questions frequently probe this.
> 4. **Engaging the half-nut in the wrong order on shutdown** — Disengage half-nut **before** retracting the tool. Reversing this order can damage the leadscrew.
> 5. **Using the wrong cutting speed** — Thread cutting requires a **reduced cutting speed** (≈ 1/3 to 1/2 of normal turning speed). Using the regular turning $V$ is a common error.
> 6. **Forgetting the thread relief groove** — For threads terminated before the end of a workpiece, a **relief (run-out) groove** must be provided so the tool can run off cleanly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Lathe Thread Cutting**

- **Thread cutting** produces a helical groove on a rotating workpiece by synchronized axial feed (leadscrew) and radial infeed (compound slide).
- **Pitch ($p$)** = axial distance between adjacent crests; **Lead ($L$)** = axial advance per revolution. For single-start threads $L = p$; for multi-start $L = n \cdot p$.
- **ISO Metric V-thread:** angle = **60°**; depth = **$0.6134 \cdot p$**; minor diameter $d = D - 2h$.
- **Whitworth (BSW/BSF):** angle = **55°**; depth = **$0.6403 \cdot p$**.
- **ACME:** angle = **29°**; preferred for power-transmission lead screws.
- **Spindle speed:** $N = (V \times 1000) / (\pi \cdot D)$, with $V$ deliberately **low** (1/3 to 1/2 of normal turning speed).
- **Feed per revolution in thread cutting is fixed:** $f = p$ — you **cannot** change it independently of the pitch.
- **Compound slide is set to half the thread angle** (29° for metric, 27.5° for Whitworth) so that the tool infeed is normal to one flank.
- **Thread Dial Indicator (TDI):** for single-start threads, any line on the dial is acceptable; for multi-start threads, the **same reference line** must be used for every pass.
- **Procedure order:** spring cut → verify pitch with pitch gauge → successive roughing passes → finishing passes → verify with thread ring gauge (Go / No-Go) → measure minor diameter with micrometer.
- **Total number of passes** $n = h / \Delta d$, with roughing depth typically 0.2 mm and finishing depth 0.05 mm.
- **Always disengage the half-nut before** retracting the tool and before stopping the spindle for return — this prevents leadscrew and tool damage.
- **Thread relief groove (run-out groove)** is mandatory wherever a thread ends in mid-workpiece, to allow the tool a clean exit.
- **Common KTU viva questions:** "Why is the cutting speed reduced for thread cutting?" (Answer: continuous tool engagement, narrow profile, heat build-up). "Why must the compound slide be set to half-angle?" (Answer: to make the infeed normal to the thread flank, improving surface finish and chip flow).
- **Safety:** always wear eye protection, never measure a rotating workpiece, and never leave the half-nut engaged while hand-cranking the carriage.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Machine Tools: Shaper, Planer, Slotter, and Milling Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Machine Tools: Shaper, Planer, Slotter, and Milling Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Reciprocating Machine Tools

In any manufacturing environment, metal removal is achieved by relative motion between a **single-point cutting tool** and the workpiece. Machine tools are classified based on how this relative motion is generated. The four machines studied in this experiment — **Shaper, Planer, Slotter, and Milling Machine** — represent two distinct families:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reciprocating Machine Tools (Shaper, Planer, Slotter):** The tool or workpiece performs a linear **back-and-forth (reciprocating)** motion. Cutting happens only on the **forward stroke**; the return stroke is idle.
> **Rotary Machine Tools (Milling Machine):** The cutter rotates continuously while the workpiece is fed linearly. Cutting happens **continuously**.

## 1.2 The Shaper

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
A **shaper** is a reciprocating-type machine tool that produces a flat surface by moving the **tool** in a linear reciprocating motion against a stationary or intermittently-fed workpiece. The cutting action occurs during the forward stroke only.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine you are icing a rectangular cake with a long, flat spatula. You push the spatula forward (cutting/frosting stroke), lift it slightly, drag it back (return stroke), and repeat. The cake (workpiece) sits still while the spatula (tool) moves. That is exactly what a shaper does — but with metal.

### Key Components
- **Base, Column, Cross-rail, Table, Ram, Tool head (Swivel), Stroke adjustment mechanism, Quick-return mechanism, Feed mechanism.**

---

## 1.3 The Planer

### Formal Definition
A **planer** is a reciprocating-type machine tool that produces large flat surfaces by moving the **workpiece** in a linear reciprocating motion against a stationary tool. Cutting occurs during the forward stroke.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Now imagine the cake is so big (like a railway sleeper or a large engine bed) that you cannot move the spatula. Instead, you slide the entire cake back and forth under a fixed knife. That is a planer — the **workpiece travels**, the tool stays put. Planers are used for **heavy, large workpieces**.

### Key Components
- **Bed, Table, Cross-rail, Tool heads (two, on cross-rail + side), Housing columns, Driving mechanism, Feed mechanism.**

---

## 1.4 The Slotter (Vertical Shaper)

### Formal Definition
A **slotter** is a reciprocating-type machine tool in which the **ram holding the tool moves vertically (up and down)** in a reciprocating motion. The cutting action occurs during the **downward stroke**. It is essentially a vertical shaper used for internal surfaces, slots, keyways, and contours.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a needle in a sewing machine — it moves straight **up and down** to pierce the fabric at one spot. A slotter does the same: the ram (like a giant needle) plunges **downward** to cut internal slots, keyways, or internal profiles inside a workpiece that is mounted on a circular table.

### Key Components
- **Base, Column, Ram (vertical), Tool head, Rotary table, Stroke adjustment, Quick-return mechanism.**

---

## 1.5 The Milling Machine

### Formal Definition
A **milling machine** is a rotary-type machine tool in which a **multi-point rotating cutter** removes metal by feeding the workpiece against it. The cutting tool has multiple teeth, and each tooth removes a small chip during each revolution, providing **continuous cutting action**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine a circular saw blade with many teeth spinning at high speed. As you push a wooden plank into it, every tooth that hits the wood takes a tiny bite. Now imagine doing this with metal — that's a milling machine. Unlike the shaper where only one tooth cuts at a time, here **many teeth cut simultaneously**, so milling is much faster.

### Key Components
- **Base, Column, Knee, Saddle, Table, Spindle, Arbor, Milling cutter, Overhanging arm (for horizontal spindle).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Vertical Milling Machine (VMC):** Spindle axis is vertical. Used for end-milling, drilling, slotting.
> **Horizontal Milling Machine (HMC):** Spindle axis is horizontal. Used for slab milling, gang milling, straddle milling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Kinematics of Reciprocating Machine Tools

The forward (cutting) stroke must be slow enough to allow proper cutting, while the return stroke must be fast to save time. This is achieved by the **Quick-Return Mechanism (Whitworth or crank-and-slot)**.

### Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)
$$QRR = \frac{\text{Time of return stroke}}{\text{Time of cutting stroke}} = \frac{T_r}{T_c}$$

For a crank-and-slot quick-return mechanism:
$$QRR = \frac{2\pi + 2\alpha}{2\pi - 2\alpha} = \frac{\pi + \alpha}{\pi - \alpha}$$

where **α** is the crank angle corresponding to the forward (cutting) stroke (in radians).

---

## 2.2 Cutting Velocity Analysis

### Cutting Speed (V) — m/min
$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{(for rotary tools)}$$

$$V = \frac{L \cdot (1 + a)}{1000 \cdot t_c} \quad \text{(for shaper/planer/slotter)}$$

where:
- $D$ = cutter diameter (mm)
- $N$ = spindle speed (rpm)
- $L$ = length of stroke (mm)
- $a$ = QRR ratio (numerical)
- $t_c$ = cutting time per stroke (min)

### Feed (f)
- **Shaper/Planer/Slotter:** Feed is the **lateral movement of the tool per stroke**, in mm/stroke.
- **Milling:** Feed is the **table movement per minute**, in mm/min, OR feed per tooth:
  $$f_m = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$$
  where $f_z$ = feed per tooth, $z$ = number of teeth, $N$ = rpm.

### Depth of Cut (d)
The perpendicular distance the tool penetrates below the original workpiece surface (mm).

---

## 2.3 Material Removal Rate (MRR)

The volume of metal removed per unit time. It is the **most important productivity index** in machining.

$$MRR = \text{Cross-sectional area of cut} \times \text{velocity of uncut chip}$$

### For Shaper / Planer / Slotter:
$$MRR = d \times f \times \frac{L \times (1 + a)}{t_c \times 1000} \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

Simplified: $MRR = d \times f \times V$  (since $V = L(1+a)/(1000\,t_c)$).

### For Milling Machine:
$$MRR = d \times w \times f_m \quad \text{(mm}^3\text{/min)}$$

where $w$ = width of cut (mm), $f_m$ = table feed (mm/min).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Shaper / Planer / Slotter** | **Milling Machine** |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed $V$ | $\dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ (rotary) or $\dfrac{L(1+a)}{1000\,t_c}$ (linear) | $\dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ |
| Feed | $f$ in mm/stroke | $f_m = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ mm/min |
| Depth of Cut | $d$ in mm | $d$ in mm |
| Width of Cut | — | $w$ in mm |
| MRR | $V \cdot d \cdot f$ mm³/min | $V \cdot d \cdot w$ mm³/min |
| Power | $P = \dfrac{MRR \times U_c}{60}$ W | Same form |
| Time Calculation | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N_s}$ (N strokes needed) | $T_m = \dfrac{L + l_c}{f_m}$ min |

**Standard Symbols:**
- $U_c$ = **specific cutting energy** (W·s/mm³) ≈ **2 to 4 J/mm³** for steel
- $l_c$ = cutter approach length (mm)
- $N_s$ = number of strokes per minute (strokes/min)

---

## 2.5 Quick-Return Mechanism Analysis

The crank-and-slot mechanism converts uniform rotary motion of the driving shaft into variable-velocity linear motion of the ram.

$$\text{Average cutting velocity } V_c = \frac{2L \cdot N_s}{1000} \text{ (m/min)}$$

$$\text{Average return velocity } V_r = V_c \times QRR$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Whitworth Quick-Return Mechanism:** Used in larger planers. Provides smoother cutting motion but is more complex.
> **Crank-and-Slot Quick-Return Mechanism:** Used in shapers and slotters. Simpler design.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Utility

| **Machine** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|
| Shaper | Small/medium flat surfaces, keyways, dovetails, simple contours |
| Planer | Large flat surfaces on heavy workpieces (engine beds, machine tool beds) |
| Slotter | Internal keyways, internal slots, contours, dies |
| Milling | Flat surfaces, slots, gears, complex 3D contours, mass production |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quick-Return Ratio for Crank-and-Slot Mechanism

**Setup:** A crank OA of length $R$ rotates uniformly. The crank-pin A slides inside a slot on the slotted lever OB. The lever OB oscillates about pivot O and drives the ram via a connecting rod. Let $\alpha$ be the angle through which the crank rotates during the forward stroke.

**Step 1 — Total crank rotation:** $360° = 2\pi$ radians per revolution.

**Step 2 — Forward (cutting) stroke corresponds to crank rotation:** $2\alpha$ radians.
Therefore the time for cutting stroke:
$$t_c = \frac{2\alpha}{2\pi} \times T = \frac{\alpha}{\pi} T$$
where $T$ is the time for one full revolution.

**Step 3 — Return stroke corresponds to crank rotation:** $2\pi - 2\alpha$ radians.
$$t_r = \frac{2\pi - 2\alpha}{2\pi} \times T = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\pi} T$$

**Step 4 — Quick-Return Ratio:**
$$QRR = \frac{t_r}{t_c} = \frac{(\pi - \alpha)/\pi}{\alpha/\pi} = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha}$$

$$\boxed{QRR = \frac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha}}$$

**Interpretation:** Smaller $\alpha$ → cutting stroke is shorter → QRR increases → return stroke is relatively faster.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cutting Time for Shaper

**Setup:** Length of stroke = $L$ mm. Number of double strokes per minute = $N_s$. Feed per stroke = $f$ mm.

**Step 1 — Distance covered in $N_s$ strokes:**
Each double stroke (forward + return) advances the tool by $f$ mm laterally.
$$\text{Total lateral feed per minute} = f \cdot N_s \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 2 — Total length to be machined:** $W$ mm (width of workpiece in feed direction).

**Step 3 — Machining time:**
$$T_m = \frac{W}{f \cdot N_s} \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 4 — Number of strokes required:**
$$N = \frac{W}{f}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Shaper (Full KTU-Style Solution)

**Problem:**
A shaper is used to machine a flat surface of length **400 mm** and width **60 mm**. The cutting speed is **18 m/min**, feed is **0.8 mm/stroke**, depth of cut is **2 mm**, and quick-return ratio is **0.6**. Calculate:
1. Cutting time per stroke
2. Number of strokes per minute
3. Material removal rate
4. Machining time

**Given:**
- $L = 400$ mm
- $W = 60$ mm (feed direction)
- $V = 18$ m/min
- $f = 0.8$ mm/stroke
- $d = 2$ mm
- $a = 0.6$ (QRR value)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cutting time per stroke:**
$$t_c = \frac{L(1 + a)}{1000 \cdot V} = \frac{400(1 + 0.6)}{1000 \times 18}$$

$$t_c = \frac{640}{18000} = 0.03556 \text{ min} \approx 2.13 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Number of strokes per minute:**
Total time per double stroke = $t_c + t_r = t_c(1 + a)$
$$= 0.03556 \times 1.6 = 0.0569 \text{ min}$$

$$N_s = \frac{1}{0.0569} \approx 17.58 \text{ strokes/min}$$

**Step 3 — Material Removal Rate:**
$$MRR = V \cdot d \cdot f = 18000 \times 2 \times 0.8$$

$$MRR = 28800 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 28.8 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time:**
Number of strokes required:
$$N = \frac{W}{f} = \frac{60}{0.8} = 75 \text{ strokes}$$

$$T_m = \frac{N}{N_s} = \frac{75}{17.58} \approx 4.27 \text{ min}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Checkpoint:** Always keep units consistent. Convert cutting speed from m/min to mm/min by multiplying by 1000 before computing MRR.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Milling Machine

**Problem:**
A milling cutter of **75 mm diameter** with **8 teeth** rotates at **200 rpm**. The feed per tooth is **0.15 mm/tooth**, depth of cut is **3 mm**, and width of cut is **40 mm**. Calculate:
1. Cutting speed
2. Table feed
3. MRR
4. Machining time for a 200 mm long surface

**Given:**
- $D = 75$ mm, $z = 8$, $N = 200$ rpm
- $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth, $d = 3$ mm, $w = 40$ mm
- $L = 200$ mm, $l_c = 15$ mm (approach)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cutting Speed:**
$$V = \frac{\pi D N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 75 \times 200}{1000}$$

$$V = \frac{47123.89}{1000} = 47.12 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 2 — Table Feed:**
$$f_m = f_z \times z \times N = 0.15 \times 8 \times 200 = 240 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — MRR:**
$$MRR = V \times d \times w = 47120 \times 3 \times 40 / 1000$$

Wait — careful with unit consistency. Use mm³/min:
$$V \text{ in mm/min} = 47123.89 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$MRR = 47123.89 \times 3 \times 40 = 5{,}654{,}867 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 5655 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Machining Time:**
$$T_m = \frac{L + l_c}{f_m} = \frac{200 + 15}{240}$$

$$T_m = \frac{215}{240} = 0.896 \text{ min} \approx 53.75 \text{ s}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Feature** | **Shaper** | **Planer** | **Slotter** | **Milling** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Motion | Linear reciprocating | Linear reciprocating | Linear reciprocating | Rotary + linear feed |
| Moved element | Tool | Workpiece | Tool | Cutter (rotary) |
| Cutting stroke | Forward | Forward | Downward | Continuous |
| Max workpiece size | Small/Medium | Very Large | Small/Medium | All sizes |
| Production rate | Low | Low–Medium | Low | **High** |
| Surface finish | Good | Good | Good | **Very Good** |
| Tool | Single-point | Single-point | Single-point | Multi-point |
| Typical MRR | Low | Low–Medium | Low | **High** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is milling faster?** In a shaper, only **one cutting edge** engages at a time. In milling, **multiple teeth** engage simultaneously, distributing the load and allowing higher feed rates.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a shaper machine.**

**Model Answer:**

A shaper is a reciprocating machine tool used to produce flat surfaces. The **ram** carrying the single-point cutting tool reciprocates linearly over the stationary workpiece mounted on the table.

**Working Principle:**
1. The drive motor rotates the **bull gear** continuously.
2. The **crank-and-slot quick-return mechanism** converts this rotary motion into linear reciprocating motion of the ram.
3. During the **forward stroke**, the tool engages the workpiece and removes metal; this stroke is slower to allow proper cutting.
4. During the **return stroke**, the tool is lifted slightly (or the ram moves faster) to return without cutting; this stroke is faster.
5. After each double stroke, the **automatic feed mechanism** advances the table laterally by a small amount (feed per stroke), gradually removing material across the workpiece width.
6. The process continues until the full surface is machined.

**Sketch (described in words):**
- Base → Column → Ram (with tool head at lower end) sliding vertically/horizontally → Work Table (on cross-rail) holding the workpiece → Drive motor at base connected via gears and crank-slot to the ram.

**(3 marks: 1 for principle, 1 for mechanism, 1 for sketch/labelling)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a shaper and a planer.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Shaper** | **Planer** |
|---|---|---|
| Moving element | **Tool (ram)** reciprocates | **Workpiece (table)** reciprocates |
| Size of workpiece | Small to medium | Very large and heavy |
| Tool head count | One tool head | Multiple (2 on cross-rail + 1 on side) |
| Stroke length | Short (typically up to 700 mm) | Long (up to several metres) |
| Cutting force | Low (light cuts) | High (heavy cuts) |
| Drive power | Small (1–10 kW) | Large (15–75 kW) |
| Application | Keyways, small surfaces | Machine beds, large plates |

**(3 marks: 1 for any 3 distinguishing points with correct terminology)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **(a)** [7 Marks]
**Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a universal horizontal milling machine. List any four operations performed on it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
The main parts of a universal horizontal milling machine are:
1. **Base** — Heavy cast iron base supporting the entire machine.
2. **Column** — Vertical casting on the base; houses the spindle and motor.
3. **Knee** — Mounted on the column ways, can be raised/lowered vertically.
4. **Saddle** — Mounted on the knee, can move transversely (cross-feed).
5. **Table** — Top-most element, moves longitudinally (longitudinal feed); has T-slots to clamp the workpiece.
6. **Overhanging Arm (Overarm)** — Supports the arbor at the outboard end for heavy cutters.
7. **Arbor** — Shaft carrying the milling cutter, supported between spindle nose and overarm.
8. **Spindle** — Rotates the arbor and cutter; driven by the main motor through gears.
9. **Universal Table Swivel** — Can be tilted up to 45° for helical milling (universal feature).

**Working:**
- The workpiece is clamped on the table.
- The motor rotates the spindle, which drives the arbor and the multi-tooth milling cutter at the required cutting speed.
- The cutter teeth engage the workpiece and remove metal.
- The table feeds the workpiece against the rotating cutter — longitudinally, transversely, or vertically (via knee).
- Coolant is applied at the cutting zone to reduce heat and improve surface finish.
- The universal swivel allows the table to be tilted, enabling helical milling.

**Four Operations on Horizontal Milling Machine:**
1. **Plain (Slab) Milling** — Producing a flat surface parallel to the cutter axis.
2. **Straddle Milling** — Two cutters mounted on the arbor to machine two parallel surfaces simultaneously.
3. **Gang Milling** — Multiple cutters stacked on the arbor for simultaneous multi-surface machining.
4. **Helical Milling** — Used for cutting helical grooves (e.g., flutes on a twist drill) using a universal table tilted to the helix angle.

**(7 marks: 3 for construction, 2 for working, 2 for operations)**

---

#### **(b)** [7 Marks]
**In a shaping operation, the length of stroke is 250 mm. The ratio of return time to cutting time is 0.5. If the cutting speed is 20 m/min and feed is 1 mm/stroke, calculate:**
**(i) Cutting time per stroke**
**(ii) Number of strokes per minute**
**(iii) Time required to machine a surface of width 80 mm**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $L = 250$ mm
- $QRR = a = 0.5$
- $V = 20$ m/min
- $f = 1$ mm/stroke
- $W = 80$ mm

**(i) Cutting time per stroke:**
$$t_c = \frac{L(1 + a)}{1000 \times V} = \frac{250 \times (1 + 0.5)}{1000 \times 20}$$

$$t_c = \frac{250 \times 1.5}{20000} = \frac{375}{20000}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 0.01875 \text{ min} = 1.125 \text{ s}}$$

**(ii) Number of strokes per minute:**
Total time per double stroke = $t_c + t_r$
$t_r = a \times t_c = 0.5 \times 0.01875 = 0.009375$ min

Total = $0.01875 + 0.009375 = 0.028125$ min/stroke

$$N_s = \frac{1}{0.028125} \approx 35.56 \text{ strokes/min}$$

$$\boxed{N_s \approx 35.56 \text{ strokes/min}}$$

**(iii) Machining time:**
Number of strokes required:
$$N = \frac{W}{f} = \frac{80}{1} = 80 \text{ strokes}$$

$$T_m = \frac{N}{N_s} = \frac{80}{35.56} \approx 2.25 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 2.25 \text{ min} \approx 135 \text{ s}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for each sub-part, 1 for final summary)**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **(a)** [7 Marks]
**Explain the working of a slotter machine with a neat sketch. List its four main applications.**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction and Working:**
A slotter (also called a vertical shaper) is a reciprocating machine tool in which the **ram moves vertically (up and down)**. The workpiece is mounted on a **rotary circular table** that can be indexed or fed transversely.

**Main Parts:**
1. **Base** — Heavy foundation.
2. **Column** — Houses the drive and guideways for the ram.
3. **Ram** — Vertical reciprocating member carrying the tool head.
4. **Tool Head** — Mounted on the ram's lower end; can be swiveled and adjusted for stroke length and position.
5. **Rotary Table** — Circular table on which the workpiece is mounted; can rotate, slide, or be fed in X-Y directions.
6. **Quick-Return Mechanism** — Crank-and-slot type, provides faster return stroke.
7. **Feed Mechanism** — Automatic, moves the rotary table after each stroke.

**Working:**
- The motor drives the crank-and-slot quick-return mechanism.
- The ram reciprocates vertically; cutting happens on the **downward stroke**.
- The workpiece, mounted on the rotary table, is fed incrementally after each stroke.
- The rotary table allows circular indexing — useful for machining internal slots at different angular positions.
- A slotting tool (often with a slight offset, called a "slotting bar") performs the cutting.

**Four Applications:**
1. **Internal keyways** in gears, pulleys, and shafts.
2. **Internal slots and grooves** in machine elements.
3. **Internal contours and profiles** in dies and moulds.
4. **Blind holes and irregular surfaces** that cannot be reached by drilling or milling.

**(7 marks: 3 for construction, 2 for working, 2 for applications)**

---

#### **(b)** [7 Marks]
**A milling cutter of 80 mm diameter having 10 teeth is used to mill a cast iron workpiece. The cutting speed is 60 m/min, feed per tooth is 0.2 mm/tooth, depth of cut is 4 mm and width of cut is 50 mm. Calculate:**
**(i) Spindle speed**
**(ii) Table feed**
**(iii) Material removal rate**
**(iv) Power required if specific cutting energy is 2.5 W·s/mm³**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- $D = 80$ mm, $z = 10$
- $V = 60$ m/min
- $f_z = 0.2$ mm/tooth
- $d = 4$ mm, $w = 50$ mm
- $U_c = 2.5$ W·s/mm³

**(i) Spindle Speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times V}{\pi \times D} = \frac{1000 \times 60}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \frac{60000}{251.327} \approx 238.73 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 239 \text{ rpm}}$$

**(ii) Table Feed:**
$$f_m = f_z \times z \times N = 0.2 \times 10 \times 238.73$$

$$\boxed{f_m \approx 477.46 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate:**
$$MRR = V \times d \times w \text{ (with V in mm/min)}$$

$V$ in mm/min = $60 \times 1000 = 60000$ mm/min

$$MRR = 60000 \times 4 \times 50 = 12{,}000{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

Convert to cm³/min:
$$\boxed{MRR = 12000 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**(iv) Power Required:**
$$P = \frac{MRR \times U_c}{60 \times 10^6} \text{ kW}$$

Using mm³/s: MRR in mm³/s = $12{,}000{,}000 / 60 = 200{,}000$ mm³/s

$$P = \frac{200000 \times 2.5}{1000} = 500 \text{ W} = 0.5 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P = 0.5 \text{ kW} = 500 \text{ W}}$$

**(7 marks: ~1.75 for each sub-part)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (Most Common Mistake):** Students frequently forget to convert $V$ from m/min to mm/min before multiplying with $d$ and $w$ in the MRR formula. This causes MRR to be off by a factor of **1000**, leading to massive cascading errors. **Always convert to consistent units before any calculation.**
> 2. **Confusion in MRR Formula:** Shaper/Planer/Slotter use $MRR = V \cdot d \cdot f$ (where $f$ is mm/stroke — but $V$ should be expressed per minute, so feed per minute is $f \cdot N_s$). Milling uses $MRR = V \cdot d \cdot w$.
> 3. **QRR Definition Confusion:** Some students write $QRR = T_c/T_r$. The correct definition is **QRR = Return time / Cutting time** — always less than 1 for the cutting stroke to be slower.
> 4. **Missing Sketch Labels:** In 14-mark questions, **unlabelled sketches get zero credit for the diagram component**. Always label all major parts.
> 5. **Stroke vs Feed in Machining Time:** Total machining time must account for approach, cutting, and overtravel — not just the workpiece length.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2**

### Core Concepts
- **Shaper:** Tool reciprocates horizontally; small/medium workpieces.
- **Planer:** Workpiece reciprocates horizontally; heavy/large workpieces; multiple tool heads.
- **Slotter:** Ram reciprocates **vertically** (downward cut); for internal slots, keyways, contours.
- **Milling Machine:** Multi-tooth **rotary** cutter; continuous cutting; high MRR.
- **Universal Milling Machine:** Has a swivel table → can perform **helical milling**.

### Key Definitions
- **Quick-Return Ratio (QRR):** $QRR = \dfrac{t_r}{t_c} = \dfrac{\pi - \alpha}{\alpha}$
- **Cutting Speed:** Relative velocity between tool and workpiece at the cutting zone.
- **Feed:** Relative displacement per stroke (reciprocating) or per minute (milling).
- **Depth of Cut (d):** Penetration perpendicular to the machined surface.
- **MRR:** Volume of metal removed per unit time; primary productivity metric.

### Master Formulas (Memorize!)
- $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ m/min
- $V = \dfrac{L(1+a)}{1000 \, t_c}$ m/min  (reciprocating)
- $MRR = V \cdot d \cdot f$ (shaper/planer/slotter)
- $MRR = V \cdot d \cdot w$ (milling)
- $T_m = \dfrac{W}{f \cdot N_s}$ (shaper)
- $T_m = \dfrac{L + l_c}{f_m}$ (milling)
- $P = \dfrac{MRR \times U_c}{60}$ (power, with consistent units)

### Differentiations to Master
- Shaper vs Planer (moving element, size, number of tool heads).
- Reciprocating vs Rotary machine tools (cutting continuity, MRR, surface finish).
- Up-milling vs Down-milling (conventional vs climb).
- Horizontal vs Vertical milling machine (spindle orientation, typical cutters).

### Lab Tip
- Always **observe and record** the QRR, stroke length, and feed mechanism of the actual shaper in the lab.
- For the milling machine, identify the **cutter type** (plain, side, end, face mill) and note the **direction of rotation vs feed** (up or down milling).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Different Types of Welding and Allied Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Different Types of Welding and Allied Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Welding** is a permanent joining process in which two or more pieces of metal (or thermoplastics) are coalesced by heating them to a suitable temperature, with or without the application of pressure, and with or without the use of a filler material, to produce a localized union through atomic diffusion and metallurgical bonding across the joint interface.

As per the **American Welding Society (AWS)** and **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCNSL308)** classification, welding processes are broadly classified into the **three primary families**:

1. **Fusion Welding** – Coalescence by melting the base metal (e.g., Arc Welding, Gas Welding, Resistance Welding).
2. **Solid-State Welding** – Coalescence without melting the base metal, primarily through pressure and diffusion (e.g., Friction Welding, Ultrasonic Welding).
3. **Brazing and Soldering** – Joining using a filler metal whose melting point is below that of the base metals, without melting the base metal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Context:** Module Experiment 2 requires the student to *physically identify, sketch, and describe* the construction and working of at least **8–10 major welding processes** along with their **allied instruments and accessories**. The viva voce (8 marks) and record (12 marks) form the bulk of the evaluation, so clear working-principle understanding is critical.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to glue two pieces of paper together permanently:
- **Arc Welding** = Using a controlled mini-lightning bolt (electric arc) to melt a metallic glue (filler rod) between the papers.
- **Gas Welding** = Using a high-temperature torch flame (oxygen + acetylene burning at ~3200 °C) to melt the papers' edges and fuse them.
- **Resistance Welding** = Squeezing two hot dogs together between two electrically heated metal plates until the surfaces melt and bond — pressure + heat in a fraction of a second.
- **Friction Welding** = Rubbing two sticks together very fast in your hands until they ignite and stick — mechanical energy converted to heat at the rubbing interface.

In essence, every welding process is simply a **controlled method of delivering sufficient energy (heat)** to the joint interface so that the atoms of the two pieces can mix and form a unified metallic bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The **energy density** (energy per unit area, measured in **W/mm²**) is the single most important factor that distinguishes one welding process from another. For example, oxy-acetylene welding has only ~10 W/mm², while laser beam welding has ~10⁶ W/mm² — that's why LBW is so precise and fast.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Welding Processes (KTU 2024 Scheme Reference)

The following hierarchy maps all major welding processes commonly examined in the **PCNSL308** lab:

| S.No | Family | Process | Energy Source | Typical Use |
|------|--------|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Fusion | Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) | Electric Arc | General fabrication, repair |
| 2 | Fusion | Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW/MIG/MAG) | Continuous Wire Arc | Automobile, structural |
| 3 | Fusion | Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW/TIG) | Non-consumable Arc | Stainless steel, aerospace |
| 4 | Fusion | Submerged Arc Welding (SAW) | Arc under Flux | Heavy plates, shipbuilding |
| 5 | Fusion | Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding (OAW) | Combustion Flame | Sheet metal, repair |
| 6 | Fusion | Plasma Arc Welding (PAW) | Constricted Arc | Precision tubes, aerospace |
| 7 | Fusion | Laser Beam Welding (LBW) | Photon Beam | Automotive, electronics |
| 8 | Fusion | Electron Beam Welding (EBW) | Electron Beam | Vacuum chamber, aerospace |
| 9 | Resistance | Spot Welding | Resistance Heating | Sheet metal, automobile bodies |
| 10 | Resistance | Seam Welding | Resistance + Rollers | Tanks, containers |
| 11 | Solid-State | Friction Welding (FW) | Frictional Heat | Shafts, valves, drill bits |
| 12 | Solid-State | Ultrasonic Welding (USW) | High-Frequency Vibration | Plastics, foils, electronics |
| 13 | Solid-State | Diffusion Welding | Heat + Pressure + Time | Aerospace superalloys |

---

## 1.4 Allied Instruments — Definition

**Allied instruments** (also called **welding accessories** or **auxiliary equipment**) are the supplementary tools and devices that support a welding process. They do **not** generate the joining heat themselves but are essential for:
- Safety (helmets, goggles, gloves, aprons)
- Process control (regulators, flowmeters, electrode holders)
- Surface preparation (wire brush, chipping hammer, grinder)
- Material handling (clamps, jigs, positioners)
- Gas delivery (cylinders, hoses, flashback arrestors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Trap:** Examiners often ask *"Is the welding transformer an allied instrument or the primary equipment?"* — **Answer:** The welding transformer/rectifier is the **primary power source**, *not* an allied instrument. Allied instruments are **everything else** (holder, helmet, earth clamp, etc.).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Working Principle of Each Welding Process

### 2.1.1 Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) — "Stick Welding"

**Principle:** An electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the workpiece. The arc melts the electrode core wire (filler metal) and a portion of the base metal, forming a molten weld pool. The flux coating disintegrates to produce:
- A **gaseous shield** of CO₂ and H₂ that protects the pool from atmospheric oxygen and nitrogen.
- A **molten slag** that floats on top of the pool, slowing cooling and protecting the solidifying weld.
- A **solid slag crust** that is chipped off after cooling.

**Polarity:**
- **DCEP** (Direct Current Electrode Positive) — standard for mild steel, deep penetration.
- **DCEN** (Direct Current Electrode Negative) — for thin sections, less penetration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** SMAW is like writing with a fountain pen — the "ink" (electrode) is consumed as you go, leaving a protective "film" (slag) over the writing.

### 2.1.2 Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW / MIG / MAG)

**Principle:** A **continuous solid wire electrode** is fed automatically through a welding gun. An arc forms between this wire and the workpiece. A shielding gas (inert for MIG — Argon; active for MAG — CO₂ or Ar+CO₂ mix) protects the weld pool.

**Modes of Metal Transfer:**
1. **Short-circuit transfer** — Low current, thin sheets, 18–22 V.
2. **Globular transfer** — Higher current, visible globules.
3. **Spray transfer** — High current (>200 A), 25–35 V, produces axial stream of fine droplets.
4. **Pulsed spray transfer** — Pulsed current provides a balance of low heat input and spray benefits.

### 2.1.3 Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW / TIG)

**Principle:** A **non-consumable tungsten electrode** produces the arc. Filler metal (if required) is added manually from a separate rod. **Inert shielding gas** (pure Argon or Argon + Helium) protects the pool.

**Special Feature:** Can use **AC current** (for aluminium, magnesium — cleaning action breaks oxide layer) or **DCEN/DCEP** (for steel, stainless steel, copper).

### 2.1.4 Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding (OAW)

**Principle:** Combustion of **acetylene (C₂H₂)** with **oxygen (O₂)** in a torch produces a flame with three distinct zones:

1. **Inner cone** — Unburnt gases, ~900 °C.
2. **Reducing zone (luminous feather)** — Partially burnt gases, ~3000 °C.
3. **Outer envelope** — Complete combustion, ~3200 °C.

**Flame Types:**
- **Neutral flame** — Equal volumes of O₂ and C₂H₂; used for mild steel, stainless steel, cast iron.
- **Carburizing (reducing) flame** — Excess acetylene; for hard-surfacing, non-ferrous metals.
- **Oxidizing flame** — Excess oxygen; for brass and bronze.

### 2.1.5 Resistance Welding

**Principle:** Uses **Joule's heating effect**: when a heavy current (5,000–20,000 A) passes through the joint under pressure, the high resistance at the interface generates localized heat, melting the metal.

**Variants:**
- **Spot welding** — Single weld nugget per cycle (used in car body assembly).
- **Seam welding** — Continuous overlapping spots using roller electrodes.
- **Projection welding** — Embossed projections localize the current path.
- **Butt welding** — Two pieces butted end-to-end and heated by resistance.

### 2.1.6 Friction Welding (Solid-State)

**Principle:** One component is held stationary while the other is rotated at high speed and pressed against it. Frictional heat raises the interface to a plastic state. Axial forge pressure then completes the bond. **No melting, no filler, no shielding gas**.

### 2.1.7 Other High-Energy Processes

- **Plasma Arc Welding (PAW):** Arc is constricted through a small copper nozzle, producing a high-velocity, high-temperature plasma jet.
- **Laser Beam Welding (LBW):** Focused coherent light beam vaporizes metal; used for high-speed, narrow welds.
- **Electron Beam Welding (EBW):** Accelerated electrons in a vacuum strike the workpiece, converting kinetic energy to heat.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Allied Instruments — Working and Purpose

### A. Primary Allied Instruments in Arc Welding

| Allied Instrument | Function | Key Specification |
|-------------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Electrode Holder** | Holds electrode at proper angle, conducts current | Rated 200/300/500 A |
| **Earth Clamp (Ground Clamp)** | Completes the electrical circuit to workpiece | Low resistance, robust jaw |
| **Welding Cable** | Carries welding current from machine to holder | 25–70 mm² copper, rubber-insulated |
| **Welding Helmet / Face Shield** | Protects face and eyes from UV/IR radiation and spatter | Auto-darkening filter (ADF) recommended |
| **Hand Gloves (Gauntlets)** | Heat and electric shock protection | Leather, heat-resistant |
| **Apron / Leather Jacket** | Body protection from spatter and heat | Split-leather preferred |
| **Wire Brush** | Removes loose slag, cleans weld bead | Steel/brass bristle |
| **Chipping Hammer** | Removes thick slag crust after welding | Pointed and chisel ends |
| **Cylinder Trolley** | Safe transport and upright storage of gas cylinders | With safety chain |

### B. Allied Instruments in Gas Welding

| Allied Instrument | Function |
|-------------------|----------|
| **Oxygen Cylinder** (black body, white shoulder) | Stores O₂ at 150 bar pressure |
| **Acetylene Cylinder** (maroon body) | Stores C₂H₂ dissolved in acetone |
| **Pressure Regulator** | Reduces cylinder pressure to working pressure (typically 0.2–0.7 bar for O₂, 0.05–0.7 bar for C₂H₂) |
| **Gas Hose** | Twin-bore rubber hose (red for fuel, blue/black for O₂), 5–10 m long |
| **Flashback Arrestor** | Prevents flame propagation back into cylinder (one each for O₂ and C₂H₂) |
| **Welding Torch** | Mixes gases in correct ratio and produces flame; has interchangeable nozzles (size 0 to 25) |
| **Spark Lighter / Friction Striker** | Safe ignition source |
| **Welding Goggles** | Shade 4–6 filter for gas welding |
| **Tip Cleaner / Nozzle Cleaner** | Cleans carbon deposits from nozzle |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes all **KTU high-yield formulas** associated with this experiment:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Where Used |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|------------|
| 1 | **Heat Input** | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S}$ | kJ/mm | All arc welding processes |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate** | $D = \dfrac{W}{t}$ | kg/h | SMAW, GMAW |
| 3 | **Power Input** | $P = V \cdot I$ | W or kW | All arc processes |
| 4 | **Travel Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L}{t}$ | mm/min | All welding |
| 5 | **Joule's Heat** | $H = I^{2} R t$ | J | Resistance welding |
| 6 | **Energy per Nugget (Spot)** | $E = \int V I \, dt$ | J or kVA·s | Spot welding |
| 7 | **Neutral Flame Ratio** | $O_{2} : C_{2}H_{2} \approx 1.1 : 1$ | by volume | Gas welding |
| 8 | **Electrode Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{W_{deposit}}{W_{electrode\,consumed}} \times 100$ | % | SMAW |
| 9 | **Acetylene Yield** | $C_{2}H_{2}$ from CaC₂ + 2H₂O → C₂H₂ + Ca(OH)₂ | kg/kg | Generation plant |
| 10 | **Electrode Coating Factor** | $\rho = \dfrac{W_{coating}}{W_{core\,wire}}$ | ratio | SMAW electrodes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Convention:** For Heat Input, current $I$ is in **Amperes**, voltage $V$ is in **Volts**, travel speed $S$ is in **mm/min**, and 60 converts seconds to minutes. Result is in **kJ/mm**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Spot welding for car bodies (≈ 4000–5000 spot welds per car); MIG/MAG for chassis; laser welding for gear blanks.
- **Shipbuilding:** SAW for thick plate (up to 50 mm single pass); flux-cored arc welding (FCAW) for outdoor work.
- **Aerospace:** Electron Beam Welding for turbine discs; Plasma Arc Welding for thin-wall tubes; Friction Stir Welding for aluminium fuselage panels.
- **Pipelines (oil & gas):** Shielded Metal Arc Welding (cellulosic E6010/E7010) for cross-country pipelines.
- **Pressure vessels & boilers:** SMAW + GMAW combination welds.
- **Microelectronics:** Ultrasonic welding for wire bonding in chips; Laser welding for battery tabs.
- **Tool manufacturing:** Friction welding of HSS tools to mild steel shanks — saves expensive HSS material.
