# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input Formula

The **Heat Input** is the most fundamental quantity that determines weld quality, mechanical properties, and distortion. KTU frequently asks to derive or apply this formula.

### Step 1 — Start with Electrical Energy Definition

The electrical energy supplied by the arc in time $t$ is:

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

where:
- $V$ = Arc voltage in **Volts (V)**
- $I$ = Welding current in **Amperes (A)**
- $t$ = Time of arc in **seconds (s)**
- $E$ = Energy in **Joules (J)**

### Step 2 — Convert to Heat Input per Unit Length

The welding arc travels along the joint. We want the energy deposited **per unit length of weld** to make it independent of travel speed. Travel speed $S$ is in mm/min, so time per mm is:

$$\dfrac{t}{L} = \dfrac{1}{S_{\,(\text{mm/min})}} \cdot 60 \;\;\text{(seconds per mm)}$$

Therefore energy per mm of weld:

$$\dfrac{E}{L} = V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{60}{S}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Joules/mm to kiloJoules/mm

$$\boxed{H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S}}$$

with $H$ in **kJ/mm**, $V$ in **V**, $I$ in **A**, and $S$ in **mm/min**.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

A welder uses SMAW with $V = 25$ V, $I = 200$ A, and travel speed $S = 200$ mm/min. Find the heat input.

**Solution:**

$$H = \dfrac{25 \times 200 \times 60}{1000 \times 200}$$

Simplify numerator and denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 25 \times 200 \times 60 \\
&= 5000 \times 60 \\
&= 300{,}000 \;\text{J/mm} \\[4pt]
\text{Denominator} &= 1000 \times 200 \\
&= 200{,}000 \\[4pt]
H &= \dfrac{300{,}000}{200{,}000} = 1.5 \;\text{kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** $1.5$ kJ/mm is a typical mild-steel SMAW heat input. Values above ~2.0 kJ/mm risk grain coarsening in carbon steel.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Heat Generated in Resistance Welding

Resistance welding uses **Joule heating** at the interface.

### Step 1 — Joule's Law

The heat generated when a current $I$ flows through a resistance $R$ for time $t$ is:

$$H = I^{2} R t$$

### Step 2 — Effective Resistance

In resistance welding, the **total resistance** $R$ at the joint is the sum of:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= 2R_{e} + 2R_{w} + R_{c} \\
\end{aligned}$$

where:
- $R_{e}$ = resistance of one electrode
- $R_{w}$ = resistance of one workpiece
- $R_{c}$ = contact resistance at the faying surface

### Step 3 — Total Heat Generated

$$\boxed{H = I^{2}\,(2R_{e} + 2R_{w} + R_{c})\,t}$$

Units: $I$ in **A**, $R$ in **Ohms (Ω)**, $t$ in **seconds (s)** → $H$ in **Joules (J)**.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical

A spot welder passes $I = 8000$ A for $t = 0.2$ s through a stack-up with $R = 200 \,\mu\Omega = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ Ω. Find the heat generated.

$$\begin{aligned}
H &= I^{2} R t \\
&= (8000)^{2} \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2 \\
&= 64{,}000{,}000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.2 \\
&= 64 \times 10^{6} \times 4 \times 10^{-5} \\
&= 2560 \;\text{J}
\end{aligned}$$

This ~2.56 kJ melts a small nugget of about 4–6 mm diameter in 0.2 seconds — exactly the regime used in automobile sheet-metal spot welding.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Study of Welding Processes

This is the **single most important summary table** for the KTU viva. Examiners frequently ask the student to compare any two welding processes.

| Parameter | SMAW | GMAW/MIG | GTAW/TIG | OAW | Spot Welding | Friction Welding |
|-----------|------|----------|----------|-----|--------------|-------------------|
| **Energy Source** | Electric arc | Continuous wire arc | Non-consumable arc | Combustion flame | Resistance heat | Mechanical friction |
| **Filler** | Coated electrode | Continuous wire | Separate rod (optional) | Separate rod | None (autogenous) | None |
| **Shielding** | Flux coating | Inert/active gas | Inert gas (Ar) | Combustion gases | None required | None required |
| **Current Type** | AC or DC | DCEP mostly | AC (Al) / DCEN (steel) | N/A | AC (low voltage, high I) | N/A |
| **Current Range** | 50–500 A | 50–600 A | 5–500 A | N/A | 5000–20000 A | N/A |
| **Voltage** | 18–35 V | 15–35 V | 10–35 V | N/A | <10 V | N/A |
| **Heat Input** | 0.5–3.0 kJ/mm | 0.3–2.0 kJ/mm | 0.2–1.5 kJ/mm | 1.0–4.0 kJ/mm | <0.1 kJ/nugget | Negligible |
| **Weld Quality** | Good | Very Good | Excellent | Moderate | Good for sheet | Excellent for round bars |
| **Skill Level** | Moderate | Moderate–High | High | Moderate | Low (automated) | Low (automated) |
| **Thickness Range** | 1.5–40 mm | 0.6–25 mm | 0.1–6 mm | 0.5–6 mm | 0.5–3 mm | 5–100 mm |
| **Outdoor Suitability** | Excellent | Poor (wind) | Poor (wind) | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate | High | Low | High (machine) | Moderate |
| **Distortion** | Moderate | Low | Very Low | High | Very Low | Negligible |

| Electrode Holder | SMAW, GTAW | Brass/copper jaws, insulated handle | High (electric shock) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Schematic of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW) Setup

The following block-level functional flow shows the energy and material path during SMAW:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Power Source<br/>Transformer or Rectifier<br/>AC or DC 80 to 400 A"] --> B["Welding Cable<br/>70 sq mm copper"]
    B --> C["Electrode Holder<br/>Insulated brass body"]
    C --> D["Consumable Electrode<br/>Flux coated mild steel"]
    D --> E["Electric Arc<br/>4000 to 7000 deg C"]
    E --> F["Weld Pool and Base Metal<br/>Molten fusion zone"]
    F --> G["Flux Decomposition<br/>CO2 and H2 shield gas"]
    G --> H["Slag Crust<br/>Chips off after cooling"]
    F --> I["Solidified Weld Bead<br/>Final joint"]
    J["Earth Clamp on Workpiece"] --> A
    K["Base Metal Plate A"] --> F
    L["Base Metal Plate B"] --> F
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **power source** delivers regulated current at the dial-set value.
- Current flows through the cable to the electrode holder and strikes an arc with the workpiece.
- The arc melts both the electrode core wire (filler) and the base metal.
- The **flux coating** simultaneously shields the pool and forms protective slag.
- The earth clamp returns current to the source, completing the circuit.

---

## 4.2 Schematic of Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding (OAW) Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Oxygen Cylinder<br/>150 bar black"] --> B["Oxygen Regulator<br/>0 to 10 bar output"]
    C["Acetylene Cylinder<br/>Dissolved in acetone"] --> D["Acetylene Regulator<br/>0 to 1.5 bar output"]
    B --> E["Flashback Arrestor O2"]
    D --> F["Flashback Arrestor C2H2"]
    E --> G["Twin Hose<br/>Blue for O2 and Red for fuel"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Welding Torch<br/>Mixing chamber"]
    H --> I["Nozzle<br/>Size 0 to 25"]
    I --> J["Neutral Flame<br/>3200 deg C"]
    J --> K["Weld Pool<br/>Base metal melted"]
    K --> L["Solidified Joint"]
    M["Spark Lighter"] --> J
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Each cylinder has its **own regulator and flashback arrestor** — never share.
- The **twin hose** carries gases to the torch where they mix in a controlled ratio.
- The **spark lighter** ignites the mixture safely — never use a match.
- The **flame type** (neutral/carburizing/oxidizing) is set by adjusting the acetylene valve on the torch.

---

## 4.3 Schematic of Resistance Spot Welding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Welding Transformer<br/>Low voltage high current"] --> B["Upper Electrode<br/>Water cooled copper"]
    A --> C["Lower Electrode<br/>Water cooled copper"]
    B --> D["Top Sheet<br/>Workpiece 1"]
    D --> E["Interface Contact<br/>Highest resistance"]
    E --> F["Bottom Sheet<br/>Workpiece 2"]
    F --> C
    G["Electrode Force<br/>Pneumatic or mechanical"] --> B
    H["Controller<br/>Weld time and current"] --> A
    H --> G
    E --> I["Weld Nugget<br/>Solidified cast structure"]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The transformer steps down mains voltage to ~5–10 V while increasing current to thousands of amps.
- **Electrode force** is applied simultaneously with current to forge the nugget.
- Cooling water circulates through the copper electrodes to prevent overheating.
- The **controller** precisely times current flow (typically 0.1–0.5 s).

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the principle of oxy-acetylene gas welding with a neat sketch and state the three types of flames.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Oxy-acetylene gas welding (OAW) uses the combustion of **acetylene (C₂H₂)** with **oxygen (O₂)** to produce a flame with a temperature of about **3200 °C**, which is sufficient to melt most commercial metals and alloys.

**Working:** Oxygen and acetylene are supplied from separate cylinders at controlled pressure through regulators. They flow through twin hoses to a **welding torch** where they mix in the **mixing chamber** and emerge from the nozzle where they are ignited by a spark lighter.

**Three Types of Flames:**

1. **Neutral Flame** — O₂ : C₂H₂ ≈ 1.1 : 1 by volume; has a clearly defined inner cone and feather; used for mild steel, stainless steel, copper.
2. **Carburizing (Reducing) Flame** — Excess acetylene; the inner cone is surrounded by a long feathered zone; used for hardfacing, nickel alloys, and non-ferrous metals.
3. **Oxidizing Flame** — Excess oxygen; inner cone is short and sharp; used for brass and bronze where a slightly oxidizing condition is desired.

## 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A

### Question A (a) — SMAW Principle, Sketch, Advantages, Limitations

**Principle (2 marks):**
In SMAW, an electric arc is struck between a **flux-coated consumable electrode** and the **workpiece**. The intense heat of the arc (about 4000–7000 °C) melts the electrode core wire and the parent metal, forming a molten weld pool that solidifies to produce the joint. The flux coating decomposes to produce a shielding gas envelope and a molten slag that protects the weld from atmospheric contamination.

**Neat Sketch (2 marks):** *[Student must draw the SMAW setup showing power source, electrode holder, coated electrode, arc, weld pool, flux shielding gas envelope, slag, workpiece, and earth clamp.]*

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Simple, portable, and inexpensive equipment.
2. Can be used outdoors as slag protects the weld from wind.
3. Suitable for a wide range of metals and thicknesses.
4. No external shielding gas required.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. Lower deposition rate compared to GMAW/SAW.
2. Slag must be removed after each pass.
3. Not suitable for reactive metals like titanium, aluminium.
4. Skilled operator required for consistent quality.

---

### Question A (b) — Oxy-Acetylene Pressure Regulator

**Construction (3 marks):**
A pressure regulator consists of:
- A **high-pressure chamber** connected to the cylinder valve.
- A **low-pressure chamber** connected to the hose leading to the torch.
- A **diaphragm** (brass or rubber) that senses the low-pressure side.
- A **spring-loaded adjusting screw** to set the desired delivery pressure.
- **Two pressure gauges** — one showing cylinder pressure, the other showing working/delivery pressure.
- A **safety relief valve** to prevent over-pressurization.

**Working (3 marks):**
When the cylinder valve is opened, high-pressure gas (e.g., oxygen at 150 bar) enters the high-pressure chamber. The operator turns the adjusting screw which compresses a spring that pushes the diaphragm downward, opening the main valve. Gas flows from high-pressure to low-pressure chamber until the low-pressure side reaches the set value. The diaphragm then moves up and closes the valve automatically — maintaining a constant delivery pressure regardless of cylinder pressure drop.

**Two Main Functions (1 mark):**
1. To **reduce** the high cylinder pressure to a safe, usable working pressure.
2. To **maintain a constant delivery pressure** even as the cylinder pressure falls during use.

---

## 5.4 Model Solutions for Question B

### Question B (a) — GMAW vs GTAW Comparison

| Parameter | GMAW (MIG/MAG) | GTAW (TIG) |
|-----------|----------------|------------|
| Electrode type | Continuous consumable wire | Non-consumable tungsten |
| Filler metal | The electrode itself | Separate filler rod added manually |
| Shielding gas | Argon (MIG) or Ar+CO₂ (MAG) | Pure argon or Ar+He |
| Metal transfer | Spray, globular, short-circuit | Only melt and flow (no transfer) |
| Current range | 50–600 A | 5–500 A |
| Heat input | Higher | Lower, more precise |
| Operator skill | Moderate | High |
| Typical thickness | 0.6–25 mm | 0.1–6 mm |

**Applications of GMAW (1 mark):** Automobile chassis, structural fabrication, pressure vessels, shipbuilding.

**Applications of GTAW (1 mark):** Aerospace stainless-steel components, aluminium alloys, root passes in pipelines, thin sheet fabrication.

---

### Question B (b) — Friction Welding Principle and Advantages

**Principle (3 marks):**
Friction welding is a solid-state welding process in which one component is rotated at high speed while being pressed against a stationary component. The **mechanical friction** at the interface generates localized heat (without any external heat source), raising the temperature to the plastic range of the material. Once sufficient plastic state is reached, rotation is stopped and an **upset/forge force** is applied to complete the bond. The interface becomes a **metallurgically bonded joint** without any melting.

**Sketch (2 marks):** *[Student should show: rotating workpiece, stationary workpiece, axial pressure, friction interface, flash ring around the joint.]*

**Advantages over fusion welding (2 marks):**
1. No melting — therefore no casting defects, porosity, or slag inclusions.
2. No shielding gas or filler material required — lower cost.
3. Excellent joint strength — often stronger than the parent metal.
4. Can join dissimilar metals (e.g., aluminium to steel, HSS to mild steel).
5. Environmentally clean — no fumes, UV, or spatter.

---

## 5.5 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps — Avoid these mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing allied instrument vs. primary equipment** — Transformer/rector is *not* an allied instrument; it is the power source. Allied instruments are auxiliary tools only.
> 2. **Wrong color codes** — Oxygen cylinder is **black with white shoulder** (not blue). Argon is peacock blue. Memorize via IS 4379.
> 3. **Incorrect flame ratio** — Neutral flame is O₂ : C₂H₂ = **1.1 : 1** by volume, not 1 : 1. Many students write 1 : 1 and lose 0.5 marks.
> 4. **Spot welding confusion** — Spot welding is **resistance** welding, *not* arc welding. Examiners frequently trick students with this.
> 5. **Heat Input units** — Must be in **kJ/mm**, not J/mm. Forgetting the factor of 1000 loses the unit mark.
> 6. **Polarity direction** — DCEP means electrode is connected to **positive** terminal of DC source; DCEN means electrode is connected to **negative**. Many students reverse these.
> 7. **Skipping sketches** — A "neat sketch" carries **2–3 marks** in most KTU questions. A blank space = automatic deduction.

---

## 5.6 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Study of Different Types of Welding and Allied Instruments**

### Welding Process Knowledge Points
- ☐ Welding = permanent joining by coalescence (with or without heat/pressure/filler).
- ☐ Three families: **Fusion, Solid-State, Brazing/Soldering**.
- ☐ **SMAW** uses coated electrode, slag-forming flux, suitable outdoors.
- ☐ **GMAW/MIG** uses continuous wire + shielding gas (Ar for MIG, Ar+CO₂ for MAG).
- ☐ **GTAW/TIG** uses non-consumable tungsten + Argon; AC for aluminium, DCEN for steel.
- ☐ **SAW** uses granular flux blanket; arc is invisible; high deposition.
- ☐ **OAW** uses C₂H₂ + O₂ flame (3200 °C); neutral, carburizing, oxidizing flames.
- ☐ **Spot welding** uses resistance heating (I²R) under electrode force.
- ☐ **Friction welding** is solid-state, no melting, joins dissimilar metals.
- ☐ **Laser/EBW** are high-energy-density beam processes.

### Allied Instrument Knowledge Points
- ☐ Electrode holder, earth clamp, welding cable — current carrying.
- ☐ Helmet, gloves, apron, goggles — personal protective equipment (PPE).
- ☐ Wire brush, chipping hammer — post-weld cleaning.
- ☐ Pressure regulators — reduce cylinder pressure to working pressure.
- ☐ Flashback arrestor — safety device for gas cylinders.
- ☐ Spark lighter — safe ignition, never use matches.
- ☐ **Cylinder color codes:** Oxygen → black/white; Acetylene → maroon; Argon → peacock blue.

### Critical Formulas to Remember
- ☐ $H = \dfrac{V I \cdot 60}{1000 \, S}$ kJ/mm
- ☐ $H = I^{2} R t$ J (Resistance welding)
- ☐ Neutral flame ratio: O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1.1 : 1

### Practical Lab Skills
- ☐ Identify SMAW/GMAW/GTAW machines and torches by sight.
- ☐ Connect regulator to cylinder safely (open valve slowly, crack before gauge).
- ☐ Strike and maintain an arc on a scrap plate.
- ☐ Select correct electrode diameter based on plate thickness (rule of thumb: electrode dia ≈ plate thickness for mild steel).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prepare a Single V Butt Joint Using Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Prepare a Single V Butt Joint Using Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Single V Butt Joint** is a type of fusion welding joint configuration in which the edges of two plates to be joined are beveled on one side only to form a V-shaped groove with an included angle (typically **60° ± 5°**), as per **IS 9595 / AWS D1.1** standards. The plates are placed edge-to-edge with a controlled **root gap** and **root face**, and the groove is filled with weld metal through a series of arc welding passes using a consumable electrode.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCNSL308)** laboratory curriculum, the experiment mandates the student to:
- Prepare a mild steel plate with a Single V-groove geometry.
- Perform **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** to fabricate a butt joint.
- Inspect the joint for surface defects (visual inspection) and measure key weld bead parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A Single V Butt Joint is the most fundamental *open-groove* butt joint used in plate fabrication. It is selected over a *square butt joint* when the plate thickness exceeds **6 mm**, because the V-groove provides adequate access for the arc to reach the root of the joint, ensuring full penetration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to glue two thick pieces of wood together. If you just press their flat edges together, the glue line is very thin and weak. Now, instead of leaving them flat, you chisel a triangular groove (a "V") into each piece. This creates a *channel* that can hold a lot more glue, and the glue locks into the groove mechanically, forming a much stronger bond.

A **Single V Butt Joint** is the metalworking equivalent:
- The **two plates** are the wooden pieces.
- The **chiseled V-groove** is the channel that holds molten weld metal (the "glue").
- The **welding arc** melts the filler electrode and the parent metal, allowing them to fuse into one continuous piece.
- As the weld cools, it becomes mechanically interlocked with the V-groove walls, making the joint stronger than the parent metal itself in many cases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Single" V?** The word *Single* indicates the V-groove is cut on only **one side** of the joint. If both sides were beveled, it would be a *Double V* or *X-groove* butt joint, used for thicker plates (typically > 20 mm) to reduce distortion and distribute heat input.

## 1.3 Governing Standards & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Governing Code |
|---|---|---|
| Included Groove Angle | **60°** (range 60°–70°) | IS 9595:1996 |
| Root Face (Land) | **1.5 mm – 2 mm** | AWS D1.1 |
| Root Gap | **1.5 mm – 2.5 mm** | IS 9595 |
| Plate Thickness Range | **6 mm – 20 mm** | Practical Lab Standard |
| Weld Symbol (Single V) | ⌵ (single V with backing) | AWS A2.4 |

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Tip:** Always verify the groove angle with a **bevel protector** before striking the arc. An incorrect angle (< 50° or > 80°) will result in incomplete fusion or excessive filler metal consumption.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Welding Process

The fabrication of a Single V Butt Joint using arc welding proceeds through the following structured logical stages:

### Stage 1: Joint Preparation
- Two **mild steel (MS) plates** of dimension typically **150 mm × 75 mm × 10 mm** are marked using a **scriber** and **steel rule**.
- The edges are machine-cut (or hand-cut using an oxy-acetylene torch in a lab setting) to form a **30° bevel** on each plate, creating a **60° included angle** V-groove.
- A **root face** of 1.5–2 mm is retained to prevent burn-through.
- A **root gap** of 1.5–2.5 mm is set using a **spacer gauge** to ensure full penetration.

### Stage 2: Tack Welding
- Small tack welds (≈ 10 mm long) are placed at both ends of the joint and at the mid-span to hold the plates in alignment.
- Tacks prevent the plates from shifting due to thermal expansion during the root pass.

### Stage 3: Root Pass (First Run)
- The **root pass** is the most critical pass because it must fuse completely with the root of the joint.
- A **small-diameter electrode** (e.g., **2.5 mm E6013**) is used to maintain arc control.
- The arc is struck at the leading edge of the tack weld, and a **stringer bead** is deposited along the joint line.

### Stage 4: Filler and Capping Passes
- Subsequent passes use a **larger electrode** (e.g., **3.15 mm E6013**).
- Each pass is allowed to cool to below **250 °C (interpass temperature)** before the next is deposited to prevent HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) embrittlement.
- The final capping pass is built slightly above the plate surface and is dressed (ground flush) to a smooth crown.

### Stage 5: Inspection
- **Visual inspection** for undercuts, porosity, cracks, and spatter.
- **Dimensional check** of bead width, reinforcement height, and leg length.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Practical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | Heat Input | **kJ/mm** | Controls penetration & distortion |
| $Q = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Total Heat Generated | **Joules (J)** | Energy supplied to workpiece |
| $D_{\text{weld}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot \rho \cdot c \cdot (T_m - T_0)}$ | Weld Pool Area Estimate | **mm²** | Thermal modeling |
| $V_{\text{deposit}} = 0.785 \cdot d^2 \cdot L$ | Volume of Weld Bead | **mm³** | Electrode consumption |
| $W_{\text{bead}} = 0.785 \cdot d^2 + \dfrac{d \cdot P}{2}$ | Theoretical Bead Width | **mm** | Bead geometry check |
| $\eta_{\text{dep}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{theoretical}}}$ | Deposition Efficiency | **%** | Cost & quality control |
| $A_{\text{groove}} = t \cdot g + t^2 \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$ | Cross-sectional Area of V-groove | **mm²** | Filler metal volume |
| $P_{\text{penetration}} = \dfrac{K \cdot I}{v^{0.7}}$ | Penetration Depth (empirical) | **mm** | Estimating root fusion |

**Where:**
- $\eta$ = Arc efficiency (≈ **0.75** for SMAW, **0.85** for GMAW)
- $V$ = Arc voltage (**V**)
- $I$ = Welding current (**A**)
- $S$ = Travel speed (**mm/min**)
- $d$ = Electrode diameter (**mm**)
- $L$ = Length of bead (**mm**)
- $t$ = Plate thickness (**mm**)
- $g$ = Root gap (**mm**)
- $\theta$ = Groove included angle (degrees)
- $T_m$ = Melting temperature (**°C**)
- $T_0$ = Ambient temperature (**°C**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The arc efficiency factor $\eta$ converts electrical energy to useful heat at the weld pool. For **SMAW**, $\eta \approx \mathbf{0.70\text{–}0.85}$. Without multiplying by $\eta$, the calculated heat input will overestimate actual fusion energy by 15–30%.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Single V Butt Joint is the **workhorse of structural fabrication**. It is used in:
- **Pressure vessel** shells (ASME Section VIII).
- **Ship hull** plate assembly.
- **Bridge girder** flange-to-web connections.
- **Storage tank** shell courses.
- **Earthmoving equipment** booms and chassis.

Engineers select the Single V geometry because it balances **weld metal volume**, **heat input**, and **accessibility** for plates of moderate thickness, ensuring a sound joint without the complexity of a double-sided groove.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Input Calculation for SMAW

The heat input is the single most important parameter in arc welding process control. It governs penetration, distortion, and mechanical properties of the HAZ. We will derive it from fundamental electrical energy principles.

### Step 1: Electrical Energy Supplied to the Arc

The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the welding arc is the product of arc voltage and welding current.

$$P = V \cdot I$$

Here, $P$ is the electrical power in **watts (W)**, $V$ is the arc voltage in **volts (V)**, and $I$ is the welding current in **amperes (A)**.

### Step 2: Total Energy Over the Welding Time

The total energy $E$ supplied over a time period $t$ (in seconds) is the integral of power over time.

$$E = P \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

Here, $E$ has units of **watt-seconds**, which is equivalent to **joules (J)**.

### Step 3: Accounting for Arc Efficiency

Not all electrical energy is converted to useful heat at the weld pool. Some energy is lost as **radiation**, **convection to surrounding air**, and **conduction into the electrode holder**. The arc efficiency $\eta$ (dimensionless, $0 < \eta < 1$) accounts for this loss.

$$E_{\text{effective}} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t$$

For SMAW, $\eta$ is taken as **0.75** under standard KTU lab conditions.

### Step 4: Converting Time to Travel Speed

In continuous welding, the time $t$ is related to the weld length $L$ and travel speed $S$ as follows:

$$t = \dfrac{L}{S}$$

Here, $L$ is the weld length in **mm** and $S$ is the travel speed in **mm/s** (or mm/min after unit conversion).

### Step 5: Defining Heat Input per Unit Length

To make the parameter independent of weld length, we define heat input $H$ as energy per unit length of weld:

$$H = \dfrac{E_{\text{effective}}}{L}$$

### Step 6: Final Heat Input Equation

Substituting Step 4 into Step 3, and then into Step 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot t}{L} \\
  &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{S}\right)}{L} \\
  &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

To obtain practical units of **kJ/mm**, we multiply by a conversion factor:

$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **60** converts minutes to seconds, and **1000** converts joules to kilojoules when $S$ is in mm/min. This is the formula you will use in KTU lab record calculations.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A Single V Butt Joint is to be prepared on 10 mm MS plate using SMAW. The welding parameters recorded are:
- Arc voltage: $V = 22 \text{ V}$
- Welding current: $I = 140 \text{ A}$
- Travel speed: $S = 120 \text{ mm/min}$
- Arc efficiency for SMAW: $\eta = 0.75$

**Calculate the heat input and comment on whether it is within the recommended range for mild steel.**

### Step 1: Identify Given Data
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 22 \text{ V} \\
I &= 140 \text{ A} \\
S &= 120 \text{ mm/min} \\
\eta &= 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Heat Input Formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\
  &= \dfrac{0.75 \cdot 22 \cdot 140 \cdot 60}{120 \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Numerator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Numerator} &= 0.75 \cdot 22 \cdot 140 \cdot 60 \\
                 &= 0.75 \cdot 22 \cdot 8400 \\
                 &= 0.75 \cdot 184800 \\
                 &= 138600
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Denominator

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Denominator} &= 120 \cdot 1000 = 120000
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{138600}{120000} \\
  &= 1.155 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Interpretation

The recommended heat input for 10 mm MS plate SMAW is **1.0 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. Our value of **1.155 kJ/mm** is within the safe range, ensuring good penetration without excessive HAZ softening.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: V-Groove Cross-Section Calculation

**Problem:** Compute the cross-sectional area of a Single V-groove on a 10 mm plate with a 60° included angle and 2 mm root gap.

### Step 1: Geometric Setup

The V-groove is a symmetric trapezoid-like section. The total area consists of the **root gap rectangle** plus the **two triangular bevels**.

### Step 2: Area of Root Gap

$$
A_{\text{gap}} = t \cdot g = 10 \cdot 2 = 20 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 3: Half-Angle of the V

$$
\dfrac{\theta}{2} = \dfrac{60°}{2} = 30°
$$

### Step 4: Depth of Bevel

The bevel depth equals the plate thickness minus the root face (assume 2 mm root face):

$$
h = t - f = 10 - 2 = 8 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 5: Width of Each Bevel (Horizontal Projection)

$$
w = h \cdot \tan\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = 8 \cdot \tan(30°) = 8 \cdot 0.5774 = 4.619 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 6: Area of One Triangular Bevel

$$
A_{\text{tri}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot w \cdot h = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4.619 \cdot 8 = 18.476 \text{ mm}^2
$$

### Step 7: Total V-Groove Area (Two Bevels + Root Gap)

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= 2 \cdot A_{\text{tri}} + A_{\text{gap}} \\
                 &= 2 \cdot 18.476 + 20 \\
                 &= 36.952 + 20 \\
                 &= 56.952 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> This area represents the *minimum* volume of weld metal required per unit length (mm² × mm = mm³ of filler). Multiplying by **7.85 g/cm³** (density of steel) gives the mass of filler per meter of weld — essential for electrode consumption costing.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram: Single V Butt Joint Fabrication

The following flowchart maps the sequential operations performed during the lab experiment, from material receipt to final inspection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive MS Plates] --> B[Mark Dimensions: 150x75x10 mm]
    B --> C[Cut Plates to Size using Hacksaw/Oxy Torch]
    C --> D[Bevel Edges to Form 30 deg Bevel]
    D --> E[Verify Groove Angle with Bevel Protector]
    E --> F[Set Root Gap 2 mm using Spacer Gauge]
    F --> G[Tack Weld at Both Ends and Center]
    G --> H{Is Alignment OK?}
    H -- No --> I[Adjust using Clamps and Re-tack]
    I --> G
    H -- Yes --> J[Select Electrode: 2.5 mm E6013]
    J --> K[Set Current: 80-100 A for Root Pass]
    K --> L[Strike Arc and Deposit Root Pass]
    L --> M[Clean Slag using Wire Brush and Chipping Hammer]
    M --> N[Change Electrode to 3.15 mm E6013]
    N --> O[Deposit Filler Passes with Interpass Cleaning]
    O --> P[Allow Interpass Cooling below 250 deg C]
    P --> Q{All Passes Complete?}
    Q -- No --> O
    Q -- Yes --> R[Deposit Capping Pass with Slight Reinforcement]
    R --> S[Final Slag Removal and Wire Brushing]
    S --> T[Visual Inspection for Defects]
    T --> U[Measure Bead Geometry: Width, Height, Leg Length]
    U --> V[Record Results in Lab Manual]
    V --> W[End: Submit Specimen for Evaluation]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Weld Joint Geometry & Critical Parameters

This diagram illustrates the geometric nomenclature of a Single V Butt Joint that students must label in their KTU lab records and theory exams.

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 5
    BlockA["Plate Top Surface"]:1
    BlockB["Capping Bead Crown"]:1
    BlockC["Filler Pass 2"]:1
    BlockD["Filler Pass 1"]:1
    BlockE["Root Pass"]:1
    BlockF["Root Face 1.5 to 2 mm"]:1
    BlockG["Root Gap 1.5 to 2.5 mm"]:1
    BlockH["HAZ Heat Affected Zone"]:1
    BlockI["Parent Metal MS Plate"]:1
    BlockJ["Groove Angle 60 deg"]:1
    BlockK["Bevel Angle 30 deg each side"]:1
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: SMAW Equipment Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DC["DC Power Source Rectifier"]
        DC1[Input 415 V AC 3 Phase]
        DC2[Transformer Step Down]
        DC3[Rectifier Bridge Diodes]
        DC4[Output DC Current Adjustable]
    end
    subgraph CA["Cables and Accessories"]
        CA1[Electrode Holder]
        CA2[Electrode 2.5/3.15 mm E6013]
        CA3[Work Cable with Clamp]
    end
    subgraph WP["Workpiece Assembly"]
        WP1[MS Plate 1 with V Groove]
        WP2[MS Plate 2 with V Groove]
        WP3[Tack Welds at Ends]
        WP4[Ground Plate on Welding Table]
    end
    subgraph OP["Operator Station"]
        OP1[Welding Helmet with Filter Lens]
        OP2[Leather Gloves and Apron]
        OP3[Chipping Hammer]
        OP4[Wire Brush]
    end
    DC4 --> CA1
    CA1 --> CA2
    CA2 -->|Arc 4000 deg C| WP1
    WP1 --> WP2
    WP2 --> WP3
    WP3 --> CA3
    CA3 --> DC4
    OP1 --> CA2
    OP3 --> WP1
    OP4 --> WP1
```

## 4.4 Defect Classification Matrix (Welding Imperfections)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Def["Welding Defects in Single V Butt Joint"] --> Surface["Surface Defects"]
    Def --> Internal["Internal Defects"]
    Def --> Dimensional["Dimensional Defects"]
    Surface --> S1["Undercut"]
    Surface --> S2["Spatter"]
    Surface --> S3["Crater Cracks"]
    Surface --> S4["Overlap"]
    Internal --> I1["Porosity"]
    Internal --> I2["Slag Inclusion"]
    Internal --> I3["Lack of Fusion"]
    Internal --> I4["Lack of Penetration"]
    Internal --> I5["Worm Holes"]
    Dimensional --> D1["Excess Reinforcement"]
    Dimensional --> D2["Insufficient Throat Thickness"]
    Dimensional --> D3["Misalignment of Plates"]
    Dimensional --> D4["Excessive Distortion"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Inspection Note:** Under the **KTU lab evaluation scheme**, the examiner will specifically look for **undercut, porosity, and lack of penetration** in your welded specimen. A specimen showing any of these defects will receive a maximum of **70% marks** in the lab record evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Single V Butt Joint. State any two advantages it has over a Square Butt Joint. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A **Single V Butt Joint** is a type of fusion welding joint in which the edges of two plates are beveled to form a V-shaped groove with an included angle of **60° ± 5°**, leaving a small root face and root gap. The groove is then filled with weld metal through successive arc welding passes.

**Two Advantages over Square Butt Joint:**
1. **Better penetration** is achieved because the arc can reach the root of the joint through the open V-groove, ensuring full fusion across the entire plate thickness.
2. **Lower filler metal consumption** for thicker plates, since the V-groove concentrates the weld metal in the load-bearing region rather than requiring a wide flat weld face.

---

### Q2. List any four welding defects commonly observed in a Single V Butt Joint. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Porosity** — Cavities formed due to trapped gas in the solidified weld metal.
2. **Lack of Penetration** — Failure of weld metal to extend through the joint thickness.
3. **Undercut** — A groove melted into the parent metal at the weld toe and not filled by weld metal.
4. **Slag Inclusion** — Non-metallic solid material trapped between weld passes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the procedure to prepare a Single V Butt Joint on 10 mm MS plate using SMAW. List the tools, equipment, and welding parameters used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Tools and Equipment Required:**

| S.No | Item | Specification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | MS Plates | 150 mm × 75 mm × 10 mm |
| 2 | Electrodes | E6013, ⌀ 2.5 mm (root), ⌀ 3.15 mm (filler/cap) |
| 3 | Welding Machine | SMAW rectifier, 300 A capacity |
| 4 | Bevel Protector | 0°–180° |
| 5 | Spacer Gauge | 2 mm thickness |
| 6 | Chipping Hammer | 200 g |
| 7 | Wire Brush | Stainless steel |
| 8 | Clamps and Fixtures | C-clamps, welding table |
| 9 | Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) | Helmet, gloves, apron |

**Procedure:**

1. **Marking and Cutting:** Mark the plate dimensions (150 × 75 mm) and cut to size using a hacksaw or band-saw.
2. **Edge Preparation:** Machine-bevel one edge of each plate to **30°**, leaving a **2 mm root face**, to produce a **60° included angle** V-groove.
3. **Cleaning:** Remove mill scale, rust, and oil from the bevel and adjacent surfaces using a wire brush and acetone.
4. **Assembly:** Place the plates on the welding table with a **2 mm root gap** maintained by a spacer gauge. Tack weld at both ends and at the mid-span (≈ 10 mm long tacks).
5. **Setting Parameters:** Set the welding current to **80–100 A** for the root pass using a 2.5 mm electrode.
6. **Root Pass:** Strike the arc at the leading edge of the starting tack. Use a **stringer bead** technique with a slight forward drag of **5°–15°**.
7. **Slag Removal:** After each pass, use the chipping hammer to remove slag, followed by wire brushing.
8. **Filler and Capping Passes:** Use a 3.15 mm electrode at **120–140 A**. Deposit 2–3 filler passes, allowing the weld to cool below **250 °C (interpass temperature)** between passes. Finish with a capping pass with a slight crown (1–2 mm reinforcement).
9. **Inspection:** Perform visual inspection and measure bead dimensions with a weld gauge.

**Sketch:**

```
         ┌──────────────┐
         │   Capping    │  ← Slight crown (1-2 mm)
         ├──────────────┤
         │  Filler Pass │
         ├──────────────┤
         │  Filler Pass │
         ├──────────────┤
         │   Root Pass  │  ← Critical fusion zone
    ═════╪══════════════╪═════  ← Plate top surface
         │  HAZ │Parent │
         │      │       │
    ╲     │      │       │     ╱
     ╲ 30°│      │     30°╱   ← Bevel angle
      ╲   │  2mm │     ╱
       ╲  │  Gap │    ╱
        ╲─┴──────┴──╱       ← Root face 2 mm
```

**Q (b)** Calculate the heat input for a Single V Butt Joint on 12 mm MS plate using the following SMAW parameters: Voltage = 24 V, Current = 160 A, Travel speed = 100 mm/min, Arc efficiency = 0.80. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= 24 \text{ V} \\
I &= 160 \text{ A} \\
S &= 100 \text{ mm/min} \\
\eta &= 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula:**
$$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$

**Substitution:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{0.80 \cdot 24 \cdot 160 \cdot 60}{100 \cdot 1000}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerator Calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Num} &= 0.80 \cdot 24 \cdot 160 \cdot 60 \\
           &= 0.80 \cdot 24 \cdot 9600 \\
           &= 0.80 \cdot 230400 \\
           &= 184320
\end{aligned}
$$

**Denominator Calculation:**
$$
\text{Den} = 100 \cdot 1000 = 100000
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= \dfrac{184320}{100000} \\
  &= 1.8432 \text{ kJ/mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The calculated heat input of **1.84 kJ/mm** is **slightly above** the recommended range of **1.0–1.5 kJ/mm** for 12 mm MS plate. The operator should increase the travel speed to **120 mm/min** (or reduce current to 130 A) to bring the heat input within the safe range, thereby avoiding excessive HAZ softening and distortion.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**Q (a)** With a neat sketch, describe the Single V-groove preparation. Explain the significance of groove angle, root face, and root gap. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Single V-Groove Preparation (Sketch):**

```
   Parent Metal
   ┌──────────────┐
   │              │
   │   ╲      ╱   │  ← Groove angle (θ = 60°)
   │    ╲    ╱    │
   │     ╲  ╱     │  ← Bevel (30° each side)
   │      ╲╱      │
   │   ←g→ │      │  ← Root gap (g = 1.5-2.5 mm)
   │   ┌──────┐   │
   │   │      │   │  ← Root face (f = 1.5-2 mm)
   │   └──────┘   │
   │              │
   └──────────────┘
```

**Significance of Key Parameters:**

| Parameter | Recommended Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Groove Angle (θ)** | **60°** | Provides adequate arc access to the root. Angles < 50° cause lack of penetration; angles > 70° waste filler metal. |
| **Root Face (f)** | **1.5–2 mm** | Prevents burn-through during the root pass by providing a metal barrier at the bottom. |
| **Root Gap (g)** | **1.5–2.5 mm** | Ensures full penetration by allowing the arc and molten metal to reach the root. Too small → lack of penetration; too large → excessive weld metal. |

**Q (b)** What are the common visual defects observed in a Single V Butt Joint? Explain any three in detail with causes and remedies. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The three most critical visual defects are explained below:

**1. Lack of Penetration**
- **Appearance:** The weld metal does not extend through the entire plate thickness; visible gap at the root.
- **Causes:** Insufficient root gap, low welding current, fast travel speed, incorrect electrode angle.
- **Remedies:** Increase root gap to 2–2.5 mm, increase current to 140 A, reduce travel speed to 100 mm/min, use a **root run with backing**.

**2. Porosity**
- **Appearance:** Small cavities or pinholes on the weld surface or within the bead.
- **Causes:** Contaminated base metal (oil, rust, moisture), damp electrodes, excessive arc length.
- **Remedies:** Clean plates with acetone, dry electrodes in a **rod oven at 150 °C for 1 hour**, maintain short arc (2–3 mm).

**3. Undercut**
- **Appearance:** A groove melted into the parent metal at the weld toe, not filled by weld metal.
- **Causes:** Excessive current, incorrect electrode angle (> 20° drag), fast travel speed.
- **Remedies:** Reduce current to recommended range, maintain **10°–15° drag angle**, reduce travel speed, use weaving technique for capping pass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Don't** confuse **Lack of Penetration** (incomplete root fusion) with **Lack of Fusion** (incomplete sidewall fusion). Examiners frequently award partial credit for correct identification.
> - **Don't** state heat input as simply $V \cdot I$ without the arc efficiency factor $\eta$. This is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU 2024 Scheme valuation.
> - **Don't** forget to state the **units** (kJ/mm) in your final answer. Unitless numerical answers lose **1 mark**.
> - **Don't** draw the V-groove with a 90° included angle — it must be 60°. This signals to the examiner that the student has not studied the standard.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- ✅ **Single V Butt Joint** is used for plate thickness **6–20 mm** with a **60° included groove angle**.
- ✅ **Root face = 1.5–2 mm**, **Root gap = 1.5–2.5 mm** (memorize these values).
- ✅ Standard lab plate size: **150 mm × 75 mm × 10 mm** mild steel.
- ✅ Electrode: **E6013** (rutile-coated, AC/DC compatible, most common in KTU labs).
- ✅ Root pass: **2.5 mm electrode at 80–100 A**; Filler/Cap: **3.15 mm at 120–140 A**.
- ✅ **Heat input formula:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ with units of **kJ/mm**.
- ✅ Arc efficiency $\eta$ for SMAW = **0.75** (commonly used value).
- ✅ Interpass temperature must stay below **250 °C** for MS plates.
- ✅ Tack welds must be **10 mm long** at both ends and the mid-span.
- ✅ Inspection tools: **Weld gauge** (bead dimensions), **bevel protector** (groove angle), **visual inspection** (defects).
- ✅ **Three most-tested defects:** Lack of penetration, porosity, and undercut.
- ✅ Visual inspection finds **surface defects**; **dye-penetrant test** finds sub-surface cracks (advanced topic).
- ✅ V-groove cross-section area = **$t \cdot g + t^2 \cdot \tan(\theta/2)$** (for filler volume calculation).
- ✅ The **capping pass** should leave a **1–2 mm reinforcement** above the plate surface; never grind flush before inspection.
- ✅ **Polarity:** Use **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** for E6013 root pass to achieve deeper penetration.
- ✅ **Safety PPE:** Welding helmet (shade #10–13), leather gloves, fire-resistant apron, and proper ventilation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prepare a Lap Joint Using Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Prepare a Lap Joint Using Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Lap Joint** is a type of joint in which the edges of two plates (or sheets) are overlapped over each other for a certain distance and then joined by welding along the edges of the overlapped region. When fabricated using the **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process — also called the **Manual Metal Arc Welding (MMAW)** or simply "stick electrode" process — the molten metal from the consumable electrode, together with flux decomposition products, fills the gap between the two overlapping plates and solidifies to form a **fillet weld** along the joint line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PCNSL308):**
> The experiment requires the student to *prepare* (i.e., cut, clean, tack, weld, and inspect) a lap joint specimen using the **SMAW / Arc Welding** process on mild steel plates, and to **report the procedure, parameters, and observations** in the lab record as per the Continuous Evaluation (CE) rubric of the course.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are pressing **two rectangular pieces of cardboard** together so that one lies on top of the other. To prevent them from sliding apart, you would staple them along the overlapped edge. **Arc welding does the same thing to metal plates** — but instead of staples, the welder uses an **electric arc** (a continuous, extremely hot electrical spark, around **6,000–7,000 °C**) to melt a filler rod (electrode) into a molten puddle that solidifies into a strong, permanent metal "staple" called a **fillet weld** along the joint edge.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The arc is the *heat source*, the electrode is the *filler material*, and the two plates are the *parent metal*. The molten metal from all three mixes and freezes as one continuous metallic bond.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Arc Temperature | **6,000 – 7,000 °C** (≈ 10× the melting point of steel) |
| Mild Steel Melting Point | **~1,500 °C** |
| Standard Plate Thickness (for lab) | **3 mm – 6 mm** |
| Standard Overlap Length | **3t to 5t** (where t = plate thickness) |
| Fillet Weld Leg Size (typical) | **4 mm – 6 mm** |
| Arc Efficiency (η) for MMAW | **0.70 – 0.85** (η ≈ 0.75 commonly used) |
| Standard Power Supply | **AC** or **DC (DCEP)** constant current |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Lap Joint and not a Butt Joint?**
> A lap joint is **easier to prepare** (no precise edge beveling required), tolerates **greater dimensional variation**, and is therefore the **preferred starting experiment** for trainee welders in any lab. It teaches bead deposition, travel-angle control, and slag removal without the complexity of root penetration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)

The SMAW process operates on the principle of **controlled electric arc generation between the electrode and the workpiece**:

1. The **electrode holder (stinger)** is connected to one terminal of the welding power source, and the **workpiece (plate)** to the other.
2. The welder strikes an arc by **scratch-start** or **tap-start** — momentarily touching the electrode tip to the plate and withdrawing it by **2 – 4 mm**.
3. The arc ionises the air gap, allowing current to flow through a **plasma column** at very high temperature.
4. Heat from the arc **melts the parent metal** at the weld zone and **consumes the electrode** (which acts as filler).
5. The **flux coating** on the electrode decomposes to release:
   - Shielding gases (CO₂, H₂) → protect the molten pool from atmospheric N₂ and O₂.
   - Slag (oxides, silicates) → floats on top of the weld pool, insulates the cooling bead, and slows cooling.
6. As the welder moves along the joint, the molten pool **solidifies behind the arc**, forming the weld bead.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Process Variable

| Variable | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| **Open Circuit Voltage (OCV)** | Provides the electrical potential to ionise the arc gap; typically 50–80 V AC. |
| **Welding Current (I)** | Governs **deposition rate** and **penetration**; too high → burn-through; too low → poor fusion (cold lap). |
| **Arc Voltage (V)** | Governs **arc length**; correct V = correct arc length = stable bead width. |
| **Travel Speed (S)** | Governs **bead size and heat input**; too fast → ropy/undercut bead; too slow → wide, overheated bead. |
| **Electrode Diameter (d)** | Determines the **current range** usable; rule of thumb: I_max ≈ 40 × d² (for MMAW). |
| **Electrode Polarity (DCEP/DCEN)** | DCEP gives **deep penetration** (most common for mild steel). |
| **Arc Efficiency (η)** | Fraction of arc energy that actually reaches the workpiece (not the surrounding atmosphere). |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for the KTU 2024 lab viva and end-semester descriptive questions on this experiment.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Typical Value (Mild Steel, 4 mm plate) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** |
| 2 | **Deposition Rate (D)** | $D = \dfrac{\text{mass of electrode consumed}}{\text{time}}$ | kg/h | **1.0 – 2.5 kg/h** |
| 3 | **Fillet Weld Throat (T)** | $T = 0.707 \times L$ (for equal-leg fillet) | mm | **≈ plate thickness t** for full-strength |
| 4 | **Minimum Weld Size** | $L_{min} \approx \sqrt{2} \cdot t$ only if $t \le 3$ mm; else per IS 9595 | mm | **4 mm** for 4–6 mm plate |
| 5 | **Overlap Length** | $l_{overlap} = 3t$ to $5t$ | mm | **12 – 25 mm** for 4 mm plate |
| 6 | **Electrode Current Rule** | $I_{max} \approx 40 \times d^{2}$ (for low-hydrogen rods) | A | For 3.15 mm rod → ≈ **130 A** |
| 7 | **Penetration (approx.)** | $P \approx \dfrac{\eta V I}{k \cdot v \cdot t}$ (qualitative only) | — | DCEP gives higher P |
| 8 | **Strength of Fillet Weld** | $F = 0.707 \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot L_{weld}$ | N | Used for design check |

Where:
- $\eta$ = arc transfer efficiency (dimensionless, typically 0.70–0.85 for MMAW).
- $V$ = arc voltage in Volts.
- $I$ = welding current in Amperes.
- $S$ = travel speed in mm/min.
- $60$ converts minutes to seconds; $1000$ converts Joules to kiloJoules.
- $L$ = leg length of the fillet weld in mm.
- $d$ = electrode diameter in mm.
- $L_{weld}$ = total length of the weld in mm.
- $\sigma_{allow}$ = allowable shear stress of weld metal (≈ 110 MPa for E6013 deposits per AWS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Heat Input:**
> Heat input directly controls the **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ) width**, **residual stress**, and **mechanical properties** of the joint. For low-carbon mild steel, a heat input in the range **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** is considered "controlled" and gives a fine-grained HAZ with good toughness. This is the single most-tracked metric in any modern welding procedure specification (WPS) — including the one you should record in your lab record.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Automotive chassis fabrication** (floor pans, side rails).
- **Sheet-metal enclosures** for electrical cabinets, control panels.
- **Storage tanks, silos, and ducting** in process industries.
- **Structural fabrication** of trusses, frames, and bracing.
- **Shipbuilding** for shell plating and stiffener attachment.
- **Repair and maintenance** work across all of the above (lap joints are the most commonly repaired joint in the field because they can be made on the existing structure without dismantling).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Equipment, Tools & Materials Required

**Equipment:**
- AC / DC arc welding machine (constant-current type) — minimum **200 A** rating.
- Electrode holder (stinger) with insulated handle.
- Earthing clamp and welding cable (work lead).
- Wire brush, chipping hammer, slag chisel.
- Personal Protective Equipment (PPE): welding helmet (shade 10–13), leather gloves, leather apron, safety shoes, UV goggles.

**Consumables:**
- Two mild steel plates, size approximately **100 mm × 50 mm × 4 mm** (length × width × thickness).
- Mild steel welding electrodes — **E6013** (rutile-coated, Ø 3.15 mm) — most common for student labs because it runs smoothly on both AC and DC.
- Filler rod can be E6013 / E7018 as per availability.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure (Exhaustive)

**Step 1 — Workplace Preparation**
- Inspect the welding area. Ensure the **fire extinguisher is within reach**, no flammable materials are within **5 metres**, and the **fume extractor is ON**.
- Lay a **fire-resistant mat** under the work area to catch hot spatter.

**Step 2 — Plate Preparation (Parent Metal)**
- Cut the two mild steel plates to size using a hacksaw or shear.
- Remove **mill scale, rust, oil, paint, and grease** from the welding zone (at least **20 mm from each edge**) using a wire brush and acetone. Contamination causes **porosity** in the weld.

**Step 3 — Edge Preparation**
- For a lap joint, **square edges** (no bevel) are sufficient. No edge preparation is required because the weld does not have to penetrate through full plate thickness from one side; it only has to fuse the top of plate B to the top of plate A.

**Step 4 — Fit-Up and Tacking**
- Overlap the two plates by a distance of approximately **3t to 5t** (i.e., **12–20 mm** for t = 4 mm).
- Align the edges and clamp them in position using **C-clamps or tack welds**.
- Place **two tack welds** — one at each end of the joint, each of about **10–15 mm length**. The tacks hold the plates during actual welding and prevent **distortion**.

**Step 5 — Setting the Welding Parameters**

For a **3.15 mm E6013 electrode** on a **4 mm mild steel plate**, use:

| Parameter | Setting |
|---|---|
| Current (DCEP) | **100 – 130 A** |
| Open-Circuit Voltage | **65 – 75 V** |
| Arc Voltage (during welding) | **22 – 26 V** |
| Electrode Angle | **70° – 80° to plate, drag angle** |
| Travel Speed | **80 – 120 mm/min** |
| Arc Length | **2 – 4 mm** (≈ electrode diameter) |

**Step 6 — Strike the Arc**
- Lower the welding helmet (shade **11** is safe for E6013).
- Hold the electrode **perpendicular** to the plate and **tap** or **scratch** it across the joint to start the arc.
- Once the arc is established, withdraw the electrode to maintain an **arc gap of 2–4 mm**.

**Step 7 — Weld the Joint**
- Maintain a **travel angle of 70°–80°** (slight drag) and a **work angle of 45°** so the arc force pushes the molten pool into the joint root.
- Move the electrode steadily along the joint at the chosen travel speed. Use a **slight weaving motion** (3–5 mm transverse oscillation) to widen the bead and ensure proper fusion with both plates.
- Listen for a **steady, crisp "frying-bacon" sound** — this indicates a correct arc length. A popping/hissing sound means the arc is too long.

**Step 8 — Slag Removal and Cleaning**
- Allow the weld to cool **until the red colour disappears** (≈ 2–3 minutes).
- Use the **chipping hammer** to break off the **slag** that covers the bead.
- Use the **wire brush** to clean the bead surface until the bright, shiny weld metal is visible.

**Step 9 — Visual Inspection (Mandatory for KTU CE Record)**
- Check the bead for: **uniform width, no undercut, no porosity, no cracks, full leg length, full fusion**.
- Measure the **leg length (L)** and **throat (T)** using a **fillet weld gauge**.
- If acceptable, repeat the weld on the **opposite edge** of the lap (a *double-lap* fillet) for additional strength.

**Step 10 — Record the Observations**
- Note the actual current, voltage, travel speed, electrode type, and electrode consumption.
- Calculate the **heat input** using the formula derived in §3.3.
- Take a photograph of the finished joint for the lab record.

### 3.3 Worked-Out Derivation: Heat Input Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Question (KTU-style):** A welder uses the SMAW process to make a lap joint on 4 mm mild steel plates. The arc voltage is 24 V, the welding current is 120 A, the travel speed is 100 mm/min, and the arc efficiency is 0.75. Calculate the heat input delivered per unit length of weld.

**Derivation:**

The electrical energy consumed by the arc per second is the **arc power** $P_{arc}$:

$$
P_{arc} = V \times I
$$

where $V$ is in volts and $I$ is in amperes, giving $P_{arc}$ in watts (= J/s).

The energy delivered to the **workpiece** (rather than lost to the surroundings) is the arc power multiplied by the **arc transfer efficiency** $\eta$:

$$
P_{useful} = \eta \times V \times I
$$

The welder covers a length of weld $L$ in a time $t$, so the travel speed is:

$$
S = \dfrac{L}{t} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \dfrac{L}{S}
$$

where $S$ is in mm/min and $L$ is in mm; the resulting $t$ is in **minutes**. Convert to seconds:

$$
t_{sec} = \dfrac{L}{S} \times 60
$$

The total energy delivered to the workpiece over length $L$ is:

$$
E_{total} = P_{useful} \times t_{sec} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \dfrac{L}{S} \cdot 60
$$

The **heat input per unit length** of weld is therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}\,} \quad \text{(kJ/mm)}
$$

where the factor $1000$ converts Joules to kiloJoules.

**Numerical Substitution:**

$$
H = \dfrac{0.75 \times 24 \times 120 \times 60}{100 \times 1000}
$$

Step 1: numerator $= 0.75 \times 24 = 18.0$

Step 2: $18.0 \times 120 = 2160$

Step 3: $2160 \times 60 = 129600$

Step 4: denominator $= 100 \times 1000 = 100000$

Step 5: $H = \dfrac{129600}{100000} = 1.296 \; \text{kJ/mm}$

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,H \approx 1.30 \; \text{kJ/mm}\,}
$$

This is within the recommended heat-input range of **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for mild steel using E6013 electrodes. ✅

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Defects vs. Causes vs. Remedies

| Defect | Visual Symptom | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Small pin-holes on bead surface | Dirty plate, wet electrode, long arc | Clean plate, dry electrode, shorten arc |
| **Undercut** | Groove at weld toe | Excessive current, fast travel, wrong angle | Lower current, slow down, correct angle |
| **Lack of fusion** | Bead sits on plate without melting in | Too low current, wrong polarity, dirty surface | Increase current, use DCEP, clean surface |
| **Incomplete penetration** | Fillet does not reach the root | Low current, slow speed, wrong electrode angle | Increase current, slow travel slightly |
| **Crater cracks** | Star-shaped crack at end of bead | Sudden arc stop at end of bead | Use crater-fill technique: hold, taper off |
| **Spatter** | Droplets of metal stuck around bead | Long arc, high current, damp electrode | Maintain short arc, reduce current, dry rod |
| **Slag inclusion** | Non-metallic material trapped in bead | Insufficient cleaning between passes | Chip & wire-brush between every pass |
| **Distortion / warping** | Plate bends after cooling | Uneven heat input, no tacking | Use balanced welding sequence, strong tacks |

### 3.5 Strength Calculation for the Lap Joint (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Question:** A lap joint is made on two 4 mm mild steel plates with a single fillet weld on each side of the overlap. The weld leg length is 5 mm and the total weld length is 100 mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is 110 MPa. Calculate the joint strength. Use the throat-thickness relation $T = 0.707 L$.

**Solution:**

The effective throat thickness is:

$$
T = 0.707 \times L = 0.707 \times 5 = 3.535 \; \text{mm}
$$

For a **double-fillet** lap joint, there are **two** welds, so the total shear area is:

$$
A_{shear} = 2 \times T \times L_{weld} = 2 \times 3.535 \times 100 = 707 \; \text{mm}^{2}
$$

Convert area to m²: $A = 707 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^{2} = 7.07 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m}^{2}$

The joint strength is:

$$
F = \tau_{allow} \times A = 110 \times 10^{6} \times 7.07 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
F = 110 \times 7.07 \times 10^{2} = 110 \times 707 = 77{,}770 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F \approx 77.8 \; \text{kN}\,}
$$

**Result interpretation:** A double-fillet lap joint with 5 mm leg length on 4 mm plate can safely carry approximately **77.8 kN** in static shear.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lap Joint Geometry (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Plate A - Top Member] --> B1[Overlapped Zone Length 3t to 5t]
    A2[Plate B - Bottom Member] --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Fillet Weld 1 - Top Edge of Overlap]
    B1 --> C2[Fillet Weld 2 - Bottom Edge of Overlap]
    C1 --> D1[Weld Bead Leg Length L]
    C1 --> D2[Effective Throat T = 0.707L]
    C2 --> E1[Shear Failure Plane along Throat]
    D1 --> F1[Load Transfer Path Plate A to Plate B]
    D2 --> F1
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Joint Strength F equals tau allow times A shear]
```

### 4.2 Arc Welding Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Power Source AC or DC Constant Current] --> P2[Electrode Holder Stinger]
    P2 --> P3[Electrode E6013 Diameter 3.15 mm]
    P3 --> P4[Arc Plasma Column Temperature 6000 to 7000 C]
    P4 --> P5[Parent Metal Plate A Melting]
    P4 --> P6[Parent Metal Plate B Melting]
    P4 --> P7[Electrode Tip Melting Filler Deposition]
    P5 --> P8[Common Molten Weld Pool]
    P6 --> P8
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[Flux Decomposition Shielding Gas and Slag Formation]
    P9 --> P10[Slag Floats on Top of Bead]
    P10 --> P11[Shielding Gas Protects Pool from Atmosphere]
    P8 --> P12[Cooling and Solidification]
    P12 --> P13[Solid Weld Bead Fillet Joint Formed]
    P11 --> P13
    P10 --> P13
    P13 --> P14[Slag Removal by Chipping Hammer and Wire Brush]
    P14 --> P15[Final Inspected Lap Joint]
```

### 4.3 Welder Action Sequence (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1[Step 1 Surface Cleaning] --> W2[Step 2 Plate Clamping and Fit Up]
    W2 --> W3[Step 3 Tack Welding at Both Ends]
    W3 --> W4[Step 4 Parameter Setting Current Voltage]
    W4 --> W5[Step 5 Arc Striking Scratch or Tap Method]
    W5 --> W6[Step 6 Bead Deposition with Drag Angle 70 to 80]
    W6 --> W7[Step 7 Slag Removal and Cleaning]
    W7 --> W8[Step 8 Visual Inspection and Measurement]
    W8 --> W9{Quality Acceptable}
    W9 -->|Yes| W10[Step 9 Record Parameters and Photograph]
    W9 -->|No| W6
    W10 --> W11[Step 10 Submit Lab Record for CE Evaluation]
```

### 4.4 Section Through the Lap Joint (Block-Level Side View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Plate A Top Plate 4 mm] --- S2[Overlap Region approx 15 mm]
    S2 --- S3[Plate B Bottom Plate 4 mm]
    S1 --> S4[Fillet Weld on Top Edge 45 degrees]
    S1 --> S5[Fillet Weld on Bottom Edge 45 degrees]
    S4 --> S6[Leg Length L equals 4 to 5 mm]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Throat Thickness T equals 0.707 times L]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `P8`, `W9`) and prefixed with letters to comply with the Mermaid parser requirements. All labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text and the standard ASCII characters (`–`, `→`, `=`, `°`) to avoid rendering errors.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**
> **Define a lap joint. State TWO advantages of a lap joint over a butt joint for sheet-metal fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **lap joint** is a type of joint in which the edges of two plates overlap each other by a distance of **3t to 5t** (where t is the plate thickness) and are joined by welding along the edges of the overlap, producing **fillet welds** (typically on one or both edges of the overlap).

**Two advantages over a butt joint:**
1. **No edge preparation required** — square edges are sufficient, saving machining time and cost.
2. **Greater dimensional tolerance** — small errors in plate length or straightness do not affect joint quality, making it ideal for thin sheet-metal work.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for each advantage, 1 mark for completeness.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> **What is the function of the flux coating on a shielded metal arc welding electrode? Name any TWO shielding gases released by the flux.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **flux coating** performs three critical functions:
1. It **decomposes in the arc heat** to release shielding gases that displace atmospheric N₂ and O₂ from the arc zone, preventing porosity and nitride embrittlement.
2. It forms a **molten slag** that floats on top of the weld pool, shielding it from oxidation and slowing the cooling rate to improve bead shape and toughness.
3. It **adds deoxidising and alloying elements** (Si, Mn, Ti) to the weld metal to improve mechanical properties.

**Two shielding gases released:** **Carbon dioxide (CO₂)** and **Hydrogen (H₂)**.

*(Award 1 mark for function, 1 mark for two gases, 1 mark for the third function or extra credit.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question, adapted]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure to prepare a **lap joint** on two 4 mm mild steel plates using the **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** process. List the equipment, materials, and safety precautions.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** During the welding of a lap joint, the welder uses an arc voltage of 25 V, a welding current of 110 A, and a travel speed of 90 mm/min. The arc efficiency of MMAW is 0.80. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** of weld. Also, comment on whether the value obtained is suitable for 4 mm mild steel.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

Award marks as follows:
- **1 mark** — List of equipment: welding machine (constant-current type), electrode holder, earthing clamp, chipping hammer, wire brush, PPE (helmet, gloves, apron, safety shoes).
- **1 mark** — List of materials: 2 × mild steel plates (100 × 50 × 4 mm), E6013 electrodes (Ø 3.15 mm).
- **2 marks** — Procedure (concise version):
  1. Clean plates with wire brush and acetone to remove mill scale and oil.
  2. Overlap the plates by 12–20 mm and clamp in position.
  3. Place two tack welds at each end of the overlap.
  4. Set the current to **100–130 A** and OCV to **65–75 V**.
  5. Strike the arc by scratch-start method; maintain 2–4 mm arc length.
  6. Weld along one edge of the overlap using a 70°–80° drag angle; chip and wire-brush the slag; repeat on the opposite edge.
  7. Inspect the bead visually for defects; measure leg length with fillet gauge.
- **1 mark** — Sketch: clean side-view diagram showing two overlapping plates, the fillet weld profile with leg length and throat labelled.
- **1 mark** — Safety precautions: wear helmet (shade 11), leather gloves, leather apron, safety shoes; keep fire extinguisher nearby; ensure fume extractor is on; never look at the arc without a helmet.
- **1 mark** — Tabulated parameter list (current, voltage, electrode type, travel speed).

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Apply the heat input formula with the given data:

$$
H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}
$$

Step 1 — Substitute the values: $\eta = 0.80$, $V = 25 \; \text{V}$, $I = 110 \; \text{A}$, $S = 90 \; \text{mm/min}$.

$$
H = \dfrac{0.80 \times 25 \times 110 \times 60}{90 \times 1000}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the numerator:

$$
0.80 \times 25 = 20
$$

$$
20 \times 110 = 2200
$$

$$
2200 \times 60 = 132000
$$

Step 3 — Compute the denominator:

$$
90 \times 1000 = 90000
$$

Step 4 — Divide:

$$
H = \dfrac{132000}{90000} = 1.467 \; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,H \approx 1.47 \; \text{kJ/mm}\,}
$$

**Comment (2 marks):**

The obtained heat input of **1.47 kJ/mm** lies at the **upper end** of the recommended range of **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for mild steel using E6013 electrodes. It is **acceptable but on the higher side**; it will produce a **slightly wider HAZ** and **coarser grain** than a lower heat input, but the joint will still be sound for 4 mm plate. To be on the safer side (better toughness, less distortion), the welder could **reduce the current to 90–100 A** or **increase the travel speed to 110–120 mm/min**, which would bring the heat input down to about **1.0–1.2 kJ/mm**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question, adapted]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principle of Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** with a labelled block diagram. Describe the functions of the **flux coating** and the **slag layer** formed during welding.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A **double-fillet lap joint** is made on two 5 mm mild steel plates. The leg length of the fillet weld is 6 mm and the total weld length on each side is 120 mm. The allowable shear stress in the weld metal is 110 MPa. Calculate the **strength of the joint** and the **length of the overlap** (use the rule $L_{overlap} = 5t$).

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

- **2 marks** — Principle: An **electric arc** is struck between the **coated electrode** (held in the stinger) and the **workpiece**. The arc temperature (**6,000–7,000 °C**) melts the parent metal and the electrode tip. The molten metal from both mixes in a common **weld pool**, which solidifies behind the arc to form the **weld bead**. A constant-current power source maintains a stable arc length despite small variations in operator distance.
- **2 marks** — Block diagram (refer to the Mermaid process-flow diagram in §4.2 of these notes). The student should show: power source → cable → electrode holder → electrode → arc → weld pool → bead → slag.
- **1.5 marks** — Functions of flux coating:
  1. Releases **shielding gases** (CO₂, H₂) to protect the weld pool from atmospheric contamination.
  2. Forms a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, prevents oxidation, and slows cooling.
  3. Adds **deoxidising elements** (Si, Mn) and **alloying elements** to the weld deposit.
  4. Stabilises the arc (especially the **rutile coating** of E6013, which contains TiO₂).
- **1.5 marks** — Functions of slag layer:
  1. **Protects** the cooling bead from atmospheric N₂ and O₂.
  2. **Shapes** the bead surface and slows cooling to refine grain structure.
  3. **Traps impurities** (sulphides, oxides) and floats them out of the weld metal.
  4. **Insulates** the red-hot bead, reducing heat loss and the risk of hardening cracks.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Effective throat thickness of the fillet weld:

$$
T = 0.707 \times L = 0.707 \times 6 = 4.242 \; \text{mm}
$$

Step 2 — Area of **one** fillet weld (single throat × weld length):

$$
A_{one} = T \times L_{weld} = 4.242 \times 120 = 508.99 \; \text{mm}^{2}
$$

Step 3 — For a **double-fillet** lap joint, the total shear area is doubled:

$$
A_{total} = 2 \times A_{one} = 2 \times 508.99 = 1017.99 \; \text{mm}^{2}
$$

Step 4 — Convert to m²:

$$
A_{total} = 1017.99 \times 10^{-6} = 1.018 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2}
$$

Step 5 — Joint strength:

$$
F = \tau_{allow} \times A_{total} = 110 \times 10^{6} \times 1.018 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
F = 110 \times 1018 = 111{,}980 \; \text{N}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,F \approx 112 \; \text{kN}\,}
$$

Step 6 — Length of overlap:

$$
L_{overlap} = 5t = 5 \times 5 = 25 \; \text{mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,L_{overlap} = 25 \; \text{mm}\,}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The double-fillet lap joint can carry approximately **112 kN** in static shear, and the plates should be overlapped by at least **25 mm** to provide sufficient weld-bearing area.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Wrong units in the heat-input formula.** Students frequently write $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{S}$ and forget the factors of **60** and **1000**. Always confirm that the answer is in **kJ/mm**, not J/mm or W/mm.
> 2. **Forgetting arc efficiency $\eta$.** A bare $H = V I / S$ gives the *arc* energy, not the *heat input to the workpiece*. Examiners explicitly test whether you know the difference. For MMAW, **$\eta \approx 0.75$** is the default.
> 3. **Confusing leg length and throat thickness.** Many students use $L$ (leg) instead of $T = 0.707 L$ (throat) in the strength formula. This **over-estimates the strength by 41%** and is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
> 4. **Single vs. double fillet not considered.** A lap joint with fillets on *both* sides has **double** the strength of a single-sided joint. Always read the question carefully.
> 5. **No mention of PPE in the procedure.** KTU's Continuous Evaluation (CE) rubric for lab courses specifically awards marks for **safety precautions**. Skipping the PPE list is a free 1-mark loss.
> 6. **Wrong polarity.** E6013 must be run on **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** or **AC** for best results. DCEN gives poor penetration and examiners will deduct marks.
> 7. **Not stating the cleaning step.** Skipping "remove mill scale, rust, and oil" loses 1 mark in the procedure question — examiners consider this a fundamental step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for KTU 2024 — PCNSL308, Module Experiment 2**
>
> ✅ **Lap Joint** = two plates overlapped by **3t–5t** and joined by **fillet welds** along the edge(s) of the overlap.
> ✅ **SMAW (Shielded Metal Arc Welding)** is the standard arc-welding process for this lab experiment; uses **constant-current** power source, **coated consumable electrode**.
> ✅ **Electrode of choice:** **E6013 (rutile-coated), Ø 3.15 mm**, runs on AC or DCEP.
> ✅ **Typical parameters for 4 mm plate:** Current **100–130 A**, arc voltage **22–26 V**, travel speed **80–120 mm/min**, arc length **2–4 mm**, drag angle **70°–80°**.
> ✅ **Heat input formula (most important):**
> $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \; \text{kJ/mm}, \quad \eta_{MMAW} \approx 0.75$$
> ✅ **Recommended heat-input range for mild steel:** **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm**.
> ✅ **Fillet weld throat formula:** $T = 0.707 \times L$ (equal-leg fillet). The strength of the joint is governed by the **throat**, not the leg.
> ✅ **Minimum overlap:** $L_{overlap} = 3t$ to $5t$. For 4 mm plate, use **12–20 mm**.
> ✅ **Always produce the weld on BOTH edges of the overlap** (double-fillet) unless told otherwise — gives double the strength and reduces distortion.
> ✅ **Mandatory procedure steps to record in the lab book:** (1) clean plates, (2) clamp and tack, (3) set parameters, (4) strike arc, (5) weld, (6) chip slag, (7) inspect, (8) measure, (9) record, (10) photograph.
> ✅ **Common defects to identify in visual inspection:** porosity, undercut, lack of fusion, slag inclusion, crater cracks, spatter, distortion.
> ✅ **PPE is non-negotiable:** welding helmet (shade 10–13), leather gloves, leather apron, safety shoes; fire extinguisher within 5 m; fume extractor ON.
> ✅ **Inspection instrument:** **fillet weld gauge** (measures leg length and throat).
> ✅ **Joint strength formula for a fillet lap joint:**
> $$F = \tau_{allow} \times T \times L_{weld} \times n$$
> where $n$ = number of fillets (1 for single-sided, 2 for double-sided).
> ✅ **Use DCEP polarity** for E6013 on mild steel — gives **deeper penetration** than DCEN.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prepare a Butt Joint Using TIG Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Prepare a Butt Joint Using TIG Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **TIG (Tungsten Inert Gas) Welding** — formally classified as **Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (GTAW)** per **ISO 4063:2023** and **AWS A3.0M/A3.0:2020** — is a non-consumable electrode arc welding process in which an arc is established between a **non-melting tungsten electrode** and the work-piece, while the weld pool and the electrode tip are **shielded by an inert gas** (typically **Argon** or **Argon–Helium mixture**) to prevent atmospheric contamination.

A **Butt Joint** is the simplest and most common edge preparation in fusion welding, formed by placing two work-pieces edge-to-edge in the same plane and fusing them along the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aim of the Experiment (KTU PCNSL308 – Module 2):** *To prepare a square-butt joint on **mild steel / stainless steel / aluminium** plates using the **TIG (GTAW) process** and to inspect the weld bead for surface defects, dimensional accuracy, and soundness.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **trying to glue two pieces of paper together** using a thin, hot pin-point flame (like a candle) instead of a big brush.

- The **tungsten electrode** is your *pin-point flame* — it gets hot but does not melt or stick to the paper.
- Your **shielding gas (Argon)** is the *invisible glove* that prevents oxygen and moisture from spoiling the glue (molten weld pool).
- The **filler rod** (if used) is the *extra glue stick* you feed in to fill any gap.
- The **butt joint** is the *seam where the two papers meet edge-to-edge*.

> [!TIP]
> **Why TIG and not stick welding?** Stick (SMAW) uses a *consumable* electrode that melts into the joint — it is messy, spattery, and harder to control. TIG uses a *non-consumable* tungsten electrode, giving you **pin-point arc control**, **cleaner welds**, and **no slag** to chip off.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (for Mild Steel, 3 mm) |
|---|---|
| **Open-circuit voltage** | **60 – 80 V** |
| **Arc voltage** | **10 – 16 V** |
| **Welding current (DCEN)** | **60 – 120 A** |
| **Argon flow rate** | **8 – 12 L/min** |
| **Electrode diameter** | **1.6 – 2.4 mm** |
| **Electrode tip angle** | **30° – 60°** (steel) |
| **Travel speed** | **80 – 150 mm/min** |
| **Filler rod diameter** | **1.6 – 2.4 mm** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet
