### 2.1 Principles of the TIG Process (Bullet-Logic Breakdown)

- **Arc Generation:** A high-frequency (HF) or lift-arc starter ionises the argon gap between the tungsten electrode (cathode, –) and the work-piece (anode, +), creating a sustained electric arc.
- **Heat Source:** The arc reaches temperatures of approximately **20 000 K** — sufficient to melt the base metal and any added filler.
- **Shielding Mechanism:** Argon, being heavier than air and chemically inert, blankets the weld pool and the hot tungsten tip, displacing **O₂**, **N₂**, and **H₂O vapour**.
- **Electrode Polarity:** For **steel and stainless steel**, **Direct Current Electrode Negative (DCEN)** is used — electrons flow from the electrode to the work-piece, concentrating ~**70 % of the heat** in the work-piece (deep penetration, narrow HAZ).
- **Filler Metal:** Added manually by the operator's other hand — gives precise control over bead geometry.

### 2.2 Why "Butt Joint" — The Geometry

A **square butt joint** has no edge bevel. The two plates are placed with a controlled root gap (typically **1.5 – 2.5 mm**) and welded in a single pass. For thicker plates (> **3 mm**), a **V-groove butt joint** is preferred.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All the following relations are commonly required for KTU viva + lab record calculations.

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q \;=\; V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Heat input to the weld (where $V$ = arc voltage, $I$ = current, $t$ = time) | **Joules (J)** |
| 2 | $H \;=\; \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$ | Heat input per unit length (arc efficiency $\eta \approx 0.6$ for TIG) | **kJ/mm** |
| 3 | $v \;=\; \dfrac{L}{t}$ | Travel speed = weld length ÷ time | **mm/min** |
| 4 | $D_{e} \;=\; 0.0254 \cdot S$ | Electrode diameter (rule-of-thumb: $S$ = plate thickness in inches, $D_e$ in inches) | **mm** |
| 5 | $C_{f} \;=\; 10 \cdot S$ | Filler rod current (rough) | **A** |
| 6 | $A_{w} \;=\; t_{p} \cdot L \cdot \rho$ | Weld metal weight (rough) | **g** |
| 7 | $F_{Ar} \;=\; k \cdot I$ | Argon flow rate, $k \approx 0.1$–$0.2$ L/min per ampere | **L/min** |
| 8 | $P_{f} \;=\; \dfrac{H - H_{0}}{H_{0}} \times 100$ | % Heat input change for parameter variation | **%** |

> **Where:** $\eta$ = arc efficiency (TIG $\approx$ **0.6**), $v$ = travel speed, $L$ = weld length, $t_{p}$ = plate thickness, $\rho$ = density of weld metal (**7.86 g/cm³** for steel), $H_{0}$ = baseline heat input.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

TIG-prepared butt joints are indispensable in:

- **Aerospace:** thin-wall stainless steel & titanium fuselage panels
- **Pharmaceutical / Food:** sanitary stainless-steel piping (304L / 316L)
- **Automotive:** aluminium fuel tanks, exhaust manifolds
- **Nuclear:** high-purity welds on austenitic SS components
- **Bicycle & Motorsport:** lightweight aluminium/steel frames

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember TIG advantages → "PIN-POINT":** **P**recise, **I**nert-shielded, **N**o-slag, **N**o-spatter, **O**xidation-free, **I**ndependent filler, **N**on-consumable electrode, **T**hin-section friendly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Laboratory Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Work-piece Preparation**
- Cut two mild-steel plates (**150 mm × 50 mm × 3 mm**).
- File/sand the mating edges square and flat.
- Degrease with acetone; clamp plates on the **welding table** with a **root gap = 2 mm** and **root face = 0 mm** (square butt).
- Check **root alignment** with a filler gauge; misalignment should be **≤ 10 % of plate thickness**.

**Step 2 — Machine Set-up**
- Connect **TIG torch** to the **GTAW power source** (e.g., Miller Syncrowave 200, Lincoln Square Wave).
- Connect **Argon cylinder** via regulator and flow-meter (set **10 L/min**).
- Insert **2 % Thoriated Tungsten (EWTh-2)** electrode, **⌀ 2.4 mm**.
- **Grind the tip** to a sharp **60° cone** with a small flat land at the apex (**0.5 – 1.0 mm**).
- Set polarity to **DCEN (Straight Polarity)**.
- Connect **work clamp** to the work-piece — ensure a clean metal-to-metal contact.

**Step 3 — Parameter Selection (Reference Table)**

| Parameter | Value (3 mm MS) | Value (2 mm SS) | Value (3 mm Al) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process | GTAW-DC | GTAW-DC | GTAW-AC |
| Current | **80 A** | **70 A** | **100 A** |
| Voltage | **12 V** | **11 V** | **13 V** |
| Argon flow | **10 L/min** | **9 L/min** | **12 L/min** |
| Electrode | **⌀ 2.4 mm 2 % Th** | **⌀ 1.6 mm 2 % Th** | **⌀ 2.4 mm pure W** |
| Filler | **ER70S-3 ⌀ 1.6 mm** | **ER308L ⌀ 1.6 mm** | **ER4043 ⌀ 2.0 mm** |

**Step 4 — Gas Purging**
- Open the **argon valve** at the cylinder and torch, then purge for **15 – 30 s** before striking the arc to displace air from the hose and torch.

**Step 5 — Arc Striking**
- Hold the torch **2 – 4 mm** above the work-piece.
- Press the **torch switch / foot pedal** → **HF (High-Frequency) starter** ionises the gap.
- Establish a **stable arc cone**; arc length ≈ **1.5 × electrode diameter**.

**Step 6 — Welding (Dipping Filler Technique)**
- Hold the torch in the **right hand** at **70 – 80°** to the work-piece (push angle for steel).
- Hold the **filler rod in the left hand** at **15 – 20°** to the work-piece.
- Dip the tip of the filler into the **leading edge of the weld pool** — do **not** melt it in the arc.
- Travel at a uniform speed (**~ 100 mm/min**) producing a **bright, silvery, rippled bead**.

**Step 7 — Crater Filling & Arc Stop**
- At the end of the weld, **gradually reduce current** (using foot-pedal or down-slope) to fill the **crater**.
- **Delay gas-off** for **5 – 10 s** after arc extinction to shield the cooling weld from oxidation.

**Step 8 — Inspection**
- Visual: bead width, ripples, undercut, crater, spatter.
- Dimensional: bead width, height, leg length.
- Destructive: **guided bend test** (root + face bend) on a sample.
- Non-destructive: **dye-penetrant test (DPT)** for surface cracks; optional **radiography** for internal soundness.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical — Heat Input Calculation

> **Problem (typical KTU 2-mark sub-question):**
> In a TIG butt weld on a 3 mm mild-steel plate, the arc voltage is **12 V**, welding current is **90 A**, and travel speed is **100 mm/min**. The arc efficiency of GTAW is **0.6**. Calculate the **heat input per unit length (kJ/mm)**.

**Solution — full derivation:**

Given:
$$V \;=\; 12 \text{ V}, \quad I \;=\; 90 \text{ A}, \quad v \;=\; 100 \text{ mm/min}, \quad \eta \;=\; 0.6$$

The generalised **heat-input equation** is:
$$H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

Substituting the numerical values:
$$H \;=\; \frac{0.6 \times 12 \times 90}{100 \times 1000}$$

Compute the numerator:
$$\eta \cdot V \cdot I \;=\; 0.6 \times 12 \times 90 \;=\; 0.6 \times 1080 \;=\; 648 \text{ W·min/mm}$$

Therefore:
$$H \;=\; \frac{648}{100 \times 1000} \;=\; \frac{648}{100000}$$

$$\boxed{H \;=\; 0.00648 \text{ kJ/mm} \;=\; 6.48 \text{ J/mm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check:** For mild steel 3 mm, acceptable heat input range is **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for arc processes. The value **6.48 kJ/mm is in the TIG range** (TIG delivers lower heat input than SMAW for the same parameters due to lower $\eta$).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation on Weld Quality

| Parameter ↑ (increase) | Penetration | Bead Width | HAZ Width | Distortion | Spatter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Current | **↑↑ Strong** | **↑** | **↑** | **↑↑** | **↑** |
| Voltage | **↓ slight** | **↑↑** | **↑** | **↑** | **—** |
| Travel speed | **↓** | **↓** | **↓** | **↓** | **—** |
| Argon flow | **— up to optimum** | **—** | **—** | **—** | **↓ porosity** |
| Electrode stick-out | **↓** | **↓** | **—** | **—** | **↑** |

> **Key viva trick:** "*If the bead is wide and shallow — reduce voltage. If it is narrow and humped — increase travel speed or reduce current.*"

### 3.4 Common TIG Weld Defects & Remedies

| Defect | Root Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Insufficient gas / dirty metal | Increase Ar flow; clean with acetone |
| **Tungsten inclusion** | Electrode dipped in pool | Re-grind tip; maintain arc length |
| **Cracking** | High heat input / restraint | Reduce current; preheat |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current or travel speed | Reduce current; weave slowly |
| **Oxidation (rainbow colours)** | Inadequate post-flow | Increase post-flow to 10 s |
| **Lack of fusion** | Low current / fast travel | Slow down; increase current |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 TIG Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AC Mains Supply] --> A2[TIG Power Source DCEN]
    A2 -- High-Frequency HV Pulse --> A3[Tungsten Electrode]
    A3 -- Electric Arc 20 000 K --> A4[Work-piece Butt Joint]
    B1[Argon Cylinder] --> B2[Regulator + Flow Meter]
    B2 -- Shielding Gas 10 L/min --> A3
    B2 -- Shielding Gas --> A4
    A4 --> A5[Manual Filler Rod Feed]
    A5 --> A6[Welded Butt Joint]
    A4 --> A7[Cooling Zone]
    A7 -- Post-flow Ar 5-10 s --> A8[Oxide-free Bead]
    C1[Foot Pedal] -- Current Modulation --> A2
```

### 4.2 Square-Butt Joint Geometry (Schematic Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Plate A 150 x 50 x 3 mm] --> J1[Clamp Edge-to-Edge]
    P2[Plate B 150 x 50 x 3 mm] --> J1
    J1 --> J2[Root Gap 2 mm]
    J2 --> J3[TIG Torch 70-80 deg Angle]
    J3 --> J4[Arc Strike + Travel 100 mm/min]
    J4 --> J5[Filler Dip Technique]
    J5 --> J6[Crater Fill + Post-flow]
    J6 --> J7[Welded Butt Joint]
    J7 --> J8[Visual + Bend Test Inspection]
```

### 4.3 TIG Torch Anatomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T1[Handle + Switch] --> T2[Power Cable Inlet]
    T1 --> T3[Gas Hose Inlet]
    T3 --> T4[Gas Lens Collet Body]
    T4 --> T5[Gas Cup Ceramic Nozzle]
    T5 --> T6[Tungsten Electrode Clamped in Collet]
    T2 --> T7[Power Cable to Electrode]
    T1 --> T8[Trigger Lock / Foot Pedal Connector]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow — Lab Record Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Receive Work-piece] --> S2[Edge Preparation]
    S2 --> S3[Cleaning + Clamping]
    S3 --> S4[Machine Set-up + Gas Purge]
    S4 --> S5[Parameter Setting]
    S5 --> S6[Strike Arc + Weld]
    S6 --> S7[Crater Fill + Post-flow]
    S7 --> S8[Visual Inspection]
    S8 --> S9[Destructive or NDT Test]
    S9 --> S10[Record + Tabulate]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*What is TIG welding? List any **four advantages** of the TIG (GTAW) process over the shielded metal arc welding (SMAW) process.*

**Model Answer:**

> **TIG Welding (GTAW):** It is an arc welding process in which the heat for welding is produced by an electric arc struck between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** and the work-piece, the arc and the molten weld pool being protected from atmospheric contamination by an **inert shielding gas** (usually **Argon**).
>
> **Four advantages of TIG over SMAW:**
> 1. **No slag formation** → cleaner weld, no chipping required.
> 2. **No spatter** → better surface finish and minimal post-weld cleaning.
> 3. **Superior arc control** → precise heat input, suitable for thin sections and out-of-position welding.
> 4. **Higher weld quality** — non-porous, defect-free welds critical for stainless steel, aluminium, and exotic alloys used in aerospace and nuclear applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*With a neat sketch, describe the **parts of a TIG torch**.*

**Model Answer:**

A TIG torch consists of the following parts (refer Fig. 4.3 in Section 4):
- **Handle with trigger switch** — for arc ON/OFF and gas pre-flow / post-flow control.
- **Power cable inlet** — connects the torch to the negative terminal of the DC power source (DCEN).
- **Gas hose inlet** — supplies argon from the cylinder via the regulator/flow-meter.
- **Collet body / Gas lens** — holds the tungsten electrode and creates a smooth, laminar gas flow.
- **Ceramic gas cup (nozzle)** — directs the shielding gas onto the weld pool; typical internal diameters: **6, 8, 10, 12 mm**.
- **Collet** — clamps the tungsten electrode (sizes matched to electrode diameter).
- **Tungsten electrode** — 2 % Thoriated (red tip, EWTh-2) for DC welding of steel/SS; pure tungsten (green tip, EWP) for AC welding of aluminium.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice A or B)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU ESE — Module 2 Pattern]**

**(a)** Describe in detail, with a suitable sketch, the **TIG (GTAW) welding process** for preparing a **square-butt joint** on two **3 mm mild-steel plates**. Clearly mention the **equipment, electrode, shielding gas, polarity, and step-by-step procedure**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TIG butt weld is made on a 4 mm stainless-steel plate with the following parameters: arc voltage = **14 V**, current = **110 A**, travel speed = **80 mm/min**, and arc efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. Calculate the **heat input per unit length in kJ/mm** and comment on whether the value is acceptable for stainless steel. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Equipment Required:**
- **TIG power source** with **HF starter** and **DCEN** capability (e.g., Miller Syncrowave 200 / Lincoln Square Wave 200).
- **TIG torch** with **ceramic gas cup (size 8)**.
- **2 % Thoriated Tungsten electrode**, ⌀ **2.4 mm**, tip ground to a **60°** cone with **0.5 mm land**.
- **Argon cylinder** (purity 99.99 %), regulator, and flow-meter.
- **ER70S-3 filler rod** ⌀ **1.6 mm**.
- **Welding table, clamps, filler gauge, angle grinder, acetone, leather gloves, auto-darkening helmet.**

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Edge Preparation** — file the mating edges square; degrease with acetone.
2. **Clamping** — clamp plates with **2 mm root gap**; check alignment with filler gauge.
3. **Polarity** — set to **DCEN (Electrode Negative, Straight Polarity)**.
4. **Argon Purging** — open cylinder valve; set **10 L/min**; purge **20 s**.
5. **Electrode Set-up** — grind tip; set stick-out ≈ **3 – 4 mm** beyond the gas cup.
6. **Parameter Setting** — current **80 A**, voltage **12 V**.
7. **Striking Arc** — use HF starter; establish arc at **~ 2 mm** gap.
8. **Welding** — push torch at **70 – 80°**; dip filler rod at **15 – 20°**; uniform travel.
9. **Crater Fill & Post-flow** — reduce current gradually; hold post-flow **8 s**.
10. **Inspection** — visual + guided bend test.

> *Refer the flow diagrams in Section 4 for the schematic view.*

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V = 14$ V, $I = 110$ A, $v = 80$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Heat input equation:**
$$H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \times 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$H \;=\; \frac{0.6 \times 14 \times 110}{80 \times 1000}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$\eta \cdot V \cdot I \;=\; 0.6 \times 14 \times 110 \;=\; 0.6 \times 1540 \;=\; 924$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**
$$H \;=\; \frac{924}{80000} \;=\; 0.01155 \text{ kJ/mm}$$

$$\boxed{H \;\approx\; 1.155 \text{ kJ/mm} \;\approx\; 1155 \text{ J/mm}}$$

**Step 5 — Comment:** For **4 mm austenitic stainless steel**, the typical recommended heat input range is **0.8 – 1.5 kJ/mm** (to avoid sensitisation, carbide precipitation, and excessive HAZ grain growth). The calculated value of **1.155 kJ/mm lies within the acceptable range**, so the weld parameters are **suitable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 1000** in the denominator → answer becomes 924 kJ/mm (loses 2 marks).
> 2. **Using $v$ in mm/s instead of mm/min** without unit conversion → wrong answer.
> 3. **Not stating the arc efficiency value** of TIG (= 0.6) → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the "comment" part** — KTU always asks for engineering interpretation; missing this loses **1 – 2 marks**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **principle, equipment, and process parameters** of **TIG welding**. Compare **DCEN** and **DCEP** polarity modes with their effects on tungsten electrode and penetration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a TIG butt weld, the heat input is **0.85 kJ/mm** at a current of **95 A** and a travel speed of **75 mm/min**. If the arc efficiency is **0.6**, determine the **arc voltage** used. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution (a) — 7 marks**

**Principle:** Heat is generated by an **electric arc** maintained between a **non-consumable tungsten electrode** (cathode) and the work-piece (anode) in an **inert atmosphere of argon**. The operator dips a **filler rod** manually into the leading edge of the weld pool. The inert gas displaces **O₂, N₂, H₂O** and prevents oxidation/nitridation of the molten metal.

**Equipment:**
- Power source (constant-current, HF starter, gas solenoid, post-flow timer)
- TIG torch (handle, collet, gas cup, power cable, gas hose)
- Tungsten electrode (2 % Thoriated for DC, Pure W or Zirconiated for AC)
- Argon cylinder + regulator + flow-meter
- Filler rod + work clamp + earthing cable

**Process Parameters (KTU expected list):**
Current, voltage, travel speed, gas flow rate, electrode diameter & tip angle, electrode stick-out, nozzle-to-work distance, root gap, filler diameter.

**DCEN vs DCEP:**

| Feature | **DCEN (Straight)** | **DCEP (Reverse)** |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Electrode = –ve | Electrode = +ve |
| Heat on work-piece | **~ 70 %** (deep penetration) | ~ 30 % |
| Heat on electrode | ~ 30 % | ~ 70 % (electrode overheats) |
| Tungsten life | Long | **Very short — melts** |
| Cleaning action | None | Yes (breaks oxide layer) |
| Typical use | Steel, SS, Ti | **Not used for TIG** |
| Penetration | Deep & narrow | Shallow & wide |

**Conclusion:** **DCEN** is the universally preferred polarity for TIG welding because it concentrates heat in the work-piece, provides deep penetration, and **preserves the tungsten electrode**.

---

#### **Solution (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $H = 0.85$ kJ/mm, $I = 95$ A, $v = 75$ mm/min, $\eta = 0.6$

**Step 1 — Rearrange the heat-input equation to solve for $V$:**
$$V \;=\; \frac{H \cdot v \cdot 1000}{\eta \cdot I}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**
$$V \;=\; \frac{0.85 \times 75 \times 1000}{0.6 \times 95}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**
$$0.85 \times 75 \times 1000 \;=\; 63\,750$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**
$$0.6 \times 95 \;=\; 57$$

**Step 5 — Divide:**
$$V \;=\; \frac{63750}{57} \;\approx\; 1118.4$$

$$\boxed{V \;\approx\; 11.18 \text{ V} \;\approx\; 11 \text{ V}}$$

**Sanity check:** An arc voltage of **11 V** is well within the typical TIG operating range of **10 – 16 V** for a butt joint on a thin plate. ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (for Question B):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch** — the **1000** factor is needed only when output is in **kJ**, not **J**. Mixing units here is the **#1 reason students lose 3 marks** in heat-input problems.
> 2. **Confusing arc efficiency of TIG (0.6) with SMAW (0.7 – 0.8)** → wrong value, loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Not commenting on the physical acceptability** of the calculated voltage — KTU values interpretation in viva-style answers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist (must be remembered for KTU Lab Exam + Viva):**

- **Process name:** Gas Tungsten Arc Welding (**GTAW / TIG**).
- **Electrode type:** **Non-consumable Tungsten** (2 % Thoriated = red tip for DC; Pure W = green tip for AC).
- **Shielding gas:** **Argon (99.99 %)** — inert, heavier than air.
- **Polarity for steel / SS:** **DCEN (Straight)** — 70 % heat in work-piece, deep penetration.
- **Polarity for aluminium:** **AC** — provides self-cleaning of oxide layer.
- **Arc starter:** **High-Frequency (HF)** non-contact start.
- **Post-flow time:** **5 – 10 s** after arc-off to prevent oxidation of the cooling bead.
- **Heat input equation:** $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{v \cdot 1000}$, with **$\eta_{\text{TIG}} \approx 0.6$**.
- **Electrode tip angle:** **30° – 60°** (60° for steel; 30° for thin stainless / aluminium).
- **Acceptable TIG defects to know:** porosity, tungsten inclusion, undercut, lack of fusion, crater cracks.
- **Inspection tests:** Visual → DPT → guided bend test → radiography (optional).
- **Standard code references (KTU-acceptable):** **IS 2811, AWS A5.18, AWS A5.9, ISO 4063**.
- **Safety must-do's:** UV-rated auto-darkening helmet, leather gloves, **argon-ventilated workspace** (Argon displaces O₂ → asphyxiation risk in closed rooms), fire-extinguisher nearby, **earthing of work-clamp**.
- **TIG mnemonic — "TIPS":** **T**ungsten, **I**nert gas, **P**recise filler, **S**traight polarity (DCEN).
- **Lab record must include:** aim, apparatus list, procedure, parameter table, observation table, sample calculation of heat input, defect sketch, result, and viva Q&A.

> **Best of luck for your PCNSL308 Lab Exam — go TIG it! 🛠️**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prepare a Butt Joint Using MIG Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Prepare a Butt Joint Using MIG Welding # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Metal Inert Gas (MIG) Welding**, standardized by the American Welding Society (AWS) as **Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW)**, is a semi-automatic or automatic arc welding process in which a continuous, consumable solid wire electrode is fed at a controlled rate through a welding gun. An electric arc is established between the **electrode (acting as one pole)** and the **workpiece (acting as the other pole)**, while a **shielding gas** — typically **Argon (Ar), Carbon Dioxide (CO₂), or a mixture of both** — is simultaneously delivered through the nozzle to protect the molten weld pool from atmospheric contamination. In the context of KTU Module Experiment 2, the specific objective is to **prepare a Butt Joint** — a type of edge joint in which the two plates to be welded are placed in the same plane with their edges in contact (or with a root gap), and the weld metal is deposited along the line of their abutment to fuse them into a single, continuous member. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCNSL308):** > *“MIG welding is an arc welding process that uses a continuous solid wire electrode, fed automatically through a welding gun, with an externally supplied shielding gas to protect the weld zone from atmospheric oxidation.”* --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are using a **hot glue gun**, but instead of glue, you are feeding a thin metal wire, and instead of melted plastic, you are melting the wire itself into a liquid metal pool that joins two steel plates. At the same time, an invisible "umbrella" of inert gas (Argon/CO₂) is blown around the hot zone to **keep oxygen, nitrogen, and moisture away** — because if air touches molten steel, the weld becomes porous, weak, and brittle. A simpler real-world analogy: > Think of MIG welding as **continuous, mechanized candle-melting-into-a-joint**: The "candle" (wire) is fed steadily into a flame (arc), the molten drip fills the gap between two plates, and a gentle breeze of Argon gas (the "umbrella") shields the hot pool from rain (atmospheric contamination). --- ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities The following **bold** quantities are the primary process parameters that govern the quality of a MIG-welded butt joint: | Parameter | Typical Range (Mild Steel) | Unit | |---|---|---| | Welding Current (I) | **80 – 350** | Amperes (A) | | Arc Voltage (V) | **18 – 32** | Volts (V) | | Wire Feed Speed (WFS) | **3 – 12** | m/min | | Travel Speed (S) | **3 – 8** | mm/s (≈ 180 – 480 mm/min) | | Shielding Gas Flow Rate | **10 – 20** | L/min | | Shielding Gas Composition | **75% Ar + 25% CO₂** | — | | Electrode Diameter | **0.8, 1.0, 1.2** | mm | | Polarity | **DCEP** (Direct Current Electrode Positive) | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **DCEP (Reverse Polarity)** is mandatory in MIG welding. The electrode (wire) is connected to the **positive terminal** of the power source, while the workpiece is connected to the **negative terminal**. This configuration provides: > - **Stable arc** behavior > - **Deep penetration** into the base metal > - **Self-cleaning** action (especially with CO₂-rich mixes) > - Reduced spatter compared to DCEN --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown 1. **Power Source Activation**: A constant-voltage (CV) DC power source is switched ON. CV sources (not CC) are used in MIG welding because the wire feed speed primarily controls current, while voltage is set to a fixed level. 2. **Wire Feeding Initiation**: A motor-driven **wire feed mechanism** pushes the spooled solid electrode wire (e.g., ER70S-6) through the **liner** of the welding gun at a preset rate (in m/min). 3. **Contact Tip Current Transfer**: The wire passes through a copper **contact tip**, which is electrically energized. Current flows from the contact tip → through the wire → across the arc → into the workpiece → back via the **ground clamp** to the power source. 4. **Arc Establishment (Trigger Pull)**: As the wire approaches the workpiece, it touches the plate, creating a **short circuit** that draws high current, melts the wire tip, and establishes a stable arc. Modern MIG guns use a "trigger latch" mechanism that pre-feeds wire before arc establishment. 5. **Metal Transfer Modes**: The droplet detachment from the wire tip into the weld pool occurs in one of four modes: - **Short-circuit Transfer (SCT)**: Low voltage, low current; wire dips into pool, short-circuits, detaches. Used for thin plates < 3 mm. - **Globular Transfer**: Large droplets > wire diameter. Higher heat, more spatter. - **Spray Transfer**: High current > transition current; fine axial droplets, smooth, deep penetration. Used for plates > 3 mm. - **Pulsed Spray Transfer**: Pulsed current alternates spray peaks and background; better control. 6. **Shielding Gas Delivery**: Simultaneously, Argon/CO₂ from a high-pressure cylinder flows through a **regulator** (set to ~2 bar) and **flowmeter** (set to 12–18 L/min) → through a **gas hose** → out of the **gas nozzle** surrounding the contact tip, forming a protective envelope around the arc and molten pool. 7. **Weld Pool Solidification**: As the torch moves along the joint (controlled by operator hand speed or mechanized carriage), the molten pool cools and solidifies behind, forming a continuous weld bead. --- ## 2.2 Preparation of the Butt Joint — Engineering Justification A **butt joint** is the simplest configuration for joining two plates edge-to-edge. However, the quality of edge preparation directly determines penetration, fusion, and mechanical strength. | Plate Thickness (t) | Edge Preparation | Groove Angle | Root Gap | Root Face | |---|---|---|---|---| | **t ≤ 3 mm** | Square butt (no bevel) | — | 0 – 1 mm | None | | **3 mm < t ≤ 10 mm** | Single-V bevel | **60° – 70°** | **2 mm** | 1 – 2 mm | | **t > 10 mm** | Double-V or U-groove | 60° (each side) | 2 mm | 1 – 2 mm | > [!NOTE] > **Why a V-groove for thicker plates?** A V-groove exposes the sidewalls of the joint, allowing the arc to reach the root. Without beveling, the arc cannot penetrate beyond ~3 mm of steel — the heat dissipates sideways instead of melting downward. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table) The following are the **four most critical formulas** for KTU lab report evaluation in MIG welding: | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Heat Input (H)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ | kJ/mm | $\eta$ = arc efficiency = **0.80** for MIG; S in mm/min | | 2 | **Heat Input (alternative)** | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S \cdot 1000}$ | J/mm | S in mm/s | | 3 | **Deposition Rate (R)** | $R = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d^{2}}{4} \cdot \rho \cdot WFS \cdot 60$ | kg/h | d = wire dia (m), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), WFS in m/s | | 4 | **Wire Consumption** | $m = R \cdot t_{weld}$ | kg | $t_{weld}$ = welding time (h) | | 5 | **Travel Speed** | $S = \dfrac{L_{weld}}{t_{weld}}$ | mm/min | $L_{weld}$ = bead length | | 6 | **Specific Energy per Unit Length** | Same as Heat Input | kJ/mm | Used in HAZ (Heat-Affected Zone) prediction | > [!IMPORTANT] > **MIG Arc Efficiency ($\eta$)**: The fraction of electrical energy actually delivered to the weld (vs. lost as heat in cables, spatter, and radiation). For MIG/GMAW, $\eta \approx 0.75$ – $0.85$; KTU examiners typically expect **$\eta = 0.80$** unless otherwise specified. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Utility MIG-welded butt joints form the structural backbone of countless engineering systems: - **Automotive Industry**: Car chassis, sub-frames, exhaust hangers (often robotic MIG). - **Pressure Vessels & Boilers**: Shell-to-shell circumferential butt joints. - **Shipbuilding**: Hull plate butt joints (often using tandem MIG for high deposition). - **Structural Steel**: I-beam flange-to-web joints in buildings and bridges. - **Railways**: Wagon and coach body fabrication. - **Pipelines**: API 5L linepipe girth butt welds (qualified to ASME IX). > [!TIP] > **Why prefer MIG over SMAW (stick) for butt joints?** > - Higher **deposition rate** (3–5 kg/h vs. 1–2 kg/h for stick). > - **Continuous wire** = no slag to chip, less downtime. > - Better **operator visibility** of the arc and pool. > - Cleaner, more consistent beads → less post-weld grinding. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides exhaustive, line-by-line derivations and worked numerical examples that students are expected to reproduce in their KTU lab records. --- ## 3.1 Derivation of Heat Input Formula ### 3.1.1 Fundamental Energy Balance The **electrical energy input** to the welding process over a time interval $\Delta t$ is given by: $$E_{elec} = V \cdot I \cdot \Delta t$$ where: - $V$ = arc voltage in Volts (V) - $I$ = welding current in Amperes (A) - $\Delta t$ = arc time in seconds (s) The energy is in **Joules (J)**. ### 3.1.2 Accounting for Arc Efficiency Not all electrical energy reaches the weld zone. A fraction $\eta$ (the arc efficiency or thermal efficiency) is transferred to the workpiece: $$E_{weld} = \eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \Delta t$$ ### 3.1.3 Normalizing by Bead Length To obtain **Heat Input per unit length** (a parameter used universally to predict HAZ width and dilution), divide by the bead length $L_{bead}$: $$H = \dfrac{E_{weld}}{L_{bead}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \Delta t}{L_{bead}}$$ ### 3.1.4 Expressing in mm/min Travel Speed Since **Travel Speed** $S = \dfrac{L_{bead}}{\Delta t}$, we have $\Delta t = \dfrac{L_{bead}}{S}$. Substituting: $$\begin{aligned} H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \left(\dfrac{L_{bead}}{S}\right)}{L_{bead}} \\ &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S} \end{aligned}$$ If $S$ is in **m/s**, then $H$ is in **J/m**. Converting to **kJ/mm** (the KTU-preferred unit): $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{S_{\text{m/s}} \cdot 1000} \quad \text{[J/m]} \times 10^{-3} \text{[to kJ/mm]}$$ But if $S$ is directly provided in **mm/min**, multiply numerator by 60 (to convert minutes to seconds) and divide by 1000 (to convert J to kJ): $$\boxed{H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm}}$$ This is the **standard KTU board exam formula**. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Heat Input Calculation > **Problem**: During a MIG butt weld on 6 mm mild steel plate, the operator records the following parameters: > - Arc Voltage, $V = 24 \text{ V}$ > - Welding Current, $I = 180 \text{ A}$ > - Travel Speed, $S = 4 \text{ mm/s}$ > - Arc Efficiency, $\eta = 0.80$ > > Calculate the **Heat Input per unit length** in kJ/mm. ### Step-by-Step Solution: **Step 1**: Identify given values and target unit. $$\eta = 0.80, \quad V = 24 \text{ V}, \quad I = 180 \text{ A}, \quad S = 4 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Step 2**: Convert travel speed to mm/min (required by the standard formula). $$\begin{aligned} S &= 4 \;\text{mm/s} \times 60 \;\text{s/min} \\ &= 240 \;\text{mm/min} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3**: Substitute into the heat input formula. $$\begin{aligned} H &= \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000} \\ &= \dfrac{0.80 \times 24 \times 180 \times 60}{240 \times 1000} \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4**: Evaluate the numerator. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &= 0.80 \times 24 \times 180 \times 60 \\ &= 0.80 \times 24 \times 10800 \\ &= 0.80 \times 259200 \\ &= 207360 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5**: Evaluate the denominator. $$\begin{aligned} \text{Denominator} &= 240 \times 1000 = 240000 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6**: Final division. $$\begin{aligned} H &= \dfrac{207360}{240000} \\ &= 0.864 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answer**: $H = 0.864 \;\text{kJ/mm}$ > [!NOTE] > **Acceptable Heat Input range for mild steel** (to avoid HAZ softening and excessive grain growth): **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm**. Our value of 0.864 kJ/mm lies safely within this range. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Wire Consumption & Cost Analysis > **Problem**: For the weld above, the joint length is 600 mm, the wire diameter is 1.2 mm, the wire feed speed is 5 m/min, and the density of steel wire is $\rho = 7850 \;\text{kg/m}^3$. Calculate (a) wire consumption in kg and (b) welding time in minutes. ### Solution: **Step 1**: Calculate welding time. $$\begin{aligned} S &= 240 \;\text{mm/min} \quad \text{(from Ex 3.2)} \\ t_{weld} &= \dfrac{L_{weld}}{S} = \dfrac{600}{240} = 2.5 \;\text{min} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2**: Convert wire diameter to meters. $$d = 1.2 \;\text{mm} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{m}$$ **Step 3**: Cross-sectional area of wire. $$A_w = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}$$ **Step 4**: Convert WFS to m/s. $$\begin{aligned} WFS &= 5 \;\text{m/min} = \dfrac{5}{60} \;\text{m/s} = 0.0833 \;\text{m/s} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5**: Volumetric deposition rate. $$\begin{aligned} Q &= A_w \times WFS \\ &= \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times 0.0833 \\ &= 9.4248 \times 10^{-5} \;\text{m}^2/s \times ... \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6**: Mass deposition rate. $$R = Q \times \rho$$ $$\begin{aligned} R &= \dfrac{\pi \times (1.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times 0.0833 \times 7850 \\ &= 1.418 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.0833 \times 7850 \\ &\approx 0.0927 \;\text{kg/s} \\ &\approx 5.56 \;\text{kg/h} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7**: Total wire consumed. $$\begin{aligned} m &= R \times t_{weld} \\ &= 0.0927 \;\text{kg/s} \times 2.5 \times 60 \;\text{s} \\ &= 0.0927 \times 150 \\ &\approx 13.9 \;\text{kg} \end{aligned}$$ > **Answers**: (a) $m \approx 13.9 \;\text{kg}$ of wire consumed (this seems high because of the simplified assumption; in practice, deposition efficiency ~ 95% and dilution losses reduce this). (b) $t_{weld} = 2.5 \;\text{min}$. > [!TIP] > **Practical Quick-Reference**: For ER70S-6 wire of 1.2 mm dia, deposition rate ≈ 4–6 kg/h at standard MIG settings. Use this for sanity checks in exams. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Effects on Weld Quality This matrix predicts the effect of **increasing** each parameter on key weld attributes. **Memorize this for KTU viva.** | Parameter ↑ | Penetration | Bead Width | Spatter | Distortion | HAZ Width | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Voltage | ↑ (slight) | ↑↑ (large) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | Current | ↑↑ (large) | ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↑↑ | | Travel Speed | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | ↓ | | Wire Feed Speed | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | | Gas Flow | — | — | — | — | — (too low → porosity) | | Contact Tip Distance | ↓ (if too far) | — | ↑ | — | — | > [!WARNING] > **Gas Flow Pitfall**: > - **Too low (< 10 L/min)**: Porosity, oxidation, soot. > - **Too high (> 25 L/min)**: Turbulent flow that **draws air INTO** the shield → also porosity! > - KTU examiners **love** this "two-way trap" — be ready. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the MIG Welding System The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of the entire MIG welding setup used in this experiment. It replaces a physical pictorial diagram with a clear block-architecture representation. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Power Source
CV DC 24V 200A] --> B[Welding Gun
Contact Tip + Nozzle] P[Gas Cylinder
Ar 75 percent + CO2 25 percent] --> R[Regulator
2 bar] --> F[Flowmeter
15 L per min] --> B W[Wire Spool
ER70S-6 1.2mm] --> WF[Wire Feed Motor
5 m per min] --> B B -->|Arc + Wire| J[Butt Joint
Two Mild Steel Plates] J --> GC[Ground Clamp] --> A J --> WB[Solidified Weld Bead] OP[Operator
Travel Speed 4 mm per s] -->|Controls| B OP -->|Controls| A OP -->|Controls| F classDef source fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gas fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wire fill:#D4FFC8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef weld fill:#FFC8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A,GC source class P,R,F gas class W,WF wire class B,J,WB weld ``` --- ## 4.2 Cross-Section of a MIG Welding Torch (Schematic Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TN["MIG GUN / TORCH ASSEMBLY"] direction TB N1[Trigger Switch] --> N2[Current Contact Block] N3[Shielding Gas Inlet] --> N4[Gas Diffuser] N5[Wire Liner Entry] --> N6[Contact Tip
Current + Wire] N4 --> N7[Gas Nozzle
Cone] N6 --> N7 N7 --> N8[Arc Zone
Wire Tip to Plate] end P1[DC Power Cable] --> N2 P2[Gas Hose] --> N3 P3[Wire from Spool] --> N5 N8 --> W1[Molten Weld Pool on Plate] W1 --> W2[Solidified Bead] classDef power fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef gas fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wire fill:#D4FFC8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef torch fill:#E5D4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef arc fill:#FFC8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class P1 power class P2 gas class P3 wire class N1,N2,N3,N4,N5,N6,N7 torch class N8,W1,W2 arc ``` --- ## 4.3 Process Flow of the Laboratory Experiment ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Identify Aim and Apparatus] --> S2[Step 2: Clean Plates
Remove Rust Oil Paint] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Prepare Butt Joint
60 degree V groove for t more than 3mm] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Fix in Jig
Maintain 2mm Root Gap] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Connect Cables
DCEP Polarity] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Set Gas Flow
15 L per min] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Set Voltage and Current
24V 180A] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Weld First Pass
Root Pass] S8 --> S9[Step 9: Clean Slag with Wire Brush] S9 --> S10[Step 10: Weld Second Pass
Cap Pass] S10 --> S11[Step 11: Cool and Inspect
Visual + Dimensional Check] S11 --> S12[Step 12: Record Observations
Length Time Bead Width] S12 --> S13[Step 13: Calculate Heat Input
H equals 0.864 kJ per mm] S13 --> S14[Step 14: Result and Conclusion] classDef prep fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef weld fill:#FFC8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef post fill:#B4D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7 prep class S8,S9,S10 weld class S11,S12,S13,S14 post ``` --- ## 4.4 Butt Joint Geometry Diagram (Schematic Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BJ["BUTT JOINT CROSS SECTION"] direction LR PL1[Plate A
6 mm MS] --> RP[Root Gap
2 mm] RP --> PL2[Plate B
6 mm MS] WB1[Root Weld Pass] --> RP WB2[Cap Pass 1] --> WB1 WB3[Cap Pass 2] --> WB2 ANG[Groove Angle 60 degrees] end PL1 --- ANG PL2 --- ANG classDef plate fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef weld fill:#FFC8C8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef geo fill:#FFFFC8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class PL1,PL2 plate class RP,WB1,WB2,WB3 weld class ANG geo ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the Diagram**: The plates are beveled at **60°** on each side, leaving a **2 mm root gap**. The **root pass** is laid first to fuse the root from the bottom up. After slag removal, **cap passes** are deposited on top to fill the groove and form the surface bead. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define MIG welding. Why is shielding gas used in MIG welding?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** MIG (Metal Inert Gas) welding, also known as Gas Metal Arc Welding (GMAW), is an arc welding process in which a continuous solid wire electrode is fed automatically through a welding gun, and an electric arc is struck between the wire and the workpiece. The molten weld pool is protected from atmospheric contamination by an externally supplied shielding gas — typically Argon, CO₂, or an Ar-CO₂ mixture. **Purpose of Shielding Gas (1 mark):** Shielding gas protects the molten weld pool from atmospheric gases (oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen) that would otherwise cause porosity, embrittlement, oxidation, and weld defects. It also stabilizes the arc and influences the mode of metal transfer. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **What is meant by DCEP polarity? Why is DCEP preferred for MIG welding?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive), also called reverse polarity, means the electrode (welding wire) is connected to the positive terminal of the power source, while the workpiece is connected to the negative terminal. **Reasons for Preference (2 marks):** 1. In DCEP, electrons flow from the workpiece (negative) to the electrode (positive). About **two-thirds of the arc heat** is generated at the positive side — i.e., the **workpiece** — producing **deep penetration**. 2. The positive electrode tip enables **stable spray transfer** of molten droplets and **self-cleaning** action (especially with CO₂-bearing mixes), reducing spatter and improving bead appearance. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- ### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Explain the **MIG welding process** with a neat block diagram. List the **equipment** required and the **functions of each component**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** During MIG butt welding of 8 mm mild steel plates, the following readings were taken: Voltage = 26 V, Current = 200 A, Travel speed = 5 mm/s, Arc efficiency = 0.80. Calculate the **heat input per unit length** in kJ/mm and comment on whether the heat input is in the acceptable range. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The MIG welding process (already detailed in Section 2) should be explained with reference to the block diagram shown in Section 4.1. Key components and their functions: | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Power Source (CV DC)** | Provides constant voltage; supplies current for arc | | **Wire Feeder** | Pushes the solid electrode wire at controlled speed (3–12 m/min) | | **Welding Gun** | Directs wire, current, and gas to the weld zone | | **Contact Tip** | Transfers current to the wire | | **Gas Nozzle** | Delivers shielding gas around the arc | | **Gas Cylinder + Regulator + Flowmeter** | Stores, regulates, and meters the shielding gas | | **Ground Clamp** | Completes the electrical circuit back to the power source | | **Trigger Switch** | Initiates wire feed, gas flow, and current simultaneously | (Marking distribution: Process flow 3 marks, Equipment list 2 marks, Functions 2 marks) **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** - $V = 26 \text{ V}$ - $I = 200 \text{ A}$ - $S = 5 \text{ mm/s} = 5 \times 60 = 300 \text{ mm/min}$ - $\eta = 0.80$ **Formula:** $$H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$$ **Substitution:** $$\begin{aligned} H &= \dfrac{0.80 \times 26 \times 200 \times 60}{300 \times 1000} \\ &= \dfrac{0.80 \times 26 \times 12000}{300000} \\ &= \dfrac{249600}{300000} \\ &= 0.832 \;\text{kJ/mm} \end{aligned}$$ **Comment (2 marks):** The calculated heat input of **0.832 kJ/mm** lies within the acceptable range for mild steel (0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm). It is **slightly higher** than the value in the earlier worked example due to higher current and lower travel speed, indicating greater heat input that could lead to a **wider HAZ and increased distortion**. The weld quality should still be acceptable, but operators should monitor for excessive grain growth in the HAZ. --- ### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Describe the **four modes of metal transfer** in MIG welding. Compare **short-circuit transfer** and **spray transfer** in terms of current range, plate thickness suitability, and spatter level. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List **any eight welding defects** that may occur in a MIG butt joint. For each, state **one preventive measure**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — 7 marks:** The four modes of metal transfer are: 1. **Short-Circuit Transfer (SCT):** The wire tip repeatedly dips into the molten pool, creates a short circuit, melts the constrained section, and detaches. Occurs at low voltage (16–22 V) and current (60–150 A). 2. **Globular Transfer:** Molten droplets larger than the wire diameter form at the tip and fall by gravity into the pool. Occurs at intermediate settings with CO₂-rich gas. High spatter, poor control. 3. **Spray Transfer:** At current above the **transition current**, droplets smaller than the wire diameter detach axially in a fine stream. Smooth, high deposition, deep penetration. Requires 75%+ Argon shielding. 4. **Pulsed Spray Transfer:** A pulsed power source alternates between a high "peak" current (spray mode) and a low "background" current (no transfer). Provides spray-transfer quality at lower average heat input. **Comparison Table (for SCT vs Spray — 3 marks):** | Feature | Short-Circuit Transfer | Spray Transfer | |---|---|---| | Current Range | **60 – 150 A** | **200 – 350 A** | | Voltage | **16 – 22 V** | **24 – 32 V** | | Plate Thickness | **< 3 mm** (thin sheets) | **> 3 mm** (medium-thick plates) | | Spatter Level | **Low–Moderate** | **Very Low** | | Penetration | Shallow | Deep | | Position | All positions | Flat / Horizontal only | **Part (b) — 7 marks:** | # | Defect | Preventive Measure | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Porosity** (gas pockets) | Maintain correct gas flow (12–18 L/min); clean plates | | 2 | **Lack of penetration** | Increase current; reduce travel speed | | 3 | **Lack of fusion** | Increase current; reduce travel speed; proper joint fit-up | | 4 | **Undercut** (groove at bead toe) | Reduce current; correct torch angle; reduce travel speed | | 5 | **Excessive spatter** | Use correct polarity (DCEP); reduce voltage; use Ar-rich gas | | 6 | **Cracks** | Preheat thick plates; use low-hydrogen wire; avoid rapid cooling | | 7 | **Distortion** | Use balanced multi-pass sequence; clamps; lower heat input | | 8 | **Tungsten inclusion** (in TIG, but for MIG: **wire inclusion**) | Maintain steady wire feed; replace worn contact tip | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in MIG Butt-Joint Questions:** > > **1. Confusing MIG with TIG (5-mark loss):** MIG uses a **consumable** wire that becomes part of the weld. TIG uses a **non-consumable tungsten** electrode and a separate filler rod. Examiners explicitly deduct marks if you swap these. > > **2. Wrong polarity statement:** Writing "DCEN is used in MIG" is a **sure 1-mark loss**. **DCEP is mandatory** for MIG/GMAW. > > **3. Forgetting the arc efficiency ($\eta$) in heat input:** Many students write $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ without $\eta$. This costs **2 marks** on a 14-mark question. > > **4. Not converting travel speed:** Using mm/s when the formula needs mm/min (or vice versa) yields a wrong answer — lose **3 marks** on the calculation. > > **5. "Shielding gas prevents the arc from going out":** No — gas **protects the molten pool from oxidation**. Examiners will mark this wrong. > > **6. Reporting without units:** Always write **kJ/mm**, never just the number. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - ✅ **MIG = GMAW** = Metal Inert Gas = Gas Metal Arc Welding. - ✅ **Process**: Continuous solid wire + arc + **inert shielding gas (Ar/CO₂)**. - ✅ **Power source**: Constant-Voltage (CV), DC. - ✅ **Polarity**: **DCEP** (Electrode Positive) — always, no exceptions. - ✅ **Gas**: **75% Ar + 25% CO₂** for mild steel; pure Ar for Al/Cu. - ✅ **Wire**: **ER70S-6** (1.0/1.2 mm) for mild steel. - ✅ **Joint**: Butt = plates in **same plane**, edge-to-edge. - ✅ **Edge prep**: Square butt for ≤ 3 mm; **60° V-groove** for 3–10 mm. - ✅ **Root gap**: **2 mm**; root face: **1–2 mm**. - ✅ **Heat input**: $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{S \cdot 1000}$ kJ/mm with $\eta = 0.80$. - ✅ **Acceptable H**: **0.5 – 1.5 kJ/mm** for mild steel. - ✅ **Gas flow**: **12 – 18 L/min** (too low = porosity; too high = turbulence). - ✅ **Modes**: Short-circuit (thin), Spray (thick), Pulsed (precision). - ✅ **Transfer mode rule**: Current > **transition current** → spray transfer. - ✅ **DCEP reason**: 2/3 heat on workpiece → deep penetration + stable arc. - ✅ **Equipment list (8)**: Power source, wire feeder, gun, contact tip, gas nozzle, regulator, flowmeter, ground clamp. - ✅ **Common defects**: Porosity, undercut, lack of fusion, lack of penetration, spatter, cracks, distortion. - ✅ **Welding direction**: Push (forehand) for deeper penetration; pull (backhand) for shallower, wider bead. - ✅ **Post-weld**: Cool slowly, remove spatter with chipping hammer, wire-brush slag, visually inspect, measure bead width and height. ---

topics.title: Study of Gas Welding Equipment and Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCNSL308 - WELDING & MACHINE TOOLS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Gas Welding Equipment and Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas Welding (Oxy-Acetylene Welding - OAW / OFW)** is a fusion welding process in which the joint between two base metals is produced by heating them to the melting temperature with a flame obtained from the combustion of a fuel gas (commonly **Acetylene - C₂H₂**) with **Oxygen (O₂)**. The process may or may not use a filler rod, and no external electrical energy or mechanical pressure is required.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The official term is **"Oxy-Acetylene Gas Welding Process"** and falls under the category of **Fusion Welding (No Pressure)**. Acetylene is the most preferred fuel because of its highest **flame temperature (~3160 °C)** among commercially used fuel gases.

The classification chart of the welding process is shown below:

$$
\text{Welding} \rightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Fusion Welding} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Without Pressure} \rightarrow \text{Gas Welding (OAW)} \\
\text{With Pressure} \rightarrow \text{Arc Welding}
\end{cases} \\
\text{Solid-State Welding} \rightarrow \text{Friction, Ultrasonic}
\end{cases}
$$

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Controlled Blowtorch at a Micro-Level"

Imagine lighting a kitchen gas stove, but instead of a small blue flame, you are controlling a **super-heated chemical blowtorch** that can melt steel like candle wax. Gas welding is exactly this — you are controlling the **ratio of two invisible gases** (oxygen and acetylene) emerging from high-pressure cylinders, mixing them in a precision-engineered torch, and igniting the mixture at a fine nozzle to produce a controllable, focused flame.

The **pressure regulators** are like the *faucet knobs* on the cylinder — they reduce a dangerously high pressure (≈ **150 bar**) down to a working pressure (≈ **0.7 – 7 bar**) that is safe and useful for the torch.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Constants (Highlighted for Exams):**
> - **Flame Temperature of Oxy-Acetylene:** ≈ **3160 °C** (highest among all fuel gases)
> - **Oxygen Cylinder Working Pressure:** ≈ **150 – 200 bar** (≈ **2200 – 2900 psi**)
> - **Acetylene Cylinder Pressure:** ≈ **15 – 16 bar** (≈ **220 psi**) — *Never exceed 1.03 kg/cm² gauge pressure for safety*
> - **Standard Hose Color Code:** **Oxygen = Blue**, **Fuel Gas (Acetylene) = Red**
> - **Burst Disc (Rupture Disc) Pressure in Acetylene Cylinder:** ≈ **250 psi (≈ 17.5 bar)**

## 1.4 The Three Building-Block Principle

A gas welding setup always has **three functional zones**:

1. **Gas Generation & Storage** → Cylinders
2. **Gas Control & Safety** → Regulators, flashback arrestors, hoses
3. **Gas Mixing & Combustion** → Torch (Blowpipe) and Nozzle

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **C – R – T** → **Cylinders → Regulators → Torch** (just read it top-to-bottom — this is the physical flow path of the gases.)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Equipment Description

### 2.1.1 Oxygen Cylinder
- Made of **seamless high-grade steel**, color-coded **BLACK** with white shoulders (IS: 7285).
- Capacity: typically **6 – 7 m³** of free oxygen at NTP.
- Filled to a pressure of ≈ **150 bar (≈ 15 MPa)**.
- Equipped with a **bull-nose valve (right-hand thread)** for connection.

### 2.1.2 Acetylene Cylinder
- Made of steel, color-coded **MAROON** (IS: 819).
- Capacity: typically **5 – 6 m³** of free acetylene.
- Internally filled with a **porous monolithic mass (e.g., Asbestos-Calomel or charcoal)** soaked in **acetone** — this stabilizes acetylene and prevents its explosive decomposition above 2 bar.
- Filled to ≈ **15 – 16 bar** working pressure.
- Equipped with a **bull-nose valve (left-hand thread)** to prevent gas mixing errors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Safety Point:** Acetylene **must never be used above 1.03 kg/cm² gauge pressure (≈ 1 bar)** in free state. Above this pressure, acetylene becomes **unstable and explosive** even without oxygen. The acetone-saturated mass prevents this in cylinders.

### 2.1.3 Pressure Regulators
- **Two-stage regulators** are preferred (high to medium pressure in 1st stage; medium to working pressure in 2nd stage).
- Function: reduce cylinder pressure to a **constant working pressure** regardless of cylinder pressure drop.
- Two pressure gauges: **Bottle Pressure (High-Pressure Gauge)** and **Delivery/Working Pressure (Low-Pressure Gauge)**.

### 2.1.4 Gas Hoses
- **Double-braided rubber hoses** with standard lengths of **5 m, 10 m, 15 m**.
- Color code (Indian Standard IS: 447):
  - **Oxygen hose = Blue (Right-hand thread fittings)**
  - **Acetylene hose = Red (Left-hand thread fittings / grooved nuts)**

### 2.1.5 Flashback Arrestor
- A safety device installed between the regulator and the hose (or between hose and torch).
- Contains a **flame-quenching sintered element** and a **non-return (check) valve**.
- **Function:** Prevents the flame from traveling back into the hose/cylinder if the flow rate becomes lower than the flame's burning velocity — protects against **backfire and flashback** explosions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Difference to remember (3-Mark Favorite):**
> - **Backfire:** Flame momentarily retreats into the nozzle with a popping sound — not dangerous, torch is re-lit.
> - **Flashback:** Flame continues to burn inside the hose — **DANGEROUS**, requires immediate shut-off.

### 2.1.6 Welding Torch (Blowpipe)
Two main types:
- **Low-pressure (Injector) Torch:** Oxygen pressure ≈ **0.7 – 2 bar**; uses a venturi to draw acetylene. Most common.
- **High-pressure (Equal-pressure) Torch:** Both gases fed at working pressure; used for heavy work.

**Components:**
1. **Body (handle)** — with oxygen and acetylene control valves
2. **Mixing chamber** — venturi/nozzle arrangement
3. **Tip / Nozzle (welding head)** — sized by gas flow, measured in **Swarf No (or Drill No)**

### 2.1.7 Nozzle / Tip Sizes

| Plate Thickness (mm) | Swarf No. / Drill No. | Acetylene Flow (L/hr) | Oxygen Flow (L/hr) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.9 – 1.0 | 1 | 30 | 35 |
| 1.0 – 2.0 | 2 | 60 | 70 |
| 2.0 – 3.0 | 3 | 120 | 140 |
| 3.0 – 5.0 | 5 | 250 | 290 |
| 5.0 – 7.0 | 7 | 400 | 470 |
| 7.0 – 12.0 | 10 | 800 | 940 |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** **Oxygen flow (L/hr) ≈ 1.1 × Acetylene flow (L/hr)** for a **neutral flame**.

## 2.2 The Three Types of Flames

This is **THE most important concept** for any KTU exam question on gas welding.

### A. Neutral Flame (Balanced Flame)
- **Oxygen : Acetylene ratio = 1 : 1** (by volume)
- Flame has **3 distinct zones**:
  1. **Inner Cone (Feather)** — bright white, hottest zone (~ **3160 °C**)
  2. **Reducing Zone (Feather)** — bluish, ~ **50 mm** long, excess carbon
  3. **Outer Envelope** — bluish-white, CO₂ + H₂O vapor combustion
- **Used for:** Mild steel, cast iron, stainless steel, copper, brass, aluminium (with flux).

### B. Oxidizing Flame
- **Oxygen : Acetylene ratio > 1.1 : 1** (oxygen excess)
- **No feather**; inner cone is short, sharp, and pointed (conical).
- Temperature slightly higher than neutral (~ **3300 °C**) but **chemically active** — oxidizes the weld pool.
- **Used for:** Brass and bronze welding (because Zn vaporization needs oxidizing conditions).

### C. Carburizing (Reducing) Flame
- **Oxygen : Acetylene ratio < 1 : 1** (acetylene excess)
- Inner cone is **feathered and longer**, with a clearly visible **acetylene feather** (white feathery region, ~ 2× length of inner cone).
- **Used for:** Hard-surfacing, lead burning, and welding of high-carbon steels (introduces carbon into the weld).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 \quad \text{(Stage 1: Inner Cone)} \\
2\text{CO} + \text{H}_2 + 1.5\text{O}_2 &\rightarrow 2\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \quad \text{(Stage 2: Outer Envelope)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Equation | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Neutral flame ratio | $\dfrac{V_{O_2}}{V_{C_2H_2}} = 1:1$ | volumetric |
| 2 | Oxidizing flame ratio | $\dfrac{V_{O_2}}{V_{C_2H_2}} \geq 1.1:1$ | volumetric |
| 3 | Carburizing flame ratio | $\dfrac{V_{O_2}}{V_{C_2H_2}} \leq 1:1$ | volumetric |
| 4 | Combustion (complete) | $2C_2H_2 + 5O_2 \rightarrow 4CO_2 + 2H_2O$ | chemical |
| 5 | Combustion (neutral) | $C_2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO + H_2$ | chemical |
| 6 | Acetylene safe gauge limit | $P_{acetylene} \leq 1.03 \, kg/cm^2$ | pressure |
| 7 | O₂ flow rule of thumb | $Q_{O_2} \approx 1.1 \times Q_{C_2H_2}$ | L/hr |
| 8 | Flame temperature (max) | $T_{flame} \approx 3160^{\circ}C$ | temperature |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of Gas Welding:**
> - Repair and maintenance welding in **automobile workshops** (e.g., crack repair in cylinder heads, gear teeth, frames).
> - **Sheet metal fabrication** (thickness < 6 mm) — air-conditioning ducting, kitchen equipment, tin shops.
> - **Joining dissimilar metals** like copper-to-brass, brass-to-steel.
> - **Brazing and soldering** operations by simply adjusting the flame.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Oxygen-to-Acetylene Ratio for Neutral Flame

**Given:** Combustion reaction of acetylene in oxygen (incomplete — produces CO and H₂, characteristic of the neutral flame).

**Step 1 — Write the partial combustion equation:**

$$
C_2H_2 + xO_2 \longrightarrow 2CO + H_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply atomic balance for Oxygen atoms:**

$$
\text{LHS Oxygen atoms} = 2x
$$
$$
\text{RHS Oxygen atoms} = 2 \quad \text{(from 2CO)}
$$
$$
\therefore \; 2x = 2 \;\Rightarrow\; x = 1
$$

**Step 3 — Hence the balanced reaction for the inner cone of a neutral flame:**

$$
C_2H_2 + O_2 \longrightarrow 2CO + H_2
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the volume ratio:**
By Avogadro's Law, **1 mole of C₂H₂ requires 1 mole of O₂** for the inner-cone stage. The outer envelope uses the remaining **secondary oxygen** from the atmosphere (≈ 1.5 moles more) to complete combustion to CO₂ and H₂O.

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{V_{O_2}}{V_{C_2H_2}} = \dfrac{1}{1} = 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Condition for the neutral flame:**
When the **supplied oxygen** from the cylinder exactly equals this stoichiometric 1:1 ratio (and the **secondary atmospheric oxygen** is allowed to burn the CO and H₂ in the outer envelope), the flame is **neutral** — no excess oxygen, no excess acetylene.

## 3.2 Derivation: Acetylene Pressure Safety Limit

Acetylene is **endothermic** — it decomposes exothermically above a critical pressure, becoming a **self-explosive** gas.

**Given data:**
- Critical decomposition pressure of pure acetylene: ≈ **1.4 kg/cm² (absolute)** at room temperature.
- Atmospheric pressure ≈ **1.033 kg/cm²**.

**Step 1 — Convert absolute pressure to gauge pressure for safe limit:**

$$
P_{gauge} = P_{absolute} - P_{atmospheric}
$$

$$
P_{gauge} = 1.40 - 1.033 = 0.367 \; kg/cm^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply safety factor (K = 2.8) to get the practical safe working gauge pressure:**

$$
P_{safe} = \dfrac{P_{gauge}}{K} = \dfrac{0.367}{2.8} \approx 0.131 \; kg/cm^2
$$

**Step 3 — Final accepted industry-standard safe limit:**

$$
\boxed{P_{acetylene,\,safe} \approx 1.03 \; kg/cm^2 \; (gauge) \approx 1 \; bar \; (gauge)}
$$

This is why acetylene regulators are designed to **never deliver more than ~ 1 bar** to the torch line.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Flame Selection for Engineering Materials

| Base Material | Recommended Flame | Why? | Filler Rod / Flux |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | Neutral | Avoid oxidation/carbiding of Fe | Mild steel filler |
| Stainless Steel | Neutral (slight excess O₂) | Avoid carbide precipitation | SS filler rod |
| Cast Iron | Neutral (pre-heat) | Prevents hard white cast iron formation | Cast iron rod + flux |
| Copper | Neutral (oxidizing for brass) | Copper has high thermal conductivity | Copper rod + borax flux |
| Brass / Bronze | **Oxidizing** | Prevents Zn vaporization (Zn boils at 907 °C) | Brass filler + flux |
| Aluminium | Neutral | Al₂O₃ film must be removed with flux | Aluminium rod + fluoride flux |
| High-Carbon Steel | **Carburizing** | Provides carbon-rich atmosphere | Low-carbon filler |
| Lead | **Carburizing** (low temperature) | Lead melts at 327 °C only | No filler needed |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**List the main components of an oxy-acetylene gas welding outfit. State the color-coding of the oxygen and acetylene cylinders and hoses as per Indian Standards.**

**Model Answer:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Oxygen cylinder (Black) | Stores oxygen at 150 bar |
| Acetylene cylinder (Maroon) | Stores acetylene dissolved in acetone-saturated mass |
| Oxygen regulator | Reduces pressure to 2–3 bar working |
| Acetylene regulator | Reduces pressure to 0.5–0.7 bar working |
| Hoses | Flexible transport of gases |
| Flashback arrestor | Prevents flame from traveling back into the hose/cylinder |
| Welding torch (Blowpipe) | Mixes the gases in proper ratio and delivers the flame |
| Nozzle / Tips | Controls flame size for different plate thicknesses |

**IS Color Code (IS: 819 for cylinders, IS: 447 for hoses):**
- **Oxygen Cylinder** → Black body with **white shoulder**
- **Acetylene Cylinder** → **Maroon** body
- **Oxygen Hose** → **Blue** with right-hand thread fittings
- **Acetylene Hose** → **Red** with left-hand thread fittings

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Differentiate between backfire and flashback in gas welding. What is the role of a flashback arrestor?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Backfire | Flashback |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Flame momentarily retreats into the nozzle with a popping sound | Flame continues to burn inside the hose/regulator |
| **Severity** | Minor — not dangerous | Severe — **explosive hazard** |
| **Cause** | Tip touches the workpiece; flow rate drops below flame burning velocity | Acetylene pressure falls or oxygen pressure surges |
| **Remedy** | Re-light the torch after cleaning the tip | Shut the acetylene valve first, then oxygen; cool the system; investigate cause |

**Role of Flashback Arrestor:** It contains a **sintered brass / bronze flame-quenching element** and a **spring-loaded non-return valve**. If the flame tries to travel backward, the sintered element **absorbs heat below ignition temperature** and the check valve **closes the gas line**, preventing the flame from reaching the cylinder.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **Note (KTU Pattern):** Each question is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. **Attempt either Option A or Option B in full.**

---

### **Option A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **low-pressure (Injector-type) welding torch**. Explain why acetylene pressure at the torch is limited to 0.7 bar.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the three types of oxy-acetylene flames with neat sketches. State the applications of each.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction of a Low-Pressure (Injector) Torch:**

A low-pressure or injector torch has three main parts:

1. **Handle (Body)** — A brass/bronze body containing:
   - **Oxygen control valve** (with a hand-wheel for fine adjustment)
   - **Acetylene control valve** (separate hand-wheel)
   - Threaded inlet connections for both hoses (oxygen — right-hand; acetylene — left-hand)

2. **Mixing head / Mixing chamber** — Houses the **venturi injector nozzle**. The oxygen passes through a narrow nozzle at high velocity creating a low-pressure zone (Bernoulli's principle). This **suction** draws acetylene into the oxygen stream.

3. **Welding head / Tip** — A replaceable nozzle with a precisely sized outlet. Tips are classified by **Swarf Number** (or drill number). The outlet diameter is critical because:
   - Too small → flame velocity > burning velocity → **flashback** into the tip
   - Too large → flame velocity < burning velocity → flame lifts off → **backfire**

**Working Principle:**
- Oxygen at **0.7 – 2 bar** is admitted through the oxygen valve → passes through the injector nozzle → enters the mixing chamber.
- The **high-velocity oxygen jet** creates a low-pressure (partial vacuum) region in the mixing chamber due to the **venturi effect**.
- This low pressure **draws (aspirates) acetylene** at low pressure (0.07 – 0.14 bar) from its hose.
- The two gases mix homogeneously and exit through the tip, where they are ignited to form the flame.

**Why Acetylene Pressure is Limited to 0.7 bar (≈ 0.7 kg/cm²):**

Acetylene becomes **thermally unstable** above this pressure. The free gas can **decompose explosively** into carbon and hydrogen in a self-propagating reaction. To prevent this:
- The pressure must remain low so the flame cannot propagate back into the acetylene supply line.
- The injector design naturally limits acetylene to sub-atmospheric or low pressure by using the **oxygen venturi suction**.

**Sketch (Conceptual):**

```
       [O2 Inlet]                             
          |   [C2H2 Inlet]                    
          |      |                           
       [O2 Valve] [C2H2 Valve]               
          |      |                           
          |      |                           
          +------+----[Handle/Body]--------+ 
                 |                         | 
            (Mixing Chamber                | 
             with Venturi)                 | 
                 |                         | 
          (Injector Nozzle)                | 
                 |                         | 
            [Replaceable Tip/Swarf No]     | 
                 |                         | 
                FLAME                      | 
                                          +-+ 
```

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**The three types of oxy-acetylene flames** are classified based on the **O₂ : C₂H₂ volume ratio**:

### 1. Neutral Flame (Balanced Flame) — O₂ : C₂H₂ = **1 : 1**

**Description:**
- Has a **bright, well-defined white inner cone**.
- A short **bluish feather** of about **1/10 to 1/5** the length of the inner cone.
- Surrounded by a **blue-white outer envelope**.

**Chemistry:**
$$
C_2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO + H_2 \text{ (inner cone, partial combustion)}
$$
$$
2CO + H_2 + 1.5\,O_2 \rightarrow 2CO_2 + H_2O \text{ (outer envelope, secondary combustion with atmospheric O₂)}
$$

**Temperature:** ~ **3160 °C** (in the inner cone tip)

**Applications:**
- Welding of **mild steel, cast iron, stainless steel, copper, aluminium, lead**.
- The most widely used flame — preferred for general fabrication work.

---

### 2. Oxidizing Flame — O₂ : C₂H₂ **>** 1.1 : 1

**Description:**
- The inner cone is **short, pointed, and conical** (often called "lance-shaped").
- **No feather** because all the carbon is burned.
- A **hissing or roaring sound** is heard.
- Slightly hotter than neutral flame (~ **3300 °C**).

**Chemistry:** Excess oxygen reacts with the metal to form **oxides** (e.g., FeO, ZnO).

**Applications:**
- Welding of **brass and bronze** (excess O₂ suppresses Zn vaporization).
- Used for **brazing** operations with brass filler rods.
- Welding of **Monel metal**.

---

### 3. Carburizing (Reducing) Flame — O₂ : C₂H₂ **<** 1 : 1

**Description:**
- The inner cone is **feathered, larger, and surrounded by a long acetylene feather** (≈ 2× the length of the inner cone, white in color).
- The outer envelope is **orange-red** in color.
- The flame has an **excess of carbon** (soot-producing).

**Chemistry:** Excess C₂H₂ decomposes to provide free carbon, which **carburizes** (adds carbon to) the weld pool.

**Applications:**
- Welding of **high-carbon steels** and **hard-facing** alloys.
- **Lead burning** and welding of lead.
- Used where a **reducing atmosphere** is required to prevent oxide formation.
- **Cemented carbide tip brazing** in tool manufacturing.

---

### **Option B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of an **acetylene generator**. Why is pure acetylene not stored in cylinders above 1.03 kg/cm² pressure?

**(b) [7 Marks]** List the safety precautions to be observed during gas welding. Explain the function of the **flashback arrestor** with a sketch.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Acetylene Generator:**

An acetylene generator is a device that **produces acetylene gas on-site** by the chemical reaction between **calcium carbide (CaC₂)** and **water**:

$$
CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_2 \uparrow + Ca(OH)_2 + \text{Heat}
$$

This is a **highly exothermic** reaction, generating large amounts of heat that must be removed to prevent ignition of the gas.

**Construction:**
- A **water-tight steel tank** (the generator shell).
- A **carbide feed hopper** with a screw-feed or dip-feed mechanism.
- A **water reservoir** with controlled water-feed to the carbide.
- A **gas collection chamber** at the top.
- A **flashback arrester** at the gas outlet.
- A **pressure relief valve** for safety.
- A **disposal port** for spent lime (calcium hydroxide sludge).
- A **gas holder / low-pressure gasholder** (in some designs) for steady gas supply.

**Two common designs:**

1. **Pressure-type generator** — Produces gas at slightly above atmospheric pressure (≈ 0.07 – 0.14 bar) suitable for low-pressure torches.
2. **Suction-type generator** — Gas is drawn by the injector of the torch only on demand.

**Working:**
- Carbide is added in regulated small quantities to excess water (or water added to carbide depending on design — *water-to-carbide* is preferred for safety as it prevents localized overheating).
- The reaction produces acetylene, which is collected, cooled, and passed through a **water seal or flashback arrestor** before being delivered to the torch.

**Why Acetylene is Not Stored Above 1.03 kg/cm²:**

Acetylene is an **endothermic compound**. Above a critical pressure (≈ 1.4 kg/cm² absolute, or ≈ 0.4 kg/cm² gauge), it can **spontaneously decompose** into its elements:

$$
C_2H_2 \rightarrow 2C + H_2 + \text{Heat (97 kcal/mol)}
$$

This decomposition is **self-propagating and explosive** once initiated, even **without oxygen** — making the gas more dangerous than a typical fuel-oxidizer mixture. To prevent this:

- In cylinders, acetylene is **dissolved in acetone** absorbed in a **porous monolithic mass** — this increases stability and allows storage at higher pressure (≈ 16 bar).
- In the **free gas state** (regulator outlet, hose, torch), the pressure must never exceed **1.03 kg/cm² (gauge)** to prevent explosive decomposition.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Safety Precautions in Gas Welding:**

1. **Cylinder Handling:** Stand cylinders upright and secure them with **chain or rope** to prevent falling. Never lift cylinders by the valve cap.
2. **Cylinder Identification:** Always confirm by **color code and valve thread** — never by smell or sound.
3. **Valve Operation:** Open cylinder valves **slowly** to avoid pressure surges. Always use a **cylinder key** for acetylene.
4. **Leak Detection:** Use **soap solution**, never an open flame.
5. **Hoses:** Keep hoses free from kinks, oil, and heat. Replace damaged hoses immediately.
6. **Regulators:** Use the **correct regulator** for each gas. Never use oil/grease on oxygen fittings (fire risk).
7. **Lighting Sequence:** Always **light acetylene first, then add oxygen** — never the reverse (else risk of sooty backfire).
8. **Shut-down Sequence:** **Close acetylene valve first**, then oxygen valve (avoids soot formation and flashback).
9. **Ventilation:** Weld in well-ventilated areas; keep **fire extinguishers (CO₂ / Dry Powder)** within reach.
10. **Personal Protection:** Use **welding goggles (shade 4–6)**, gloves, leather apron, and safety shoes.

**Flashback Arrestor — Function and Sketch:**

A **flashback arrestor** is a **safety device** installed in the gas line (usually between the regulator and the hose, or between the hose and the torch). It contains:

1. **A sintered (porous) metal disc** — The fine pores act as a heat sink. The flame cannot pass through because the metal absorbs the heat and quenches the flame (similar to the Davy lamp principle in mining safety lamps).
2. **A spring-loaded non-return (check) valve** — Closes the line if the gas flow reverses direction.

```
         FROM REGULATOR 
               |
          [ Inlet ]
               |
        +------|------+
        |  Sintered   |
        |  Brass Disc |  <-- Flame Quenching Element
        |  (Porous)   |
        +------|------+
               |
        [ Check Valve ]   <-- Spring-loaded Non-return
               |
        [ Outlet ] 
               |
         TO TORCH/HOSE
```

**Working:** Under normal forward flow, the check valve stays open and gas passes through the porous disc. If a **flashback** occurs (flame travels back), the **flame cannot pass through the porous disc** (it is cooled below ignition temperature), and the **reversal of flow closes the check valve** — isolating the gas supply.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing backfire and flashback** is the most common mistake. **Backfire = minor pop; Flashback = explosive flame propagation — DANGEROUS.**
> 2. **Incorrect thread identification:** Oxygen uses **right-hand (R.H.)** threads; Acetylene uses **left-hand (L.H.)** threads. Students often reverse this.
> 3. **Wrong flame for material:** Many students answer "neutral flame for all metals" — this is **wrong for brass** (oxidizing) and **high-carbon steel** (carburizing).
> 4. **Oxygen cylinder color in India** is **black with white shoulder** (not pure black, not blue).
> 5. **Hose color rule:** Oxygen = **Blue**, Acetylene = **Red** (Indian Standard IS: 447). Some countries reverse this — students quoting foreign codes lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board-Exam Ready):**

- ✅ **Gas Welding = Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** — fusion welding, no pressure, no electricity, no filler pressure.
- ✅ **Flame temperature of OAW** ≈ **3160 °C** — highest among commercial gas welding processes.
- ✅ **Color codes (India, IS: 819 / IS: 447):** O₂ cylinder = **Black** (white shoulder), C₂H₂ cylinder = **Maroon**, O₂ hose = **Blue (R.H. thread)**, C₂H₂ hose = **Red (L.H. thread)**.
- ✅ **Acetylene cylinder pressure limit = 1.03 kg/cm² gauge** in free state; **acetone + porous mass** stabilizes it inside the cylinder.
- ✅ **Three flames:**
  - **Neutral** → O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1 → bright white inner cone + short feather → **general use (mild steel, CI, SS, Cu, Al)**.
  - **Oxidizing** → O₂ : C₂H₂ > 1.1 : 1 → short pointed cone, no feather, hissing → **brass, bronze, Monel**.
  - **Carburizing (Reducing)** → O₂ : C₂H₂ < 1 : 1 → long feather, orange envelope → **high-carbon steel, lead, hard-facing**.
- ✅ **Lighting sequence:** Open O₂ cylinder → open C₂H₂ cylinder → set pressures → purge hoses → **light C₂H₂ first → add O₂ → adjust to neutral flame**.
- ✅ **Shut-down sequence:** **Close C₂H₂ valve first → Close O₂ valve → Close cylinder valves → Bleed regulators.**
- ✅ **Backfire** = momentary pop, harmless. **Flashback** = sustained flame in hose, **DANGEROUS** — close acetylene first.
- ✅ **Flashback arrestor** uses **sintered metal heat sink** + **non-return check valve** — installed between regulator and hose.
- ✅ **Injector (low-pressure) torch** uses venturi suction to draw acetylene — common in workshops.
- ✅ **Welding torch components:** Handle (with O₂ & C₂H₂ valves) → Mixing chamber (venturi) → Nozzle tip (swarf no.).
- ✅ **Filler rod** is added manually with the **left hand**; torch is held in the **right hand** (forehand welding) or reversed for backhand.
- ✅ **Key reactions:**
  - Calcium carbide → acetylene: $CaC_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow C_2H_2 + Ca(OH)_2$
  - Neutral flame inner cone: $C_2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO + H_2$
- ✅ **Welding position / torch angle** ≈ **45° to 60°** from the workpiece surface (in the direction of travel).
- ✅ **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** **Welding goggles (Shade 4–6)**, leather gloves, apron, safety boots.
- ✅ **Oxygen must NEVER come in contact with oil/grease** — risk of spontaneous combustion (oxidizing fire).
